content:
# Pre-commit is the first local gate before CI.
# Heavy repository-wide hooks such as Checkov and mypy are intentionally excluded from the default pre-push stage
# so final push feedback stays fast; only the lightweight gitleaks secret scan runs at pre-push by default.
minimum_pre_commit_version: '3.7.0'
default_install_hook_types:
  - pre-commit
  - pre-push
default_stages:
  - pre-commit

repos:
  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  # Secret detection
  # Catch credentials before they ever leave a developer machine.
  # Mirrors the CI gitleaks workflow but runs locally so secrets never leave the machine.
  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  - repo: https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks
    rev: v8.18.4
    hooks:
      - id: gitleaks
        stages: [pre-push]
        pass_filenames: false

  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  # IaC quality
  # Keep infrastructure syntax and policy checks close to the edit so broken Terraform, weak policies,
  # and Dockerfile issues are caught before the slower CI security workflows run.
  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform
    rev: v1.96.3
    hooks:
      - id: terraform_fmt
        files: ^terraform/.*\.tf(vars)?$
      - id: terraform_validate
        files: ^terraform/.*\.tf(vars)?$
        pass_filenames: false

  - repo: local
    hooks:
      - id: checkov-terraform
        name: checkov (terraform)
        entry: checkov --directory terraform --framework terraform --quiet --compact
        language: python
        additional_dependencies:
          - checkov==3.2.72
        files: ^terraform/.*\.tf(vars)?$
        pass_filenames: false

  - repo: https://github.com/hadolint/hadolint
    rev: v2.12.0
    hooks:
      - id: hadolint
        files: (^|/)([Dd]ockerfile|.*\.Dockerfile)$

  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  # General code quality
  # These fast text-level checks enforce the repository's EditorConfig-style whitespace, file endings,
  # and structured-data hygiene before language-specific linters spend time on deeper analysis.
  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v5.0.0
    hooks:
      - id: trailing-whitespace
      - id: end-of-file-fixer
      - id: check-yaml
        args: [--allow-multiple-documents]
      - id: check-json
      - id: check-merge-conflict
      - id: detect-private-key
      - id: mixed-line-ending
        args: [--fix=lf]

  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  # Python
  # Reuse the shared pyproject settings for linting, formatting, and type checks so local edits match CI behaviour
  # without forcing developers to remember separate command lines.
  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  - repo: local
    hooks:
      - id: ruff-check
        name: ruff check
        entry: ruff check --config quality/python/pyproject.toml
        language: python
        additional_dependencies:
          - ruff==0.6.9
        files: \.py$
      - id: ruff-format
        name: ruff format
        entry: ruff format --config quality/python/pyproject.toml
        language: python
        additional_dependencies:
          - ruff==0.6.9
        files: \.py$
      - id: mypy
        name: mypy
        entry: mypy --config-file quality/python/pyproject.toml
        language: python
        additional_dependencies:
          - mypy==1.10.0
        files: \.py$
        pass_filenames: false

  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  # Shell scripts
  # ShellCheck catches quoting, word-splitting, and portability defects early, which matters for the helper scripts
  # in this repository because they are usually executed in automation after only a light manual review.
  # ------------------------------------------------------------
  - repo: https://github.com/shellcheck-py/shellcheck-py
    rev: v0.10.0.1
    hooks:
      - id: shellcheck
        files: \.sh$

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: AWS RDS Automated Backups + Cross-Region Replica
# WHEN TO USE: Add to your existing Terraform module that creates an
#   RDS instance. Enables automated point-in-time recovery (PITR) and
#   optionally creates a read replica in a secondary region for DR.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Variables at the top. Integrate into your existing
#   Terraform module — do not apply standalone.
# RELATED FILES: terraform/aws-ecs/, terraform/aws-eks/
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# ── Variables ────────────────────────────────────────────────
variable "project" {}
variable "environment" {}
variable "aws_region" { default = "us-east-1" }                    # <-- CHANGE THIS
variable "dr_region" { default = "us-west-2" }                     # <-- CHANGE THIS: secondary region for DR
variable "db_instance_class" { default = "db.t3.medium" }          # <-- CHANGE THIS
variable "db_engine_version" { default = "15.4" }                  # <-- CHANGE THIS: PostgreSQL version
variable "backup_retention_days" { default = 7 }                   # <-- CHANGE THIS: 1–35 days
variable "enable_cross_region_replica" { default = false }         # <-- CHANGE THIS: set true to create DR replica

# ── RDS Instance with PITR ───────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  identifier        = "rds-${var.project}-${var.environment}"
  engine            = "postgres"
  engine_version    = var.db_engine_version
  instance_class    = var.db_instance_class
  allocated_storage = 100           # <-- CHANGE THIS (GiB)
  storage_encrypted = true

  # Note 1: backup_retention_period > 0 enables automated backups and
  # point-in-time recovery. PITR allows restore to any second within the
  # retention window. Minimum recommended: 7 days.
  backup_retention_period = var.backup_retention_days

  # Note 2: The backup window should be outside your peak traffic hours.
  # Format: hh24:mi-hh24:mi UTC.
  backup_window      = "02:00-03:00"    # <-- CHANGE THIS
  maintenance_window = "sun:04:00-sun:05:00"  # <-- CHANGE THIS

  # Note 3: Multi-AZ creates a synchronous standby replica in a different AZ.
  # Automatic failover happens in 1-2 minutes if the primary fails.
  # Enable for production workloads.
  multi_az = var.environment == "production" ? true : false

  deletion_protection = var.environment == "production" ? true : false
  skip_final_snapshot = var.environment == "production" ? false : true

  final_snapshot_identifier = var.environment == "production" ? (
    "rds-${var.project}-${var.environment}-final-snapshot"
  ) : null

  db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name  # <-- CHANGE THIS: reference your subnet group
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.rds.id]    # <-- CHANGE THIS

  tags = {
    Name        = "rds-${var.project}-${var.environment}"
    Project     = var.project
    Environment = var.environment
  }
}

# ── Cross-Region Read Replica for DR ─────────────────────────
# Note 4: This creates a read replica in a secondary region. In a DR event,
# promote the replica with:
#   aws rds promote-read-replica --db-instance-identifier <replica-id> --region <dr_region>
# Promotion takes ~5 minutes. The replica becomes an independent standalone DB.
resource "aws_db_instance" "dr_replica" {
  count    = var.enable_cross_region_replica ? 1 : 0
  provider = aws.dr   # Requires an 'aws' provider alias for the DR region

  identifier             = "rds-${var.project}-${var.environment}-dr"
  replicate_source_db    = aws_db_instance.main.arn
  instance_class         = var.db_instance_class
  backup_retention_period = var.backup_retention_days
  backup_window          = "03:00-04:00"
  storage_encrypted      = true
  skip_final_snapshot    = false
  deletion_protection    = true

  tags = {
    Name        = "rds-${var.project}-${var.environment}-dr"
    Project     = var.project
    Environment = var.environment
    Role        = "dr-replica"
  }
}

# ── Manual Snapshot for pre-migration backup ─────────────────
# Note 5: Use aws_db_snapshot to trigger a snapshot before a risky migration.
# Uncomment and apply, then comment out again (snapshots are not re-created on every plan).
# resource "aws_db_snapshot" "pre_migration" {
#   db_instance_identifier = aws_db_instance.main.id
#   db_snapshot_identifier = "rds-${var.project}-${var.environment}-pre-migration"
# }

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server — Backups
# WHEN TO USE: Add to your existing Terraform module that creates an
#   Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server. Enables geo-redundant backups
#   and configures a geo-restore replica for DR.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Variables at the top. Integrate into your existing module.
# RELATED FILES: terraform/azure-aks/, terraform/azure-app-service/
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

variable "project" {}
variable "environment" {}
variable "location" { default = "eastus" }                 # <-- CHANGE THIS: primary region
variable "dr_location" { default = "westus" }              # <-- CHANGE THIS: secondary region for geo-restore
variable "postgres_sku_name" { default = "GP_Standard_D4s_v3" }   # <-- CHANGE THIS
variable "postgres_version" { default = "15" }             # <-- CHANGE THIS
variable "backup_retention_days" { default = 7 }           # <-- CHANGE THIS: 7–35 days
variable "enable_geo_redundant_backup" { default = true }  # <-- CHANGE THIS: required for geo-restore

# ── PostgreSQL Flexible Server with PITR ─────────────────────
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server" "main" {
  name                = "psql-${var.project}-${var.environment}"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  location            = var.location
  version             = var.postgres_version
  sku_name            = var.postgres_sku_name

  # Note 1: geo_redundant_backup_enabled = true replicates backup files to
  # the paired Azure region. This is a prerequisite for geo-restore (RPO ~1h).
  # It cannot be changed after server creation — plan ahead.
  geo_redundant_backup_enabled = var.enable_geo_redundant_backup
  backup_retention_days        = var.backup_retention_days

  # Note 2: zone = "1" combined with standby_availability_zone = "2" enables
  # High Availability with a synchronous hot standby in a different AZ.
  # Automatic failover takes < 120 seconds.
  high_availability {
    mode                      = var.environment == "production" ? "ZoneRedundant" : "Disabled"
    standby_availability_zone = "2"   # <-- CHANGE THIS
  }

  zone = "1"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: primary availability zone

  administrator_login    = "psqladmin"    # <-- CHANGE THIS: admin username
  administrator_password = var.db_admin_password

  storage_mb = 32768   # <-- CHANGE THIS: 32 GiB minimum

  tags = {
    Project     = var.project
    Environment = var.environment
  }
}

# ── Geo-Restore (DR) ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Note 3: Geo-restore creates a new server from the geo-redundant backup
# in the paired region. This is a MANUAL step — it is not automatic.
#
# To perform geo-restore via az CLI (not Terraform — do this during a DR event):
#   az postgres flexible-server geo-restore \
#     --name psql-<project>-<env>-dr \
#     --resource-group <dr-rg> \
#     --location westus \
#     --source-server /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.DBforPostgreSQL/flexibleServers/psql-<project>-<env> \
#     --geo-redundant-backup Enabled
#
# Geo-restore RPO: ~1 hour (replication lag to paired region)
# Geo-restore RTO: ~30–60 minutes to provision a new server

# ── Point-in-Time Restore ────────────────────────────────────
# Note 4: To restore to a specific point in time (up to backup_retention_days ago):
#   az postgres flexible-server restore \
#     --name psql-<project>-<env>-restored \
#     --resource-group <rg> \
#     --source-server psql-<project>-<env> \
#     --restore-time "2024-01-15T02:00:00Z"
#
# The restored server is independent — no traffic is automatically redirected.
# Update your connection string after validating the restore.

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GCP Cloud SQL — Backups and Cross-Region Replica
# WHEN TO USE: Add to your existing Terraform module that creates a
#   Cloud SQL instance. Enables automated backups, PITR, and optionally
#   creates a cross-region read replica for DR.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Variables at the top. Integrate into your existing module.
# RELATED FILES: terraform/gcp-gke/
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

variable "project_id" {}                                # <-- CHANGE THIS: GCP project ID
variable "project" {}
variable "environment" {}
variable "region" { default = "us-central1" }           # <-- CHANGE THIS
variable "dr_region" { default = "us-east1" }           # <-- CHANGE THIS: DR region
variable "db_tier" { default = "db-custom-4-15360" }    # <-- CHANGE THIS: 4 vCPU, 15 GB RAM
variable "postgres_version" { default = "POSTGRES_15" } # <-- CHANGE THIS
variable "backup_start_time" { default = "02:00" }      # <-- CHANGE THIS: HH:MM UTC
variable "backup_retained_backups" { default = 7 }      # <-- CHANGE THIS: 1–365
variable "enable_dr_replica" { default = false }        # <-- CHANGE THIS: set true to create cross-region replica

# ── Cloud SQL Instance with PITR ─────────────────────────────
resource "google_sql_database_instance" "main" {
  name             = "sql-${var.project}-${var.environment}"
  project          = var.project_id
  region           = var.region
  database_version = var.postgres_version

  deletion_protection = var.environment == "production" ? true : false

  settings {
    tier              = var.db_tier
    availability_type = var.environment == "production" ? "REGIONAL" : "ZONAL"  # Note 1: REGIONAL = HA with automatic failover

    backup_configuration {
      enabled    = true
      start_time = var.backup_start_time

      # Note 2: point_in_time_recovery_enabled uses transaction logs to allow
      # restore to any second within the retained_backups * 1-day window.
      # Requires binary logging (PostgreSQL: WAL archiving enabled automatically).
      point_in_time_recovery_enabled = true
      transaction_log_retention_days = 7   # <-- CHANGE THIS

      backup_retention_settings {
        retained_backups = var.backup_retained_backups
        retention_unit   = "COUNT"
      }
    }

    ip_configuration {
      ipv4_enabled    = false   # Note 3: Disable public IP — use Cloud SQL Auth Proxy or Private IP
      private_network = google_compute_network.main.id  # <-- CHANGE THIS: reference your VPC
    }

    maintenance_window {
      day          = 7   # Sunday
      hour         = 4   # 04:00 UTC
      update_track = "stable"
    }

    insights_config {
      query_insights_enabled  = true
      record_application_tags = true
      record_client_address   = false
    }
  }
}

# ── Cross-Region Read Replica for DR ─────────────────────────
# Note 4: Cloud SQL read replicas in a different region serve as the
# DR target. During a DR event, promote the replica:
#   gcloud sql instances promote-replica sql-<project>-<env>-dr --project <project>
# Promotion takes ~5-10 minutes. The replica becomes a standalone primary.
resource "google_sql_database_instance" "dr_replica" {
  count = var.enable_dr_replica ? 1 : 0

  name                 = "sql-${var.project}-${var.environment}-dr"
  project              = var.project_id
  region               = var.dr_region
  database_version     = var.postgres_version
  master_instance_name = google_sql_database_instance.main.name

  deletion_protection = true

  settings {
    tier = var.db_tier

    # Note 5: Read replicas use async replication. Typical lag is < 5 seconds
    # under normal load but can increase during heavy write bursts.
    # Monitor replication lag in Cloud SQL metrics: database/replication/replica_lag.
    backup_configuration {
      enabled = true

      backup_retention_settings {
        retained_backups = var.backup_retained_backups
      }
    }

    ip_configuration {
      ipv4_enabled = false
    }
  }
}

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Install Velero on EKS with S3 backend (IRSA)
# WHEN TO USE: First-time Velero install on an EKS cluster.
#   Uses IRSA for authentication — no static AWS credentials
#   are stored inside the cluster.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - eksctl, velero CLI, kubectl configured against your cluster
#   - An S3 bucket for backup storage already created
#   - An IAM role with the Velero permissions policy attached
#     (see: https://velero.io/docs/main/contributions/aws-config/)
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Variables in the CONFIG section below
# RELATED FILES: backup/velero/schedule.yaml
# ============================================================

set -euo pipefail

# ── CONFIG ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
CLUSTER_NAME="my-cluster"                      # <-- CHANGE THIS
AWS_REGION="us-east-1"                         # <-- CHANGE THIS
BUCKET_NAME="my-cluster-velero-backups"        # <-- CHANGE THIS: S3 bucket name (must already exist)
VELERO_NAMESPACE="velero"
IAM_ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/velero-irsa-role"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
VELERO_VERSION="v1.13.0"                       # <-- CHANGE THIS: pin to a version
# ── END CONFIG ───────────────────────────────────────────────

echo "Installing Velero ${VELERO_VERSION} on cluster: ${CLUSTER_NAME}"
echo "S3 bucket: ${BUCKET_NAME} in ${AWS_REGION}"

# Install Velero with the AWS plugin using IRSA
velero install \
  --provider aws \
  --plugins velero/velero-plugin-for-aws:${VELERO_VERSION} \
  --bucket "${BUCKET_NAME}" \
  --backup-location-config region="${AWS_REGION}" \
  --snapshot-location-config region="${AWS_REGION}" \
  --namespace "${VELERO_NAMESPACE}" \
  --no-secret \
  --sa-annotations "eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn=${IAM_ROLE_ARN}" \
  --features=EnableCSI

# Wait for Velero pod to be ready
echo "Waiting for Velero deployment to be ready..."
kubectl rollout status deployment/velero -n "${VELERO_NAMESPACE}" --timeout=120s

# Verify BackupStorageLocation is available
echo "Checking BackupStorageLocation..."
kubectl get backupstoragelocation -n "${VELERO_NAMESPACE}"

echo ""
echo "Velero installed successfully."
echo "Next: kubectl apply -f backup/velero/schedule.yaml"

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Velero On-Demand Namespace Backup
# WHEN TO USE: Before a risky change (major upgrade, data migration,
#   infra rebuild) or to capture a named restore point that
#   falls outside the scheduled backup window.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: backup/velero/schedule.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: velero.io/v1
kind: Backup
metadata:
  # Note 1: Give the backup a descriptive name that includes the reason and date,
  # so the team can identify it without looking at metadata.
  name: pre-upgrade-2024-01-15                # <-- CHANGE THIS: use format pre-<reason>-<YYYY-MM-DD>
  namespace: velero
spec:
  # Note 2: For on-demand backups before a change, scope to only the affected
  # namespaces to keep size manageable and restore time short.
  includedNamespaces:
    - production                              # <-- CHANGE THIS: namespace(s) to back up

  includeClusterResources: true
  snapshotVolumes: true

  # Note 3: ttl for on-demand backups. Shorter than scheduled backups because
  # these are point-in-time snapshots rather than regular retention archives.
  # 168h = 7 days — enough time to validate the change and roll back if needed.
  ttl: 168h0m0s                              # <-- CHANGE THIS: 168h = 7 days

  storageLocation: default                   # <-- CHANGE THIS: BackupStorageLocation name

  hooks:
    resources: []

---
# Note 4: To restore from this backup:
#   velero restore create \
#     --from-backup pre-upgrade-2024-01-15 \
#     --namespace-mappings production:production-restored  # optional: restore into a different NS
#
# To check restore status:
#   velero restore describe <restore-name> --details
#
# To restore a single Deployment:
#   velero restore create \
#     --from-backup pre-upgrade-2024-01-15 \
#     --include-resources deployments \
#     --selector app=my-app

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Velero Backup Schedule
# WHEN TO USE: After Velero is installed. Creates a recurring
#   backup of all namespaces (or a filtered subset) stored in
#   the configured BackupStorageLocation (S3 / Azure Blob / GCS).
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: backup/velero/aws-install.sh
#                backup/velero/namespace-backup.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: velero.io/v1
kind: Schedule
metadata:
  name: daily-full-backup                     # <-- CHANGE THIS
  namespace: velero
spec:
  # Note 1: Cron syntax — this runs at 02:00 UTC every day.
  # Adjust to a low-traffic window in your time zone.
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"                       # <-- CHANGE THIS

  template:
    # Note 2: ttl controls how long backups are retained before Velero
    # automatically deletes them. 720h = 30 days. Balance retention
    # requirements against storage cost.
    ttl: 720h0m0s                             # <-- CHANGE THIS: 720h = 30 days

    # Note 3: To back up only specific namespaces, replace the empty
    # includedNamespaces list with your target namespaces.
    # An empty list or omitted field means ALL namespaces.
    includedNamespaces:
      - "*"                                   # <-- CHANGE THIS: e.g., [default, production, monitoring]

    excludedNamespaces:
      - kube-system
      - kube-public
      - kube-node-lease
      - velero                                # Never back up Velero itself

    # Note 4: includeClusterResources: true backs up cluster-scoped objects
    # like Namespaces, PersistentVolumes, ClusterRoles, and CustomResourceDefinitions.
    # Set to false if you manage cluster resources separately via GitOps.
    includeClusterResources: true

    # Note 5: snapshotVolumes: true takes CSI VolumeSnapshots of PersistentVolumes.
    # Requires the CSI snapshot controller and VolumeSnapshotClass to be installed,
    # and the --features=EnableCSI flag in the Velero install command.
    snapshotVolumes: true

    storageLocation: default                  # <-- CHANGE THIS: name of your BackupStorageLocation

    # Note 6: Label selectors let you include/exclude specific workloads.
    # Uncomment to filter by label instead of namespace.
    # labelSelector:
    #   matchLabels:
    #     backup: "enabled"

    hooks: {}

  useOwnerReferencesInBackup: false

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: ArgoCD App of Apps — Multi-Application Management
# WHEN TO USE: Managing multiple ArgoCD Applications from one parent
# PREREQUISITES: ArgoCD installed, Application manifests in Git
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: repoURL, path to Application manifests directory
# RELATED FILES: cd/gitops/argocd/application.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# ArgoCD App of Apps pattern
# One application that manages all other applications
# Note 1: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
# Note 2: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
metadata:
  name: app-of-apps
  # Note 3: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  project: default

  source:
    # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    repoURL: https://github.com/my-org/gitops-repo.git
    targetRevision: HEAD
    # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    path: argocd/apps   # Directory containing Application manifests

  destination:
    # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: argocd

  # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      prune: true
      selfHeal: true

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: ArgoCD Application — Single Application
# WHEN TO USE: Deploying one service via ArgoCD GitOps
# PREREQUISITES: ArgoCD installed, Git repo accessible from cluster
# SECRETS NEEDED: None (ArgoCD uses its own repo credentials)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: repoURL, path, destination namespace
# RELATED FILES: cd/gitops/argocd/app-of-apps.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# ArgoCD Application — single app
# Note 1: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
# Note 2: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
metadata:
  name: my-app
  # Note 3: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
  namespace: argocd
  finalizers:
    # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    - resources-finalizer.argocd.argoproj.io  # Cascade delete on app removal
spec:
  # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  project: default

  source:
    # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    repoURL: https://github.com/my-org/my-app.git
    targetRevision: HEAD
    # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    path: cd/kubernetes/_overlays/prod

  destination:
    # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc  # In-cluster
    namespace: production

  # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      prune: true       # Remove resources deleted from Git
      selfHeal: true    # Revert manual changes in cluster
      # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      allowEmpty: false
    syncOptions:
      # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - CreateNamespace=true
      - PrunePropagationPolicy=foreground
    # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    retry:
      limit: 3
      backoff:
        duration: 5s
        factor: 2
        maxDuration: 2m

  # Health check customization
  ignoreDifferences:
    - group: apps
      kind: Deployment
      jsonPointers:
        - /spec/replicas  # Ignore HPA-managed replica count

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: ArgoCD ApplicationSet — Auto-generate Applications
# WHEN TO USE: Creating one ArgoCD Application per service/environment
# PREREQUISITES: ArgoCD installed, ApplicationSet controller
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: repoURL, directory path, template namespace
# RELATED FILES: cd/gitops/argocd/application.yaml
# MATURITY: Beta
# ============================================================

# ArgoCD ApplicationSet — generate apps from a list or Git directories
# Note 1: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: ApplicationSet
# Note 2: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
metadata:
  name: microservices
  # Note 3: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  generators:
    # Git generator: create one app per directory in the repo
    - git:
        # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        repoURL: https://github.com/my-org/gitops-repo.git
        revision: HEAD
        # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        directories:
          - path: services/*

    # List generator: create apps from a hardcoded list
    # - list:
    #     elements:
    #       - service: user-service
    #         namespace: production
    #       - service: order-service
    #         namespace: production

  # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  template:
    metadata:
      # Note 8: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
      name: "{{path.basename}}"
    spec:
      # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      project: default
      source:
        # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        repoURL: https://github.com/my-org/gitops-repo.git
        targetRevision: HEAD
        # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        path: "{{path}}"
      destination:
        # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
        namespace: production
      # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      syncPolicy:
        automated:
          prune: true
          selfHeal: true

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Flux Kustomization — GitOps Reconciliation
# WHEN TO USE: Deploying Kubernetes manifests via FluxCD
# PREREQUISITES: Flux installed, GitRepository source configured
# SECRETS NEEDED: Git credentials (configured in Flux)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: sourceRef, path, interval, namespace
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/_overlays/prod/kustomization.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Flux Kustomization — reconcile Kubernetes manifests from Git
# Note 1: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
apiVersion: kustomize.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v1
kind: Kustomization
# Note 2: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
metadata:
  name: my-app
  # Note 3: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
  namespace: flux-system
spec:
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  interval: 5m      # Reconcile every 5 minutes
  path: ./cd/kubernetes/_overlays/prod
  # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  prune: true       # Remove resources deleted from Git
  sourceRef:
    # Note 6: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
    kind: GitRepository
    name: my-app-repo

  # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  healthChecks:
    - apiVersion: apps/v1
      # Note 8: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
      kind: Deployment
      name: app
      # Note 9: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
      namespace: production

  # Post-build variable substitution
  postBuild:
    # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    substituteFrom:
      - kind: ConfigMap
        name: flux-vars
      - kind: Secret
        name: flux-secrets
        optional: true

﻿# Note 1: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  # Note 2: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
  name: {{ include "webapp.fullname" . }}
  labels:
    {{- include "webapp.labels" . | nindent 4 }}
# Note 3: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
spec:
  {{- if not .Values.autoscaling.enabled }}
  replicas: {{ .Values.replicaCount }}
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  {{- end }}
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      {{- include "webapp.selectorLabels" . | nindent 6 }}
  template:
    metadata:
      # Note 6: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
      labels:
        {{- include "webapp.selectorLabels" . | nindent 8 }}
    spec:
      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      {{- with .Values.imagePullSecrets }}
      imagePullSecrets:
        {{- toYaml . | nindent 8 }}
      # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      {{- end }}
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        runAsUser: 1000
        fsGroup: 2000
      containers:
        # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        - name: {{ .Chart.Name }}
          image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag | default .Chart.AppVersion }}"
          imagePullPolicy: {{ .Values.image.pullPolicy }}
          # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: {{ .Values.service.targetPort }}
              # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              protocol: TCP
          resources:
            {{- toYaml .Values.resources | nindent 12 }}
          # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          securityContext:
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
            # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            capabilities:
              drop: ["ALL"]
          {{- if .Values.probes }}
          # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: {{ .Values.probes.liveness.path }}
              # Note 16: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              port: http
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 20
          # Note 17: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: {{ .Values.probes.readiness.path }}
              # Note 18: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              port: http
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          {{- end }}
          {{- if .Values.env }}
          env:
            {{- range $key, $value := .Values.env }}
            - name: {{ $key }}
              value: {{ $value | quote }}
            {{- end }}
          {{- end }}
          volumeMounts:
            - name: tmp
              mountPath: /tmp
      volumes:
        - name: tmp
          emptyDir: {}

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: cert-manager Bootstrap — ClusterIssuer + Let's Encrypt
# WHEN TO USE: First-time cert-manager setup on any Kubernetes cluster.
#              Provisions TLS certificates automatically via ACME
#              (Let's Encrypt) for all Ingress resources that reference
#              the cluster-issuer annotation.
#
# INSTALL ORDER:
#   Step 1 — Install cert-manager CRDs + controller (once per cluster)
#   Step 2 — Apply this file (ClusterIssuers)
#   Step 3 — Annotate your Ingress (see _base/ingress.yaml) — done
#
# STEP 1 — Install cert-manager via Helm (run once):
#
#   helm repo add jetstack https://charts.jetstack.io
#   helm repo update
#   helm upgrade --install cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager \
#     --namespace cert-manager --create-namespace \
#     --version v1.14.4 \                          # <-- CHANGE THIS: use latest stable
#     --set crds.enabled=true \
#     --set global.leaderElection.namespace=cert-manager \
#     --set prometheus.enabled=true \              # recommended for production
#     --set webhook.timeoutSeconds=30
#
#   # Verify all pods are Running before applying this file:
#   kubectl get pods -n cert-manager
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/_base/ingress.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

---
# ── 1. Staging ClusterIssuer (use FIRST to avoid rate-limit hits) ─────────
# Let's Encrypt staging has very generous rate limits but produces
# untrusted certificates.  Use it during initial setup and CI testing.
#
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: ClusterIssuer
metadata:
  name: letsencrypt-staging
spec:
  acme:
    server: https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
    email: platform@example.com                 # <-- CHANGE THIS: your ops email
    privateKeySecretRef:
      name: letsencrypt-staging-account-key     # stored in cert-manager namespace
    solvers:
      # HTTP-01 challenge: works for any public-facing cluster.
      # For private clusters or wildcard certs, use DNS-01 (see below).
      - http01:
          ingress:
            ingressClassName: nginx             # <-- CHANGE THIS: match your ingress class

---
# ── 2. Production ClusterIssuer ──────────────────────────────────────────
# Only switch to this after staging works — production has strict
# rate limits (50 certs/domain/week).
#
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: ClusterIssuer
metadata:
  name: letsencrypt-prod
spec:
  acme:
    server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
    email: platform@example.com                 # <-- CHANGE THIS
    privateKeySecretRef:
      name: letsencrypt-prod-account-key
    solvers:
      - http01:
          ingress:
            ingressClassName: nginx             # <-- CHANGE THIS

---
# ── 3. DNS-01 ClusterIssuer (for wildcard or private-cluster certs) ───────
# Uncomment and configure one of the DNS provider blocks.
# See https://cert-manager.io/docs/configuration/acme/dns01/
#
# --- Route53 (AWS) ---
# apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
# kind: ClusterIssuer
# metadata:
#   name: letsencrypt-prod-dns
# spec:
#   acme:
#     server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
#     email: platform@example.com
#     privateKeySecretRef:
#       name: letsencrypt-prod-dns-key
#     solvers:
#       - dns01:
#           route53:
#             region: us-east-1               # <-- CHANGE THIS
#             hostedZoneID: ABCDEF1234567     # <-- CHANGE THIS
#             role: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/cert-manager-route53  # <-- CHANGE THIS (IRSA)
#
# --- Azure DNS ---
# apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
# kind: ClusterIssuer
# metadata:
#   name: letsencrypt-prod-dns
# spec:
#   acme:
#     server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
#     email: platform@example.com
#     privateKeySecretRef:
#       name: letsencrypt-prod-dns-key
#     solvers:
#       - dns01:
#           azureDNS:
#             subscriptionID: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000  # <-- CHANGE THIS
#             resourceGroupName: dns-rg                              # <-- CHANGE THIS
#             hostedZoneName: example.com                           # <-- CHANGE THIS
#             environment: AzurePublicCloud
#             managedIdentity:
#               clientID: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000      # <-- CHANGE THIS (workload identity)
#
# --- Cloud DNS (GCP) ---
# apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
# kind: ClusterIssuer
# metadata:
#   name: letsencrypt-prod-dns
# spec:
#   acme:
#     server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
#     email: platform@example.com
#     privateKeySecretRef:
#       name: letsencrypt-prod-dns-key
#     solvers:
#       - dns01:
#           cloudDNS:
#             project: my-gcp-project         # <-- CHANGE THIS
#             serviceAccountSecretRef:
#               name: clouddns-sa-key         # <-- CHANGE THIS: k8s secret holding SA JSON key
#               key: key.json

---
# ── 4. Certificate — explicit (optional, Ingress annotation is simpler) ───
# When you need full control over the Certificate resource, define it
# explicitly instead of relying on cert-manager's ingress-shim.
# Most teams just use the annotation on the Ingress and skip this.
#
# apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
# kind: Certificate
# metadata:
#   name: app-tls
#   namespace: default               # <-- CHANGE THIS: must match the Ingress namespace
# spec:
#   secretName: app-tls              # must match Ingress tls[].secretName
#   duration: 2160h                  # 90 days (Let's Encrypt default)
#   renewBefore: 360h                # renew 15 days before expiry
#   issuerRef:
#     name: letsencrypt-prod
#     kind: ClusterIssuer
#   dnsNames:
#     - app.example.com              # <-- CHANGE THIS
#     - "*.app.example.com"          # wildcard — requires DNS-01 issuer above

---
# ── 5. Ingress reference reminder ────────────────────────────────────────
# Your Ingress (cd/kubernetes/_base/ingress.yaml) already has:
#
#   annotations:
#     cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-prod
#   spec:
#     tls:
#       - hosts: [app.example.com]
#         secretName: app-tls
#
# That is all cert-manager needs to issue and renew the certificate.
# cert-manager watches for Ingress resources with this annotation and
# creates a Certificate object automatically (ingress-shim).

            # Note 16: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

      http:
        paths:

            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: app
                port:
