﻿# Note 1: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

# Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
resources:
  - deployment.yaml
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  - service.yaml
  - ingress.yaml
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  - hpa.yaml
  - configmap.yaml
  # Note 5: pdb.yaml — PodDisruptionBudget. Prevents more than one pod being
  # evicted at once during node drains and cluster upgrades. Required for HA.
  # Overlays can patch spec.minAvailable for environment-specific thresholds.
  - pdb.yaml
  # Note 6: networkpolicy.yaml — default-deny-all + targeted allow rules.
  # Requires a CNI that enforces NetworkPolicy (Calico, Cilium, Weave).
  # Remove this line if your cluster uses Flannel without a network policy add-on.
  - networkpolicy.yaml
  # Note 7: rbac.yaml — ServiceAccounts, Roles, and RoleBindings for the
  # developer, ci-deploy, and ns-admin personas. Scoped to this namespace.
  - rbac.yaml
  # Note 8: vpa.yaml — VerticalPodAutoscaler in "Off" (recommendation-only) mode.
  # Do NOT switch to Auto/Recreate mode if hpa.yaml uses CPU/memory targets —
  # see the warning comment inside vpa.yaml for the full explanation.
  - vpa.yaml

commonLabels:
  managed-by: kustomize

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Allow egress from app pods to the database
# WHEN TO USE: Apply this to namespaces that run application pods
#   that connect to a PostgreSQL (or other) database running in a
#   separate namespace. Allows only the required port; all other
#   egress remains blocked by default-deny.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: default-deny.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-database
  namespace: default          # <-- CHANGE THIS: namespace where app pods run
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app             # <-- CHANGE THIS: label that identifies the app pods needing DB access

  policyTypes:
    - Egress

  egress:
    - to:
        # Note 1: namespaceSelector restricts this rule to pods inside the
        # database namespace. Using both namespaceSelector + podSelector
        # requires the traffic to originate from a pod matching BOTH selectors.
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: database   # <-- CHANGE THIS: namespace where DB pods run
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres                           # <-- CHANGE THIS: label on DB pods
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432    # <-- CHANGE THIS: 5432 = PostgreSQL, 3306 = MySQL, 1433 = MSSQL, 6379 = Redis

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Allow egress to CoreDNS (DNS resolution)
# WHEN TO USE: Apply this after default-deny in every namespace.
#   Without DNS egress, pods cannot resolve any hostname including
#   Kubernetes service names, which breaks virtually every workload.
#   This is almost always the first allow-egress rule you need.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: default-deny.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dns
  namespace: default          # <-- CHANGE THIS: apply to each namespace that needs DNS
spec:
  podSelector: {}             # Note 1: applies to all pods in the namespace

  policyTypes:
    - Egress

  egress:
    # Note 2: CoreDNS runs in kube-system on port 53 (UDP and TCP).
    # UDP is used for queries that fit in a single packet; TCP is the
    # fallback for large responses. Both must be allowed.
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              k8s-app: kube-dns
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Allow ingress from the ingress controller
# WHEN TO USE: Apply this after default-deny to permit HTTP/HTTPS
#   traffic from the NGINX (or other) ingress controller into
#   application pods. Without this policy, the ingress controller
#   cannot forward requests to pods even though an Ingress resource
#   exists.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: default-deny.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-ingress-from-ingress-controller
  namespace: default                          # <-- CHANGE THIS: target namespace
spec:
  # Note 1: Label-select only the pods that expose an HTTP port.
  # Adjust the selector to match your application's pod labels.
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app                             # <-- CHANGE THIS

  policyTypes:
    - Ingress

  ingress:
    - from:
        # Note 2: namespaceSelector + podSelector together act as a logical AND —
        # the source pod must be in the ingress-nginx namespace AND have the
        # ingress-controller label. This is more specific than allowing the
        # entire ingress namespace.
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: ingress-nginx  # <-- CHANGE THIS: namespace where your ingress controller runs
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app.kubernetes.io/name: ingress-nginx        # <-- CHANGE THIS: label on the ingress controller pod

      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8080    # <-- CHANGE THIS: port your application container listens on

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Allow ingress from Prometheus for metrics scraping
# WHEN TO USE: Apply this in every namespace where application pods
#   expose a /metrics endpoint (Prometheus scrape port). Without this
#   policy, Prometheus in the monitoring namespace cannot reach the
#   metrics port and will report pods as scrape targets DOWN.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: default-deny.yaml
#                observability/prometheus/
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-prometheus-scrape
  namespace: default          # <-- CHANGE THIS: namespace where app pods expose /metrics
spec:
  # Note 1: Only allow scraping for pods that expose a metrics port.
  # Restrict by label to avoid accidentally opening all pods in the namespace.
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      monitoring: "enabled"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: label to identify pods that expose /metrics

  policyTypes:
    - Ingress

  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: monitoring    # <-- CHANGE THIS: namespace where Prometheus runs
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app.kubernetes.io/name: prometheus         # <-- CHANGE THIS: label on Prometheus pod
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 9090    # <-- CHANGE THIS: your application's metrics port (commonly 9090, 8080, 8081)

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Default Deny — all ingress and egress
# WHEN TO USE: Apply this first in every namespace before any
#   allow policies. It establishes a zero-trust baseline: no pod
#   can receive or send traffic unless explicitly permitted.
#
# APPLY ORDER: This policy must be applied BEFORE any allow policies.
# RELATED FILES: allow-ingress-from-ingress-controller.yaml
#                allow-egress-to-dns.yaml
#                allow-egress-to-database.yaml
#                allow-prometheus-scrape.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: default           # <-- CHANGE THIS: apply to each namespace individually
spec:
  podSelector: {}              # Note 1: empty podSelector matches ALL pods in the namespace
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  # Note 2: No ingress/egress rules defined = deny all traffic.
  # Add allow policies in separate files — one concern per file —
  # so the intent of each permission is clear and reviewable.

apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

# Note 1: Apply default-deny BEFORE the allow policies.
# Kustomize applies resources in order; listing default-deny first
# ensures no window exists where pods are open before the deny rule is in place.
resources:
  - default-deny.yaml
  - allow-egress-to-dns.yaml                         # Required by all pods — DNS resolution
  - allow-ingress-from-ingress-controller.yaml        # Internet traffic via NGINX/Traefik
  - allow-egress-to-database.yaml                     # Outbound to PostgreSQL
  - allow-prometheus-scrape.yaml                      # Inbound from Prometheus

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy — default-deny + allow rules
# WHEN TO USE: Restrict pod-to-pod and pod-to-external traffic.
#   Apply in any namespace that requires network isolation.
#   Start with default-deny-all, then add targeted allow rules.
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - A CNI plugin that enforces NetworkPolicy:
#     Calico, Cilium, Weave Net, Antrea, or AWS VPC CNI (with policy enabled)
#   - Flannel does NOT enforce NetworkPolicy — install Calico on top of it
#   - Verify support: kubectl get networkpolicies -A (should not error)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES:
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/service.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# ── Policy 1: Default deny all ingress and egress ────────────────────────
# Note 1: Apply this policy FIRST before any allow rules. It creates a
# "zero trust" baseline: nothing is allowed unless explicitly permitted below.
# The empty podSelector ({}) matches ALL pods in the namespace.
# The empty ingress/egress arrays mean: no traffic is allowed at all.
#
# Apply order matters: if a pod matches multiple NetworkPolicy objects,
# Kubernetes takes the UNION of all matching rules. A deny policy with no
# rules and an allow policy with rules = traffic is allowed (allow wins).
# This "default deny" only works because it has no ingress/egress rules.
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: default    # <-- CHANGE THIS: apply to each namespace that needs isolation
spec:
  podSelector: {}   # matches all pods in the namespace
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  # Note 2: No ingress: or egress: keys = deny all. Adding an empty array
  # (ingress: []) has the same effect and is more explicit.

---
# ── Policy 2: Allow ingress from pods in the SAME namespace ──────────────
# Note 3: This lets services within the same namespace talk to each other
# (e.g., a web pod calling an api pod). Remove this if you want per-service
# isolation and instead add more specific policies below.
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-ingress-same-namespace
  namespace: default    # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  podSelector: {}   # applies to all pods in the namespace
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}   # any pod in THIS namespace (namespaceSelector not needed here)

---
# ── Policy 3: Allow ingress from the ingress controller ──────────────────
# Note 4: The ingress-nginx controller lives in the ingress-nginx namespace.
# Without this rule, external HTTP/S traffic routed by ingress.yaml will be
# blocked at the pod level even though the Ingress object exists.
# Adjust namespaceSelector.matchLabels to match your ingress controller namespace.
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-ingress-from-ingress-controller
  namespace: default    # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: app            # <-- CHANGE THIS: only allow traffic to the app pods (not all pods)
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        # Note 5: Namespace must be labelled for the selector to work. Verify with:
        #   kubectl get namespace ingress-nginx --show-labels
        # Label it if missing:
        #   kubectl label namespace ingress-nginx kubernetes.io/metadata.name=ingress-nginx
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: ingress-nginx   # <-- CHANGE THIS: your ingress namespace
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8080    # <-- CHANGE THIS: your container port

---
# ── Policy 4: Allow egress to kube-dns (required for DNS resolution) ─────
# Note 6: Without this rule, pods cannot resolve any DNS name, breaking
# almost all outbound connections. This is almost always needed when using
# default-deny-all. kube-dns runs in kube-system on port 53 (UDP + TCP).
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-kube-dns
  namespace: default    # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              k8s-app: kube-dns
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53     # Note 7: TCP fallback for large DNS responses (>512 bytes)

---
# ── Policy 5: Allow egress to pods in the SAME namespace ─────────────────
# Note 8: Required for any service-to-service calls within the namespace
# (e.g., app → database, app → cache). Pair with Policy 2 (allow ingress
# from same namespace) so both sides of the connection are permitted.
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-same-namespace
  namespace: default    # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}   # any pod in THIS namespace

---
# ── Policy 6: Allow egress to specific external endpoint (optional) ───────
# Note 9: Use ipBlock to allow egress to an external IP range that is not
# inside the cluster — e.g., a managed database, an external API, or a
# monitoring endpoint. Remove this policy if you don't need external egress.
#
# Example: allow only the app pods to reach an external DB on port 5432.
# Replace the CIDR and port with your actual values.
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-external-db
  namespace: default    # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: app          # <-- CHANGE THIS: restrict to pods that actually need DB access
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - ipBlock:
            cidr: 10.0.0.0/24     # <-- CHANGE THIS: your external DB / service CIDR
            except:
              - 10.0.0.0/28       # <-- CHANGE THIS: exclude any subnets within the CIDR if needed (or remove)
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432              # <-- CHANGE THIS: your service port

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Kubernetes PodDisruptionBudget
# WHEN TO USE: Prevent Kubernetes from evicting too many pods at
#   once during voluntary disruptions (node drain, rolling updates,
#   cluster upgrades). Apply to any Deployment with >= 2 replicas.
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - Deployment with >= 2 replicas (PDB has no effect on 1 replica)
#   - Label selector must match the Deployment's pod template labels
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES:
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/hpa.yaml
#   cd/kubernetes/_overlays/prod/kustomization.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: PodDisruptionBudget is a policy/v1 resource (GA since k8s 1.21).
# For clusters < 1.21, use policy/v1beta1 (deprecated and removed in 1.25).
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  # Note 2: By convention, name the PDB after the Deployment it protects.
  name: app-pdb             # <-- CHANGE THIS: e.g., my-service-pdb
  namespace: default        # <-- CHANGE THIS: must match your Deployment namespace
spec:
  # Note 3: Choose EITHER minAvailable OR maxUnavailable — not both.
  #
  # minAvailable: N  — at least N pods must remain running during disruption.
  #   Use this when you need a hard guarantee on running capacity.
  #   Example: minAvailable: 1  → keep at least 1 pod healthy at all times
  #   Example: minAvailable: "50%"  → percentage-based (rounds up)
  #
  # maxUnavailable: N — at most N pods may be simultaneously unavailable.
  #   Use this for proportional guarantees that scale with replica count.
  #   Example: maxUnavailable: 1  → allow 1 pod to be disrupted at a time
  #   Example: maxUnavailable: "25%"  → allow 25% of pods to be disrupted
  #
  # For production with 3 replicas: minAvailable: 2 (or maxUnavailable: 1)
  # For staging/dev with 2 replicas: minAvailable: 1 (or maxUnavailable: 1)
  # For HPA-managed Deployments: prefer maxUnavailable so the PDB scales
  # with the replica count rather than becoming overly restrictive.
  minAvailable: 1           # <-- CHANGE THIS: see guidance above

  # Note 4: The selector MUST match the pod template labels in your Deployment.
  # Mismatched labels = PDB silently does nothing. Verify with:
  #   kubectl get pdb app-pdb -o yaml
  #   kubectl describe pdb app-pdb    ← shows "Allowed disruptions" count
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: app              # <-- CHANGE THIS: match deployment.yaml spec.selector.matchLabels

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Kubernetes RBAC — ServiceAccounts, Roles, RoleBindings
# WHEN TO USE: Grant least-privilege access to users, groups, and
#   workloads within a namespace. Three common personas are included:
#     1. developer  — read-only access to observe running workloads
#     2. ci-deploy  — deploy access for CI pipelines (no secret read)
#     3. ns-admin   — full namespace control for namespace owners
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - Kubernetes >= 1.20 (stable RBAC API)
#   - Namespace must exist before applying RoleBindings
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES:
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/configmap.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# PERSONA 1: Developer (read-only)
# Purpose: Let developers view running resources without modifying anything.
# Typical subjects: AD groups, individual users, dev-facing service accounts
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Note 1: A ServiceAccount is for workloads (pods). For human users, skip the
# ServiceAccount and set subject.kind: User or Group in the RoleBinding instead.
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: developer               # <-- CHANGE THIS: e.g., team-name-developer
  namespace: default            # <-- CHANGE THIS
  annotations:
    # Note 2: Description annotations help future operators understand purpose.
    description: "Read-only access for developers to observe namespace resources"

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: developer-role
  namespace: default            # <-- CHANGE THIS
rules:
  # Note 3: Principle of least privilege — list only the specific resources
  # and verbs this persona actually needs. Avoid wildcards (*) in production.
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log", "services", "endpoints", "configmaps", "events"]
    verbs:     ["get", "list", "watch"]

  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments", "replicasets", "statefulsets", "daemonsets"]
    verbs:     ["get", "list", "watch"]

  - apiGroups: ["autoscaling"]
    resources: ["horizontalpodautoscalers"]
    verbs:     ["get", "list", "watch"]

  - apiGroups: ["policy"]
    resources: ["poddisruptionbudgets"]
    verbs:     ["get", "list", "watch"]

  # Note 4: Developers do NOT get secrets access here. To allow reading
  # non-sensitive secret metadata (not values), add a separate rule:
  # - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["secrets"] verbs: ["list"]
  # Never grant: secrets/get, secrets/watch — these expose secret values.

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: developer-rolebinding
  namespace: default            # <-- CHANGE THIS
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind:     Role
  name:     developer-role
subjects:
  # Note 5: Bind the Role to a ServiceAccount (for pod workloads) and/or
  # a Group (for human users authenticated via your IdP/OIDC provider).
  - kind:      ServiceAccount
    name:      developer
    namespace: default          # <-- CHANGE THIS
  # - kind:  Group
  #   name:  "team-platform-developers"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: OIDC group claim
  #   apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io


# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# PERSONA 2: CI/CD Pipeline (deploy access)
# Purpose: Allow a CI system to deploy workloads without read access to secrets.
# Typical subjects: GitHub Actions OIDC service account, Jenkins agent SA
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: ci-deploy               # <-- CHANGE THIS: e.g., github-actions-deploy
  namespace: default            # <-- CHANGE THIS
  annotations:
    description: "CI/CD pipeline deploy account — least-privilege deployment access"

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: ci-deploy-role
  namespace: default            # <-- CHANGE THIS
rules:
  # Note 6: CI needs to create and update Deployments, ConfigMaps, and Services.
  # It does NOT need to delete them (prevents accidental teardown from pipelines).
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments", "replicasets"]
    verbs:     ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]

  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["services", "configmaps"]
    verbs:     ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]

  # Note 7: CI needs to read pod status to verify rollout health
  # (e.g., kubectl rollout status deployment/app).
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log", "events"]
    verbs:     ["get", "list", "watch"]

  # Note 8: If your CI creates or rotates Kubernetes Secret objects (e.g., for
  # image pull secrets), add this. Remove if CI should not touch secrets at all.
  # - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["secrets"] verbs: ["get", "create", "update", "patch"]

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: ci-deploy-rolebinding
  namespace: default            # <-- CHANGE THIS
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind:     Role
  name:     ci-deploy-role
subjects:
  - kind:      ServiceAccount
    name:      ci-deploy
    namespace: default          # <-- CHANGE THIS


# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# PERSONA 3: Namespace Admin (full namespace control)
# Purpose: Allow a team lead or platform engineer to fully manage a namespace.
# Uses a ClusterRole binding to a namespace (NOT a ClusterRoleBinding).
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Note 9: We use the built-in "admin" ClusterRole here rather than a custom one.
# The "admin" ClusterRole grants full control of namespace-scoped resources
# but NOT cluster-scoped resources (nodes, namespaces, PVs, ClusterRoles, etc.).
# This is why we bind it via a RoleBinding (namespace-scoped), not a
# ClusterRoleBinding (cluster-scoped) — preventing privilege escalation.
#
# NEVER use ClusterRoleBinding with "admin" or "cluster-admin" unless the
# subject genuinely needs cluster-wide access. Prefer RoleBinding always.

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: ns-admin-rolebinding
  namespace: default            # <-- CHANGE THIS
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind:     ClusterRole         # Note 10: Referencing ClusterRole but binding via RoleBinding
  name:     admin               # built-in k8s ClusterRole — full namespace access
subjects:
  - kind:      ServiceAccount
    name:      ns-admin         # <-- CHANGE THIS: the SA that should have admin access
    namespace: default          # <-- CHANGE THIS
  # - kind:  Group
  #   name:  "team-platform-leads"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: OIDC group claim
  #   apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: RBAC — CI/CD Deployer (namespace-scoped Role)
# WHEN TO USE: Grant the CI/CD pipeline service account just enough
#   permission to deploy workloads to a specific namespace. This
#   uses a namespace-scoped Role (not ClusterRole) to prevent the
#   pipeline from touching other namespaces.
#
# LEAST PRIVILEGE PRINCIPLE: The CI deployer can update Deployments
#   and ConfigMaps but cannot read Secrets or modify RBAC resources.
#   If your pipeline needs to read secrets (e.g., for env var validation),
#   add `secrets: ["get", "list"]` but NOT `create` or `update`.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/_base/rbac/readonly-developer.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: ci-deployer                          # <-- CHANGE THIS
  namespace: production                      # <-- CHANGE THIS: namespace this SA deploys to
  annotations:
    description: "Service account for CI/CD pipeline deployments"
    # For EKS IRSA (if pipeline runs on EKS):
    # eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/ci-deployer-irsa

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: ci-deployer
  namespace: production                      # <-- CHANGE THIS
rules:
  # Note 1: The pipeline needs to update existing Deployments and create new
  # ones during first-time deploys. It does NOT need delete — use separate
  # runbooks for decommissioning workloads.
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources:
      - deployments
      - replicasets
      - statefulsets
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]

  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources:
      - services
      - configmaps
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]

  # Note 2: The pipeline needs to read pods to check rollout status
  # (e.g., `kubectl rollout status`) but not exec into them.
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources:
      - pods
      - pods/status
      - pods/log
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

  - apiGroups: ["batch"]
    resources:
      - jobs
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "watch"]

  - apiGroups: ["networking.k8s.io"]
    resources:
      - ingresses
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]

  # Note 3: HPA management — needed if your pipeline scales workloads
  # as part of a canary or load-test deploy.
  - apiGroups: ["autoscaling"]
    resources:
      - horizontalpodautoscalers
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: ci-deployer-binding
  namespace: production                      # <-- CHANGE THIS
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: ci-deployer                        # <-- CHANGE THIS: must match the ServiceAccount name above
    namespace: production                    # <-- CHANGE THIS
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: ci-deployer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

resources:
  - readonly-developer.yaml   # ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding: read-only for all developers
  - ci-deployer.yaml          # ServiceAccount + Role + RoleBinding: CI/CD pipeline deployer
  - namespace-admin.yaml      # Role + RoleBinding: full namespace admin for a team

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: RBAC — Namespace Admin (full control within namespace only)
# WHEN TO USE: Grant a team or individual full administrative access
#   to a single namespace (e.g., a team owns the `payments` namespace)
#   without giving them any cluster-level permissions (no node access,
#   no ClusterRole management, no PersistentVolume creation).
#
# DIFFERENCE FROM cluster-admin:
#   - cluster-admin can manage PersistentVolumes, Nodes, ClusterRoles, Namespaces
#   - namespace-admin is bounded to a single namespace
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/_base/rbac/readonly-developer.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin
  namespace: payments                        # <-- CHANGE THIS: the namespace this team owns
rules:
  # Note 1: Granting `*` verbs on core workload resources within a namespace
  # is intentional here — this team owns the namespace completely.
  # The key constraint is that this is a Role (not ClusterRole), so it
  # cannot escape the namespace boundary.
  - apiGroups: ["", "apps", "batch", "autoscaling", "networking.k8s.io", "policy"]
    resources:
      - pods
      - pods/log
      - pods/exec
      - pods/portforward
      - deployments
      - replicasets
      - statefulsets
      - daemonsets
      - services
      - endpoints
      - configmaps
      - persistentvolumeclaims
      - jobs
      - cronjobs
      - horizontalpodautoscalers
      - ingresses
      - networkpolicies
      - poddisruptionbudgets
    verbs: ["*"]

  # Note 2: ServiceAccounts — allow creation so the team can set up workload
  # identity for their services without escalation. They cannot create RBAC
  # bindings (that would allow privilege escalation via impersonation).
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources:
      - serviceaccounts
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]

  # Note 3: Secrets — full CRUD within the namespace. The team must be able
  # to create/update secrets for their workloads, but they cannot access
  # secrets in other namespaces.
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources:
      - secrets
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]

  # Note 4: External Secrets Operator CRDs — allow the team to manage
  # ExternalSecret resources in their own namespace.
  - apiGroups: ["external-secrets.io"]
    resources:
      - externalsecrets
    verbs: ["*"]

  # Note 5: Events — read-only. Events are helpful for debugging but
  # modifying them provides no operational benefit.
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources:
      - events
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin-binding
  namespace: payments                        # <-- CHANGE THIS
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: payments-team                      # <-- CHANGE THIS: your OIDC group or team name
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: namespace-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: RBAC — Read-Only Developer Access (ClusterRole)
# WHEN TO USE: Grant developers view-only access to inspect
#   workloads cluster-wide without the ability to modify anything.
#   Suitable for on-call triage (read logs, describe pods) without
#   write permissions.
#
# SECURITY: This role grants read access to Secrets by name listing
#   only (no data values). Remove the Secrets rule below if even
#   listing secret names is too broad for your threat model.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/_base/rbac/ci-deployer.yaml
#                cd/kubernetes/_base/rbac/namespace-admin.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: readonly-developer
  labels:
    rbac.example.com/aggregate-to-view: "true"  # <-- CHANGE THIS: optional aggregation label
rules:
  # Note 1: The built-in ClusterRole "view" covers most read resources but
  # explicitly excludes Secrets. We start from scratch to be explicit about
  # what is permitted rather than inheriting from "view" and adding exceptions.
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources:
      - pods
      - pods/log
      - pods/status
      - services
      - endpoints
      - configmaps
      - events
      - namespaces
      - nodes               # Cluster-scoped — useful for node capacity checks
      - persistentvolumeclaims
      - replicationcontrollers
      - resourcequotas
      - serviceaccounts
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources:
      - deployments
      - replicasets
      - statefulsets
      - daemonsets
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

  - apiGroups: ["batch"]
    resources:
      - jobs
      - cronjobs
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

  - apiGroups: ["networking.k8s.io"]
    resources:
      - ingresses
      - networkpolicies
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

  - apiGroups: ["autoscaling"]
    resources:
      - horizontalpodautoscalers
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

  - apiGroups: ["policy"]
    resources:
      - poddisruptionbudgets
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

  # Note 2: Secrets — only listing allowed (not reading data values).
  # A user can see secret NAMES but cannot execute `kubectl get secret <name> -o yaml`.
  # Remove this rule if listing secret names is too broad.
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources:
      - secrets
    verbs: ["list"]

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: readonly-developer-binding
subjects:
  # Note 3: Add individual users or a group (e.g., GitHub team mapped via OIDC).
  # Prefer groups over individual users for easier onboarding/offboarding.
  - kind: Group
    name: developers                 # <-- CHANGE THIS: your OIDC group name (e.g., GitHub team)
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: readonly-developer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

kind: Service
metadata:

spec:

    # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Kubernetes VerticalPodAutoscaler (VPA)
# WHEN TO USE: Get CPU/memory request recommendations for pods
#   that are over- or under-resourced. Start in "Off" mode to
#   observe recommendations before enabling automatic updates.
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - VPA controller installed (see install command below)
#   - Metrics Server installed (VPA depends on it for recommendations)
# INSTALL VPA CONTROLLER:
#   kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/releases/latest/download/vertical-pod-autoscaler.yaml
#   # Or via Helm:
#   helm repo add fairwinds-stable https://charts.fairwinds.com/stable
#   helm upgrade --install vpa fairwinds-stable/vpa --namespace vpa --create-namespace
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES:
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/hpa.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: VPA update modes explained:
#   "Off"      — Recommendations only. VPA never modifies pods. Safest starting point.
#                Read recommendations with: kubectl describe vpa app-vpa
#   "Initial"  — Sets resources only on pod CREATION. Running pods are not evicted.
#                Safe for stateful workloads that should not be interrupted.
#   "Recreate" — Evicts and recreates pods when resources are significantly off.
#                Use only if your workload tolerates restarts.
#   "Auto"     — Currently behaves like "Recreate". Future versions may add in-place
#                resize (in-place resource resize graduated to stable in k8s 1.33).
#
# CRITICAL WARNING — VPA + HPA combination rules:
#   - NEVER run VPA (Recreate/Auto) together with HPA (cpu/memory) targets.
#     VPA changes resource requests → HPA recalculates CPU utilisation %
#     against new requests → HPA scales unexpectedly. This creates a fight loop.
#   - SAFE combinations:
#     a) VPA (Off) + HPA (cpu/memory) — VPA provides info only, HPA owns scaling
#     b) VPA (Auto) + HPA (custom metrics: RPS, queue depth) — each owns different axis
#     c) VPA (Auto) alone — VPA owns vertical scaling, no HPA
#   - If using HPA with CPU/memory targets (as in hpa.yaml), keep VPA in "Off" mode.

apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1
kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: app-vpa                 # <-- CHANGE THIS: match your deployment name
  namespace: default            # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  targetRef:
    # Note 2: targetRef points to the workload VPA should manage.
    # Supported: Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, ReplicaSet, Job, CronJob.
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind:       Deployment
    name:       app             # <-- CHANGE THIS: must match Deployment metadata.name

  updatePolicy:
    # Note 3: Start with "Off" to collect recommendations without touching pods.
    # Read current recommendation:
    #   kubectl describe vpa app-vpa | grep -A 20 "Recommendation:"
    # Switch to "Initial" after validating recommendations are reasonable.
    updateMode: "Off"           # <-- CHANGE THIS: Off | Initial | Recreate | Auto

  resourcePolicy:
    containerPolicies:
      - containerName: app      # <-- CHANGE THIS: match your container name in deployment.yaml
        # Note 4: minAllowed prevents VPA from recommending values so low that the
        # container becomes unstable. Always set a floor for production workloads.
        minAllowed:
          cpu:    "50m"         # <-- CHANGE THIS: minimum CPU the container needs
          memory: "64Mi"        # <-- CHANGE THIS: minimum memory the container needs
        # Note 5: maxAllowed caps recommendations to prevent VPA from requesting
        # resources the node cannot provide. Set to ~70-80% of your largest node size.
        maxAllowed:
          cpu:    "2"           # <-- CHANGE THIS: maximum CPU this container should use
          memory: "1Gi"         # <-- CHANGE THIS: maximum memory this container should use
        # Note 6: controlledResources defaults to [cpu, memory]. Set to ["memory"] only
        # if you want HPA to manage CPU (and VPA should only manage memory).
        controlledResources: ["cpu", "memory"]  # <-- CHANGE THIS if using VPA+HPA
        # Note 7: controlledValues:
        #   "RequestsAndLimits" — VPA adjusts both requests and limits (maintains ratio)
        #   "RequestsOnly"      — VPA adjusts requests only, leaves limits unchanged.
        #                         Preferred when you want predictable limits.
        controlledValues: RequestsAndLimits

      # Repeat for each container in the pod (init containers, sidecars, etc.)
      # - containerName: sidecar   # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #   minAllowed:
      #     cpu:    "10m"
      #     memory: "32Mi"
      #   maxAllowed:
      #     cpu:    "500m"
      #     memory: "256Mi"

﻿# Note 1: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

resources:
  # Note 2: The base contains the Deployment, Service, Ingress, HPA, PDB, and ConfigMap.
  # All resources in _base/kustomization.yaml are included automatically.
  - ../../_base

namespace: production

patches:
  # Note 3: Scale the Deployment to 3 replicas in production (base default is 1).
  - patch: |-
      - op: replace
        path: /spec/replicas
        value: 3
    target:
      kind: Deployment
      name: app

  # Note 4: Tighten the PDB for production — with 3 replicas, keep at least 2 healthy.
  # This means at most 1 pod can be disrupted at a time during node drains or upgrades.
  # The base pdb.yaml sets minAvailable: 1 (safe for dev/staging with 2 replicas).
  # This patch upgrades it to minAvailable: 2 for production.
  #
  # IMPORTANT: minAvailable must always be < replica count, otherwise voluntary
  # disruptions (kubectl drain, cluster upgrades) will block indefinitely.
  - patch: |-
      - op: replace
        path: /spec/minAvailable
        value: 2
    target:
      kind: PodDisruptionBudget
      name: app-pdb               # <-- CHANGE THIS: must match pdb.yaml metadata.name

images:
  - name: my-registry/my-app
    newTag: v1.0.0   # Pin to immutable tag in production               # <-- CHANGE THIS
