﻿# Blue-Green Deployment Pattern
# Two identical environments; switch traffic between them
# Note 1: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
---
# Blue deployment (currently active)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
# Note 2: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
metadata:
  name: app-blue
  labels:
    # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    version: blue
spec:
  replicas: 3
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
      # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      version: blue
  template:
    metadata:
      # Note 6: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
      labels:
        app: my-app
        version: blue
    # Note 7: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: app
          # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          image: my-registry/my-app:1.0.0

---
# Green deployment (new version being tested)
apiVersion: apps/v1
# Note 9: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: app-green
  # Note 10: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
  labels:
    version: green
spec:
  # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      app: my-app
      version: green
  template:
    # Note 13: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
        # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        version: green
    spec:
      containers:
        # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        - name: app
          image: my-registry/my-app:2.0.0

---
# Service — switch between blue/green by changing selector
# Note 16: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: app
spec:
  selector:
    app: my-app
    version: blue  # Change to "green" to switch traffic
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080

﻿# Canary Deployment Pattern
# Route small percentage of traffic to new version
# Note 1: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
---
# Stable deployment (90% of replicas)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
# Note 2: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
metadata:
  name: app-stable
spec:
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  replicas: 9
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      app: my-app
      track: stable
  template:
    # Note 5: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
        # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        track: stable
    spec:
      containers:
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        - name: app
          image: my-registry/my-app:1.0.0

---
# Canary deployment (10% of replicas)
# Note 8: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  # Note 9: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
  name: app-canary
spec:
  replicas: 1  # 1 canary : 9 stable = ~10% canary traffic
  # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
      # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      track: canary
  template:
    metadata:
      # Note 12: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
      labels:
        app: my-app
        track: canary
    # Note 13: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: app
          # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          image: my-registry/my-app:2.0.0

---
# Service selects BOTH stable + canary (no track label)
apiVersion: v1
# Note 15: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: app
spec:
  selector:
    app: my-app   # Matches both stable and canary pods
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Database Migration — Helm Pre-Upgrade Hook
# WHEN TO USE: When you manage your application with Helm and want
#   migrations to run automatically before every `helm upgrade`.
#   The upgrade will block until this Job succeeds or fails.
#
# HOW HELM HOOKS WORK:
#   Helm pauses the upgrade lifecycle at the hook phase, waits for all
#   pre-upgrade Jobs to complete (exit 0), then proceeds with the chart
#   upgrade. If the Job fails (non-zero exit), Helm marks the release
#   FAILED and rolls back to the previous revision automatically.
#
# DECISION GUIDE:
#   - Using Helm charts → use this file
#   - Not using Helm, migration < 5 min → use db-migration-init-container.yaml
#   - Not using Helm, migration > 5 min → use db-migration-job.yaml
#
# PLACEMENT IN YOUR CHART:
#   Copy this file to: <chart-root>/templates/db-migration-hook.yaml
#   The values it references (image.repository, image.tag, etc.) follow
#   the naming convention in cd/helm/webapp/values.yaml.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/_patterns/db-migration-job.yaml
#                cd/kubernetes/_patterns/db-migration-init-container.yaml
#                cd/helm/webapp/templates/deployment.yaml
#                docs/guides/database-migrations.md
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: "{{ .Release.Name }}-db-migrate-{{ .Values.image.tag | replace \".\" \"-\" | trunc 20 }}"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: must be unique per release
  namespace: "{{ .Release.Namespace }}"
  labels:
    app: "{{ .Release.Name }}"
    component: db-migrate
    chart: "{{ .Chart.Name }}-{{ .Chart.Version }}"
  annotations:
    # Note 1: `pre-upgrade,pre-install` runs this Job before both first installs
    # and all subsequent upgrades. This covers the "brand-new cluster" case
    # where there is no schema at all.
    "helm.sh/hook": pre-upgrade,pre-install

    # Note 2: Hook weight controls ordering when multiple pre-upgrade hooks exist.
    # Lower (more negative) weights run first. Use -10 for infra setup, -5 for migrations,
    # 0 for seed data. Migrations should always run after schema bootstrapping but before
    # any seed-data hooks.
    "helm.sh/hook-weight": "-5"

    # Note 3: `before-hook-creation` deletes any previous Job object with the same
    # name before creating a new one. Without this, Helm fails if the previous Job
    # still exists (e.g., a failed migration that was never cleaned up).
    # Do NOT use `hook-succeeded` here — keep failed Job objects so you can inspect logs.
    "helm.sh/hook-delete-policy": before-hook-creation
spec:
  # Note 4: backoffLimit: 0 means the Job will NOT retry a failed pod.
  # Retrying a partially-applied migration is dangerous unless your migration
  # tool is strictly idempotent. If you are certain your tool handles retries
  # (Flyway, Liquibase, EF Core, Alembic all do), increase this to 2 or 3.
  backoffLimit: 0                     # <-- CHANGE THIS: 0 for non-idempotent tools, 2-3 for idempotent tools

  # Note 5: The Helm upgrade will wait for this Job to finish. If the Job does
  # not finish (success or failure) within activeDeadlineSeconds, Kubernetes
  # marks it Failed, which causes Helm to mark the release FAILED and roll back.
  # Set this to (expected_migration_seconds × 3) to allow for transient DB slowness.
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 1800         # <-- CHANGE THIS: 30-minute default; adjust for your slowest migration

  # Note 6: Completed Job objects are cleaned up automatically after 1 hour.
  # Failed Jobs are NOT covered by ttlSecondsAfterFinished — you need to clean them
  # manually (or inspect them for debugging). This is intentional: failed migrations
  # should be visible for post-mortem, not silently garbage-collected.
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 3600

  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: "{{ .Release.Name }}"
        component: db-migrate
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never           # Note 7: Never — if the container exits non-zero, the Job fails
                                     # (backoffLimit controls re-creation of pods, not container restarts)

      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 1000
        fsGroup: 2000

      serviceAccountName: "{{ .Values.serviceAccount.name | default \"default\" }}"  # <-- CHANGE THIS

      initContainers:
        # Note 8: Even in the hook Job, we wait for the DB to be reachable.
        # During a cluster node drain or DB failover, the migration may be
        # triggered while the DB is temporarily unavailable.
        - name: wait-for-db
          image: busybox:1.36
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
            runAsUser: 65534
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          command:
            - sh
            - -c
            - |
              set -eu
              echo "[wait-for-db] Waiting for ${DB_HOST}:${DB_PORT}..."
              until nc -z "${DB_HOST}" "${DB_PORT}"; do
                echo "[wait-for-db]   not ready — retrying in 3s"
                sleep 3
              done
              echo "[wait-for-db] Database is up. Starting migration."
          env:
            - name: DB_HOST
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: "{{ .Values.db.secretName | default \"app-db-credentials\" }}"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
                  key: DB_HOST
            - name: DB_PORT
              value: "{{ .Values.db.port | default \"5432\" }}"                          # <-- CHANGE THIS
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "16Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "50m"
              memory: "32Mi"

      containers:
        - name: db-migrate
          # Note 9: Use the same image as the main application container to guarantee
          # that the migration scripts embedded in the image match the code that will
          # be deployed. The tag comes from .Values.image.tag — the same value used
          # by the Deployment template — so they always advance together.
          image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          imagePullPolicy: "{{ .Values.image.pullPolicy | default \"IfNotPresent\" }}"
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
            runAsUser: 1000
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true   # <-- Set false if migration tool writes temp files

          # Note 10: Pick ONE command block. Delete the others.
          # All listed tools are idempotent — running twice is safe.

          # EF Core (.NET)
          command: ["dotnet", "ef", "database", "update", "--no-build"]

          # Flyway
          # command: ["flyway", "migrate"]

          # Liquibase
          # command: ["liquibase", "--changelog-file=db/changelog.xml", "update"]

          # Alembic (Python)
          # command: ["alembic", "upgrade", "head"]

          # golang-migrate
          # command: ["migrate", "-path", "/migrations", "-database", "$(DATABASE_URL)", "up"]

          # Prisma (Node.js)
          # command: ["npx", "prisma", "migrate", "deploy"]

          env:
            - name: DB_HOST
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: "{{ .Values.db.secretName | default \"app-db-credentials\" }}"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
                  key: DB_HOST
            - name: DB_PORT
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: "{{ .Values.db.secretName | default \"app-db-credentials\" }}"
                  key: DB_PORT
            - name: DB_NAME
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: "{{ .Values.db.secretName | default \"app-db-credentials\" }}"
                  key: DB_NAME
            - name: DB_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: "{{ .Values.db.secretName | default \"app-db-credentials\" }}"
                  key: DB_USER
            - name: DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: "{{ .Values.db.secretName | default \"app-db-credentials\" }}"
                  key: DB_PASSWORD
            - name: ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection
              value: "Host=$(DB_HOST);Port=$(DB_PORT);Database=$(DB_NAME);Username=$(DB_USER);Password=$(DB_PASSWORD)"

          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              memory: "512Mi"           # <-- CHANGE THIS: Liquibase/Flyway may need more

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Database Migration via Init Container
# WHEN TO USE: Short migrations (< 5 minutes) where you want migrations
#   to run atomically with each pod start — no separate CI step needed.
#   The pod will not start accepting traffic until migrations succeed.
#
# DECISION GUIDE:
#   - Migration < 5 min AND app cannot start with old schema → use this file
#   - Migration > 5 min OR migration needs to run once across the cluster → use db-migration-job.yaml
#   - Migrating via Helm upgrade → use db-migration-hook.yaml
#
# TRADEOFF — IMPORTANT:
#   Init containers run every time a pod starts (including restarts and
#   scale-out). Your migration tool MUST be idempotent (Flyway, Liquibase,
#   Alembic, EF Core, golang-migrate all handle this natively by tracking
#   applied versions in a schema_versions table). If your migration is NOT
#   idempotent, use the Job pattern instead.
#
# ORDERING GUARANTEE:
#   Kubernetes runs init containers strictly in order. All init containers
#   must reach Completed status before the main container starts.
#   The sequence here is:
#     1. wait-for-db      → confirms DB port is open
#     2. db-migrate       → applies pending migrations
#     3. <main container> → starts only when both above succeed
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/_patterns/db-migration-job.yaml (long-running migrations)
#                cd/kubernetes/_patterns/db-migration-hook.yaml  (Helm pre-upgrade hook)
#                cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml             (base deployment pattern)
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app                        # <-- CHANGE THIS
  namespace: default                  # <-- CHANGE THIS
  labels:
    app: my-app                       # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app                     # <-- CHANGE THIS
  # Note 1: RollingUpdate ensures pods with the new schema are brought up one at
  # a time. Because migrations ran in the init container, the new pod already has
  # the schema the new app version expects — no partial upgrade window.
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 0   # Never take a pod down before the new one is healthy
      maxSurge: 1

  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app                   # <-- CHANGE THIS
    spec:
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 1000
        fsGroup: 2000

      initContainers:

        # ── Init 1: wait-for-db ─────────────────────────────────────────
        # Note 2: This init container spins until the database TCP port is
        # reachable. Without it, the migration container may fail immediately
        # if the DB pod restarts (node drain, upgrade, failover) while this
        # Deployment is being rolled out.
        # It uses busybox `nc` (netcat) — no extra tools or credentials needed.
        - name: wait-for-db
          image: busybox:1.36
          # Note 3: Run as non-root even for a minimal init container — defence in depth.
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
            runAsUser: 65534         # nobody
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          command:
            - sh
            - -c
            - |
              set -eu
              echo "[wait-for-db] Waiting for ${DB_HOST}:${DB_PORT}..."
              until nc -z "${DB_HOST}" "${DB_PORT}"; do
                echo "[wait-for-db]   not ready — retrying in 3s"
                sleep 3
              done
              echo "[wait-for-db] Database is reachable. Proceeding."
          env:
            - name: DB_HOST
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials  # <-- CHANGE THIS
                  key: DB_HOST
            - name: DB_PORT
              value: "5432"           # <-- CHANGE THIS: 3306=MySQL, 1433=MSSQL, 5984=CouchDB
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "16Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "50m"
              memory: "32Mi"

        # ── Init 2: db-migrate ──────────────────────────────────────────
        # Note 4: This init container uses the same image as the main application.
        # That ensures the migration scripts bundled inside the image exactly match
        # the code that will run — no version skew between migration files and app code.
        #
        # activeDeadlineSeconds on the Pod spec (below) acts as a hard timeout for
        # ALL init containers combined. Set it to a value slightly longer than your
        # longest expected migration. This prevents a stuck migration from blocking
        # the rollout indefinitely and holding a PodDisruptionBudget slot.
        - name: db-migrate
          image: my-registry/my-app:latest   # <-- CHANGE THIS: same tag as main container
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
            runAsUser: 1000
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true      # <-- Set false if your migration tool writes to /tmp
          # Note 5: Pick ONE command block that matches your migration tool.
          # All of these tools are idempotent: running them twice leaves the schema unchanged.

          # EF Core (.NET) — requires `dotnet ef` in the image
          command: ["dotnet", "ef", "database", "update", "--no-build"]

          # Flyway — requires `flyway` in the image
          # command: ["flyway", "migrate"]

          # Liquibase — requires `liquibase` in the image
          # command: ["liquibase", "--changelog-file=db/changelog.xml", "update"]

          # Alembic (Python) — requires `alembic` in the image
          # command: ["alembic", "upgrade", "head"]

          # golang-migrate — requires `migrate` binary in the image
          # command: ["migrate", "-path", "/migrations", "-database", "$(DATABASE_URL)", "up"]

          # Prisma (Node.js) — requires `npx` in the image
          # command: ["npx", "prisma", "migrate", "deploy"]

          env:
            - name: DB_HOST
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials  # <-- CHANGE THIS
                  key: DB_HOST
            - name: DB_PORT
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_PORT
            - name: DB_NAME
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_NAME
            - name: DB_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_USER
            - name: DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_PASSWORD
            # Note 6: Connection string assembled from individual keys avoids
            # storing a composed string in Secrets (which must be rotated atomically).
            # Adjust the format for your driver:
            #   EF Core / Npgsql:  Host=..;Database=..;Username=..;Password=..
            #   JDBC:              jdbc:postgresql://$DB_HOST:$DB_PORT/$DB_NAME
            #   SQLAlchemy:        postgresql+psycopg2://$DB_USER:$DB_PASSWORD@$DB_HOST/$DB_NAME
            - name: ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection
              value: "Host=$(DB_HOST);Port=$(DB_PORT);Database=$(DB_NAME);Username=$(DB_USER);Password=$(DB_PASSWORD)"
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              memory: "512Mi"          # <-- CHANGE THIS: Liquibase needs 512Mi-1Gi; Alembic needs ~128Mi

      # Note 7: activeDeadlineSeconds is the wall-clock limit for ALL init containers
      # combined. If init containers are still running after this many seconds,
      # Kubernetes kills the pod and marks it Failed.
      # Set this to (expected_migration_duration_seconds × 2) as a safety margin.
      # If your migration routinely takes > 5 minutes, switch to db-migration-job.yaml.
      activeDeadlineSeconds: 300        # <-- CHANGE THIS: 5-minute hard cap

      containers:
        - name: app
          image: my-registry/my-app:latest  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          env:
            - name: DB_HOST
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials  # <-- CHANGE THIS
                  key: DB_HOST
            - name: DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_PASSWORD
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "1000m"
              memory: "512Mi"
          securityContext:
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz       # <-- CHANGE THIS
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz       # <-- CHANGE THIS
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 15

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Database Migration Job (Kubernetes)
# WHEN TO USE: Run schema migrations safely before a new app version
#              is deployed.  Works with any migration tool that runs
#              as a CLI command (Flyway, Liquibase, EF Core, Alembic,
#              golang-migrate, Prisma, Rails, etc.).
#
# STRATEGY:
#   1. CI pipeline builds the app image (includes migration tooling).
#   2. This Job runs first — it is idempotent and uses advisory locks
#      so only one pod wins even during parallel deploys.
#   3. The Deployment rollout begins only after the Job succeeds.
#   4. On failure the Job is retried backoffLimit times, then the
#      pipeline fails and the Deployment is never updated.
#
# ROLLBACK STRATEGY:
#   - Always write backward-compatible migrations (expand/contract pattern).
#   - Use a separate "rollback Job" (see bottom of file) rather than
#     kubectl rollout undo — that only rolls back the app, not the schema.
#   - Keep N-1 migration rollback scripts tested in CI.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/_patterns/init-containers.yaml (lighter pattern)
#                cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

---
# ── 1. Migration Job ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: db-migrate-{{ .Release.Name }}-{{ .Values.image.tag | replace "." "-" }}  # <-- CHANGE THIS: unique per release
  namespace: default                        # <-- CHANGE THIS
  labels:
    app: my-app                             # <-- CHANGE THIS
    component: db-migrate
    version: "{{ .Values.image.tag }}"      # <-- CHANGE THIS: use your image tag variable
  annotations:
    # Helm: run this Job before the Deployment upgrade
    "helm.sh/hook": pre-upgrade,pre-install
    "helm.sh/hook-weight": "-5"
    "helm.sh/hook-delete-policy": before-hook-creation,hook-succeeded
spec:
  # Retry up to 3 times before marking the Job Failed.
  # Each attempt runs a fresh Pod — connection drops and transient DB
  # errors are retried without re-running succeeded migrations (idempotent).
  backoffLimit: 3

  # Hard cap: if migrations take > 10 minutes something is wrong.
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 600              # <-- CHANGE THIS

  # Keep the last 3 succeeded and 1 failed Job for post-mortem.
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1

  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
        component: db-migrate
    spec:
      restartPolicy: OnFailure

      # Run as non-root
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 1000
        fsGroup: 2000

      # Give the migration pod an RBAC-scoped service account with no
      # extra Kubernetes permissions — it only needs DB access.
      serviceAccountName: db-migrate-sa   # <-- CHANGE THIS or remove if using default

      initContainers:
        # Wait until the DB is accepting connections before attempting migration.
        # This prevents spurious Job failures during DB startup / failover.
        - name: wait-for-db
          image: busybox:1.36
          command:
            - sh
            - -c
            - |
              echo "Waiting for database at $DB_HOST:$DB_PORT..."
              until nc -z "$DB_HOST" "$DB_PORT"; do
                echo "  not ready — sleeping 3s"
                sleep 3
              done
              echo "Database is up"
          env:
            - name: DB_HOST
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials  # <-- CHANGE THIS
                  key: DB_HOST
            - name: DB_PORT
              value: "5432"               # <-- CHANGE THIS: 3306 for MySQL

      containers:
        - name: migrate
          image: my-registry/my-app:latest  # <-- CHANGE THIS: same image as deployment
          imagePullPolicy: Always

          # ── Pick ONE of the command examples below ──────────────────────

          # Flyway
          # command: ["flyway", "migrate"]

          # Liquibase
          # command: ["liquibase", "--changelog-file=db/changelog.xml", "update"]

          # EF Core (.NET)
          command: ["dotnet", "ef", "database", "update", "--no-build"]

          # Alembic (Python)
          # command: ["alembic", "upgrade", "head"]

          # golang-migrate
          # command: ["migrate", "-path", "/migrations", "-database", "$(DATABASE_URL)", "up"]

          # Prisma
          # command: ["npx", "prisma", "migrate", "deploy"]

          # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

          env:
            - name: DB_HOST
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials  # <-- CHANGE THIS
                  key: DB_HOST
            - name: DB_NAME
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_NAME
            - name: DB_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_USER
            - name: DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_PASSWORD
            # Convenience connection string built from individual keys (EF Core / ADO.NET)
            - name: ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection
              value: "Host=$(DB_HOST);Database=$(DB_NAME);Username=$(DB_USER);Password=$(DB_PASSWORD)"

          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "100m"
            limits:
              memory: "512Mi"
              cpu: "500m"

          securityContext:
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
            capabilities:
              drop: ["ALL"]

          # Mount a writable tmp dir if the migration tool needs to write temp files
          volumeMounts:
            - name: tmp
              mountPath: /tmp

      volumes:
        - name: tmp
          emptyDir: {}

---
# ── 2. Service Account for the migration Job ─────────────────────────────
# Minimal permissions — the Job needs no Kubernetes API access.
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: db-migrate-sa
  namespace: default              # <-- CHANGE THIS
  labels:
    app: my-app
    component: db-migrate
  # For IRSA (EKS) or Workload Identity (AKS/GKE), annotate the SA here:
  # annotations:
  #   eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/db-migrate-role
  #   azure.workload.identity/client-id: <client-id>

---
# ── 3. Rollback Job ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Run this MANUALLY (kubectl apply) when a release must be reverted.
# Do NOT automate rollback — a human should decide when a schema rollback
# is safe because it may involve data loss.
#
# Usage:
#   kubectl apply -f db-migration-job.yaml --selector app=my-app,component=db-rollback
#   kubectl wait --for=condition=complete job/db-rollback-v1-2-0 --timeout=300s
#   kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app   # roll back app AFTER schema
#
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: db-rollback-v1-2-0          # <-- CHANGE THIS: match the target version
  namespace: default                 # <-- CHANGE THIS
  labels:
    app: my-app
    component: db-rollback
spec:
  backoffLimit: 1                    # Fail fast on rollback — don't keep retrying
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 300
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
        component: db-rollback
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never           # Never retry a rollback automatically
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 1000
      serviceAccountName: db-migrate-sa
      containers:
        - name: rollback
          image: my-registry/my-app:v1.2.0  # <-- CHANGE THIS: the PREVIOUS stable version
          imagePullPolicy: Always

          # ── Pick the rollback command matching your migration tool ────────

          # Flyway — roll back to version 5
          # command: ["flyway", "undo", "-target=5"]

          # Liquibase — roll back last 1 change
          # command: ["liquibase", "rollback-count", "1"]

          # EF Core — roll back to a specific migration name
          command: ["dotnet", "ef", "database", "update", "PreviousMigrationName", "--no-build"]

          # Alembic — roll back to a specific revision
          # command: ["alembic", "downgrade", "-1"]

          # golang-migrate — roll back 1 step
          # command: ["migrate", "-path", "/migrations", "-database", "$(DATABASE_URL)", "down", "1"]

          # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

          env:
            - name: DB_HOST
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_HOST
            - name: DB_NAME
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_NAME
            - name: DB_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_USER
            - name: DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-db-credentials
                  key: DB_PASSWORD

          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "100m"
            limits:
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "200m"

          securityContext:
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
            capabilities:
              drop: ["ALL"]

          volumeMounts:
            - name: tmp
              mountPath: /tmp

      volumes:
        - name: tmp
          emptyDir: {}

﻿# Init Containers Pattern
# Run setup tasks before the main container starts
# Note 1: This Kubernetes style field captures a contract; keeping schema keys stable improves portability across environments.
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  # Note 2: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
  name: app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        app: my-app
    spec:
      initContainers:
        # Wait for database to be ready
        # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        - name: wait-for-db
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ['sh', '-c', 'until nc -z db-service 5432; do echo waiting; sleep 2; done']

        # Run database migrations
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        - name: db-migrate
          image: my-registry/my-app:latest
          command: ['dotnet', 'ef', 'database', 'update']
          # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          env:
            - name: ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection
              valueFrom:
                # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: app-secrets
                  key: db-connection-string

      # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      containers:
        - name: app
          image: my-registry/my-app:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Secrets Store CSI Driver — SecretProviderClass
# WHEN TO USE: Mount secrets from a cloud vault directly into pods
#   as files (not Kubernetes Secret objects). Secrets are fetched
#   at pod start and kept in sync while the pod is running.
#
# HOW THIS DIFFERS FROM EXTERNAL SECRETS OPERATOR (ESO):
#   ESO (security/secret-rotation/external-secrets-operator.yaml)
#     — Syncs secrets into a Kubernetes Secret object
#     — Pods read secrets as env vars or volume mounts from K8s Secret
#     — Secret persists in etcd (encrypted at rest if KMS enabled)
#     — Decoupled: pod restart not required for secret rotation
#   CSI Driver (this file)
#     — Mounts secrets directly as files in the pod filesystem
#     — No Kubernetes Secret object created by default (optional sync)
#     — Secrets vanish when the pod is deleted (not stored in etcd)
#     — Pod restart required to pick up rotated secrets unless using
#       auto-rotation (CSI driver polls the vault periodically)
#
#   CHOOSE ESO when: you need env var injection or secret sharing across pods.
#   CHOOSE CSI when: compliance requires secrets never hit etcd, or you need
#                    file-based secrets (TLS certs, config files, keystores).
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   Install the CSI driver + cloud provider plugin:
#     # AWS provider
#     helm repo add secrets-store-csi-driver https://kubernetes-sigs.github.io/secrets-store-csi-driver/charts
#     helm install csi-secrets-store secrets-store-csi-driver/secrets-store-csi-driver \
#       --namespace kube-system --set syncSecret.enabled=true
#     kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aws/secrets-store-csi-driver-provider-aws/main/deployment/aws-provider-installer.yaml
#
#     # Azure provider
#     helm repo add csi-secrets-store-provider-azure https://azure.github.io/secrets-store-csi-driver-provider-azure/charts
#     helm install csi-azure csi-secrets-store-provider-azure/csi-secrets-store-provider-azure \
#       --namespace kube-system
#
#     # GCP provider
#     kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/secrets-store-csi-driver-provider-gcp/main/deploy/provider-gcp-plugin.yaml
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES:
#   security/secret-rotation/external-secrets-operator.yaml (ESO alternative)
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml  (add the CSI volume + volumeMount)
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# AWS — Secrets Manager + Parameter Store
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Note 1: The AWS provider authenticates via IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts).
# The pod's ServiceAccount must have an IAM role annotation with permission to
# call secretsmanager:GetSecretValue and/or ssm:GetParameter.
# Create the IRSA role with: eksctl create iamserviceaccount (or Terraform)
---
apiVersion: secrets-store.csi.x-k8s.io/v1
kind: SecretProviderClass
metadata:
  name: aws-secrets              # <-- CHANGE THIS
  namespace: default             # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  provider: aws
  parameters:
    # Note 2: objects is a YAML string (not a nested object). Each entry maps
    # a secret/parameter to a file path that will appear inside the pod.
    objects: |
      - objectName: "my-app/prod/db-password"    # <-- CHANGE THIS: Secrets Manager secret name or ARN
        objectType: "secretsmanager"
        objectAlias: "db-password"               # <-- CHANGE THIS: filename in the mounted volume
      - objectName: "/my-app/prod/api-key"       # <-- CHANGE THIS: SSM Parameter Store path
        objectType: "ssmparameter"
        objectAlias: "api-key"
      # Note 3: To mount a specific version/stage of a secret:
      # - objectName: "my-app/prod/db-password"
      #   objectType: "secretsmanager"
      #   objectVersionId: "abc123"           # specific version
      #   objectVersionStage: "AWSPREVIOUS"   # or stage label

  # Note 4: secretObjects syncs the CSI-mounted secret into a Kubernetes Secret.
  # This is OPTIONAL. Enable it only if pods need the secret as an env var
  # rather than (or in addition to) a file mount.
  # Requires: helm install ... --set syncSecret.enabled=true
  # secretObjects:
  #   - secretName: app-db-secret         # <-- CHANGE THIS: name of the K8s Secret to create
  #     type: Opaque
  #     data:
  #       - objectName: "db-password"     # must match objectAlias above
  #         key: DB_PASSWORD              # <-- CHANGE THIS: key in the K8s Secret


# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Azure — Key Vault
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Note 5: The Azure provider supports three authentication methods:
#   a) Workload Identity (RECOMMENDED for AKS) — annotate the pod ServiceAccount
#      with azure.workload.identity/client-id and the AKS cluster has OIDC issuer enabled.
#   b) Managed Identity (MSI) — uses the node's managed identity (less granular).
#   c) Service Principal — clientID/clientSecret stored in a K8s Secret (least preferred).
---
apiVersion: secrets-store.csi.x-k8s.io/v1
kind: SecretProviderClass
metadata:
  name: azure-secrets            # <-- CHANGE THIS
  namespace: default             # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  provider: azure
  parameters:
    usePodIdentity: "false"
    # Note 6: clientID for Workload Identity — matches the annotation on the ServiceAccount.
    # Get it from: az identity show --name my-identity --resource-group my-rg --query clientId
    clientID: "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: managed identity client ID
    keyvaultName: "my-keyvault"                         # <-- CHANGE THIS: Key Vault name (not URL)
    # Note 7: cloudName can be AzurePublicCloud, AzureChinaCloud, AzureUSGovernmentCloud
    cloudName: "AzurePublicCloud"
    tenantId: "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: Azure tenant ID
    objects: |
      array:
        - |
          objectName: db-password         # <-- CHANGE THIS: Key Vault secret name
          objectType: secret              # secret | key | cert
          objectAlias: db-password        # <-- CHANGE THIS: filename in the volume
          objectVersion: ""              # empty = latest version
        - |
          objectName: tls-cert            # <-- CHANGE THIS
          objectType: cert
          objectAlias: tls.crt
          objectVersion: ""


# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# GCP — Secret Manager
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Note 8: GCP provider authenticates via Workload Identity Federation.
# Annotate the Kubernetes ServiceAccount to bind it to a GCP Service Account:
#   kubectl annotate serviceaccount my-sa \
#     iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account=my-sa@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com
# The GCP SA needs: roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor
---
apiVersion: secrets-store.csi.x-k8s.io/v1
kind: SecretProviderClass
metadata:
  name: gcp-secrets              # <-- CHANGE THIS
  namespace: default             # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  provider: gcp
  parameters:
    secrets: |
      - resourceName: "projects/my-project/secrets/db-password/versions/latest"   # <-- CHANGE THIS
        fileName: "db-password"    # <-- CHANGE THIS: filename in the volume
      - resourceName: "projects/my-project/secrets/api-key/versions/latest"       # <-- CHANGE THIS
        fileName: "api-key"

# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# DEPLOYMENT SNIPPET — how to mount SecretProviderClass into a pod
# Copy these stanzas into your Deployment (deployment.yaml)
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Note 9: Paste these into the relevant sections of your Deployment spec.
# Replace "aws-secrets" with the SecretProviderClass name from above.
#
# spec:
#   serviceAccountName: my-app-sa   # Must have IRSA/WI/MSI permissions  # <-- CHANGE THIS
#   containers:
#     - name: app
#       image: my-registry/my-app:v1.0.0
#       volumeMounts:
#         - name: secrets-store-vol
#           mountPath: "/mnt/secrets"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: path where secrets appear as files
#           readOnly: true
#       # Note 10: Read secrets from files rather than env vars for security.
#       # Avoid valueFrom.secretKeyRef pointing to K8s Secrets when using CSI.
#       # If you need env vars, enable syncSecret in the SecretProviderClass above
#       # and use valueFrom.secretKeyRef referencing the synced K8s Secret.
#   volumes:
#     - name: secrets-store-vol
#       csi:
#         driver: secrets-store.csi.k8s.io
#         readOnly: true
#         volumeAttributes:
#           secretProviderClass: aws-secrets   # <-- CHANGE THIS: SecretProviderClass name

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Velero — Cluster Backup & Disaster Recovery
# WHEN TO USE: Take scheduled backups of Kubernetes workloads
#              (namespaces, PVs, secrets, configmaps) and restore
#              them in a different cluster or after accidental deletion.
#
# INSTALL VELERO (once per cluster):
#
#   # AWS (S3 + EBS snapshots via EBS CSI driver)
#   velero install \
#     --provider aws \
#     --plugins velero/velero-plugin-for-aws:v1.9.0 \
#     --bucket my-velero-backups \                   # <-- CHANGE THIS
#     --backup-location-config region=us-east-1 \   # <-- CHANGE THIS
#     --snapshot-location-config region=us-east-1 \ # <-- CHANGE THIS
#     --secret-file ./credentials-velero \           # IAM key or leave empty for IRSA
#     --use-node-agent \                             # required for PV backups via restic/kopia
#     --default-volumes-to-fs-backup                # backup all PVs as filesystem snapshots
#
#   # Azure (Blob Storage + Azure Disk snapshots)
#   velero install \
#     --provider azure \
#     --plugins velero/velero-plugin-for-microsoft-azure:v1.9.0 \
#     --bucket velero \
#     --secret-file ./credentials-velero \
#     --backup-location-config resourceGroup=velero-rg,storageAccount=velerobackups \
#     --snapshot-location-config apiTimeout=5m,resourceGroup=velero-rg \
#     --use-node-agent
#
#   # GCP (GCS + GCE Persistent Disk snapshots)
#   velero install \
#     --provider gcp \
#     --plugins velero/velero-plugin-for-gcp:v1.9.0 \
#     --bucket my-velero-gcs-bucket \
#     --secret-file ./credentials-velero \
#     --use-node-agent
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: terraform/aws-eks/backup.tf
#                terraform/azure-aks/backup.tf
#                terraform/gcp-gke/backup.tf
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

---
# ── 1. BackupStorageLocation — where backups are persisted ───────────────
apiVersion: velero.io/v1
kind: BackupStorageLocation
metadata:
  name: default
  namespace: velero
spec:
  provider: aws                       # <-- CHANGE THIS: azure | gcp
  objectStorage:
    bucket: my-velero-backups         # <-- CHANGE THIS
    prefix: cluster-name/             # <-- CHANGE THIS: separate clusters share a bucket
  config:
    region: us-east-1                 # <-- CHANGE THIS
    kmsKeyId: "arn:aws:kms:..."       # <-- CHANGE THIS: optional — encrypt backups at rest
    # For Azure: resourceGroup, storageAccount
    # For GCP: no extra config needed (project is inferred from SA)

---
# ── 2. VolumeSnapshotLocation — where PV snapshots are stored ────────────
apiVersion: velero.io/v1
kind: VolumeSnapshotLocation
metadata:
  name: default
  namespace: velero
spec:
  provider: aws                       # <-- CHANGE THIS: azure | gcp
  config:
    region: us-east-1                 # <-- CHANGE THIS

---
# ── 3. Daily backup — all production namespaces ───────────────────────────
# Backs up everything in the namespaces list, retains 14 copies.
# PV snapshots are included automatically when snapshotVolumes: true.
#
apiVersion: velero.io/v1
kind: Schedule
metadata:
  name: daily-prod-backup
  namespace: velero
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"              # 02:00 UTC daily  # <-- CHANGE THIS
  useOwnerReferencesInBackup: false
  template:
    includedNamespaces:
      - default                       # <-- CHANGE THIS: list your production namespaces
      - monitoring
      - ingress-nginx
      - cert-manager
    # Exclude namespaces that don't need backup
    excludedNamespaces:
      - kube-system
      - kube-public
      - velero
    snapshotVolumes: true
    volumeSnapshotLocations:
      - default
    storageLocation: default
    ttl: 336h                         # 14 days retention  # <-- CHANGE THIS
    # Include cluster-scoped resources needed for full restore
    includeClusterResources: true
    defaultVolumesToFsBackup: false   # use CSI snapshots; set true for restic/kopia fallback
    hooks:
      resources: []                   # add pre/post hooks here if apps need quiescing

---
# ── 4. Weekly full-cluster backup ─────────────────────────────────────────
# A "belt-and-suspenders" weekly backup that captures everything,
# retained for 90 days for compliance.
#
apiVersion: velero.io/v1
kind: Schedule
metadata:
  name: weekly-full-backup
  namespace: velero
spec:
  schedule: "0 3 * * 0"              # 03:00 UTC every Sunday  # <-- CHANGE THIS
  template:
    includedNamespaces:
      - "*"
    excludedNamespaces:
      - kube-system
      - kube-public
      - velero
    snapshotVolumes: true
    volumeSnapshotLocations:
      - default
    storageLocation: default
    ttl: 2160h                        # 90 days retention  # <-- CHANGE THIS
    includeClusterResources: true
    defaultVolumesToFsBackup: false

---
# ── 5. Pre-deploy backup (on-demand) ─────────────────────────────────────
# Trigger this from your CI pipeline before a major release so you have
# a clean restore point.
#
# Usage:
#   velero backup create pre-release-v2-0-0 \
#     --from-schedule daily-prod-backup \
#     --wait
#
# Or apply this manifest and delete it afterwards:
#
apiVersion: velero.io/v1
kind: Backup
metadata:
  name: pre-release-placeholder       # <-- CHANGE THIS: pre-release-v2-0-0
  namespace: velero
  labels:
    trigger: manual
spec:
  includedNamespaces:
    - default                         # <-- CHANGE THIS
  snapshotVolumes: true
  volumeSnapshotLocations:
    - default
  storageLocation: default
  ttl: 720h                           # 30 days
  includeClusterResources: true

---
# ── 6. Restore (use during DR — manual) ──────────────────────────────────
# Step-by-step restore process (do not automate — human decision required):
#
#   1. Install Velero on the target cluster with the same BackupStorageLocation.
#   2. List available backups:
#        velero backup get
#   3. Describe a backup before restoring:
#        velero backup describe <backup-name> --details
#   4. Apply the Restore manifest below (or use velero restore create).
#   5. Monitor progress:
#        velero restore describe <restore-name> --details
#        kubectl get events -n velero
#
# apiVersion: velero.io/v1
# kind: Restore
# metadata:
#   name: restore-prod-2024-01-15       # <-- CHANGE THIS
#   namespace: velero
# spec:
#   backupName: daily-prod-backup-20240115020000  # <-- CHANGE THIS: from velero backup get
#   includedNamespaces:
#     - default                         # <-- CHANGE THIS: or "*" for full restore
#   # Restore PV data from snapshots
#   restorePVs: true
#   # Preserve original node ports during restore
#   preserveNodePorts: true
#   # Remap namespaces if restoring into a different environment
#   # namespaceMapping:
#   #   prod: prod-restored

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: ClusterIssuer — Let's Encrypt Production
# WHEN TO USE: Use this issuer for real workloads once the staging
#   issuer is working correctly. Production certificates are trusted
#   by all major browsers and operating systems.
#
# RATE LIMITS (production):
#   - 50 certificates per registered domain per week
#   - 5 duplicate certificates per week
#   - 5 failed validations per account per hour
#   See: https://letsencrypt.org/docs/rate-limits/
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - Validated the flow with letsencrypt-staging first
#   - Same ingress prerequisites as staging issuer
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/cert-manager/cluster-issuer-staging.yaml
#                cd/kubernetes/cert-manager/README.md
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: ClusterIssuer
metadata:
  name: letsencrypt-prod
spec:
  acme:
    server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory

    email: devops@example.com   # <-- CHANGE THIS

    privateKeySecretRef:
      name: letsencrypt-prod-account-key

    solvers:
      - http01:
          ingress:
            class: nginx          # <-- CHANGE THIS: your IngressClass name

      # Note 1: DNS-01 challenge is required for wildcard certificates
      # (e.g., *.example.com) or when your ingress is not publicly reachable.
      # Uncomment and configure one of the DNS provider blocks below.
      # You cannot use HTTP-01 for wildcard certificates.

      # DNS-01 via AWS Route 53:
      # - dns01:
      #     route53:
      #       region: us-east-1                         # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #       hostedZoneID: Z1234567890EXAMPLE          # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #       accessKeyIDSecretRef:
      #         name: route53-credentials
      #         key: access-key-id
      #       secretAccessKeySecretRef:
      #         name: route53-credentials
      #         key: secret-access-key

      # DNS-01 via Azure DNS:
      # - dns01:
      #     azureDNS:
      #       subscriptionID: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000  # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #       resourceGroupName: my-dns-rg                          # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #       hostedZoneName: example.com                           # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #       environment: AzurePublicCloud
      #       managedIdentity:
      #         clientID: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000      # <-- CHANGE THIS

      # DNS-01 via Google Cloud DNS:
      # - dns01:
      #     cloudDNS:
      #       project: my-gcp-project                               # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #       serviceAccountSecretRef:
      #         name: clouddns-credentials
      #         key: service-account.json

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: ClusterIssuer — Self-Signed (local dev / internal services)
# WHEN TO USE: Local development clusters (kind, minikube), internal
#   services that only need TLS-in-transit without browser trust, or
#   as a CA bootstrapper (issue a self-signed CA cert then reference
#   it as a CA issuer for all internal services).
#
# NOT FOR PRODUCTION: Self-signed certificates are not trusted by
#   browsers. Use letsencrypt-prod for any internet-facing service.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/cert-manager/cluster-issuer-staging.yaml
#                local-dev/kind/
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: The selfsigned issuer simply signs certificate requests with
# the key that cert-manager generates for the Certificate resource itself.
# There is no external ACME server or CA involved.
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: ClusterIssuer
metadata:
  name: selfsigned
spec:
  selfSigned: {}

---
# Note 2: A common pattern for local dev is to create a self-signed CA cert
# first (using the selfsigned issuer above), then create a separate CA issuer
# that references that cert. All app certificates are then signed by this local
# CA, and you only need to trust one root cert in your browser/OS.

# Step 1: Self-signed root CA certificate
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Certificate
metadata:
  name: local-root-ca
  namespace: cert-manager
spec:
  isCA: true
  commonName: local-root-ca
  secretName: local-root-ca-tls
  privateKey:
    algorithm: ECDSA
    size: 256
  issuerRef:
    name: selfsigned
    kind: ClusterIssuer

---
# Step 2: A CA issuer that uses the self-signed root CA above
# Reference this issuer in app Certificate resources for local dev.
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: ClusterIssuer
metadata:
  name: local-ca                      # <-- CHANGE THIS: rename to match your dev workflow
spec:
  ca:
    secretName: local-root-ca-tls     # Must match the Certificate.spec.secretName above

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: ClusterIssuer — Let's Encrypt Staging
# WHEN TO USE: Use this issuer during initial setup and testing.
#   Let's Encrypt staging has much higher rate limits and issues
#   certificates trusted only by its staging root CA (not browsers).
#   Validate the full flow here before switching to the production issuer.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   1. cert-manager installed:
#        helm repo add jetstack https://charts.jetstack.io
#        helm install cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager \
#          --namespace cert-manager \
#          --create-namespace \
#          --set crds.enabled=true \
#          --version v1.14.0        # <-- CHANGE THIS: pin to a version
#   2. Ingress controller with a publicly reachable IP/hostname
#      (required for HTTP-01 challenge)
#   3. DNS A/CNAME record pointing to the ingress IP
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/cert-manager/cluster-issuer-prod.yaml
#                cd/kubernetes/cert-manager/README.md
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: ClusterIssuer
metadata:
  name: letsencrypt-staging
spec:
  acme:
    # Note 1: The staging ACME endpoint issues certificates signed by
    # "Fake LE Root X1". These are NOT trusted by browsers but let you
    # verify the full certificate issuance flow without hitting production
    # rate limits (50 certificates/domain/week for prod vs. 30k for staging).
    server: https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory

    email: devops@example.com   # <-- CHANGE THIS: Let's Encrypt will send expiry warnings here

    # Note 2: cert-manager stores the ACME account private key in a Kubernetes
    # Secret. This is NOT your TLS certificate — it authenticates your account
    # with Let's Encrypt. Keep it backed up.
    privateKeySecretRef:
      name: letsencrypt-staging-account-key   # Must be unique per issuer

    solvers:
      # Note 3: HTTP-01 challenge tells Let's Encrypt to verify domain ownership
      # by placing a token at http://<domain>/.well-known/acme-challenge/<token>.
      # Your ingress must allow port 80 traffic (even if you redirect to 443).
      - http01:
          ingress:
            class: nginx                      # <-- CHANGE THIS: your IngressClass name (nginx, traefik, haproxy, etc.)

apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

# Note 1: Apply namespace.yaml FIRST (it is listed first here).
# Kustomize does not guarantee order by default, but listing the namespace
# before the issuers avoids race conditions on first apply.
resources:
  - namespace.yaml
  - cluster-issuer-selfsigned.yaml    # Local dev / internal CA — no external dependencies
  - cluster-issuer-staging.yaml       # Let's Encrypt staging — validate before using prod
  - cluster-issuer-prod.yaml          # Let's Encrypt production — internet-facing services

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: cert-manager Namespace
# WHEN TO USE: Always apply this first — it creates the cert-manager
#   namespace before Helm installs the controller into it.
#   Keep this in source control to ensure the namespace label
#   (disable-validation) is always present.
#
# PREREQUISITES: kubectl
# RELATED FILES: cd/kubernetes/cert-manager/kustomization.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: cert-manager
  labels:
    # Note 1: This label tells cert-manager's own webhook to skip validation
    # of resources in this namespace, preventing a chicken-and-egg problem
    # where the webhook itself cannot start because certs don't exist yet.
    cert-manager.io/disable-validation: "true"

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Pulumi Preview / Deploy
# WHEN TO USE: Automating Pulumi infrastructure deployments with PR review
# PREREQUISITES: Pulumi project with Pulumi.yaml, cloud credentials via OIDC
# SECRETS NEEDED: PULUMI_ACCESS_TOKEN, cloud auth (see sections below)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: cd/pulumi/aws/, cd/pulumi/azure/, cd/pulumi/gcp/
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Pulumi Preview / Deploy

# Note 2: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
on:
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    branches: [main]
    # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    paths:
      # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - "cd/pulumi/**"                         # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to your Pulumi project
  # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    branches: [main]
    # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    paths:
      # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - "cd/pulumi/**"                         # <-- CHANGE THIS: match above

# Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
concurrency:
  # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  group: pulumi-${{ github.ref }}
  # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  cancel-in-progress: false                    # Never cancel in-progress deploys

# Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
permissions:
  # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  contents: read
  # Note 16: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull-requests: write
  # Note 17: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  id-token: write                              # For OIDC authentication

# Note 18: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
env:
  # Note 19: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  PULUMI_WORKING_DIR: "cd/pulumi/aws"          # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to Pulumi project
  # Note 20: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  PULUMI_STACK: "prod"                         # <-- CHANGE THIS: your stack name
  # Note 21: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  NODE_VERSION: "22"                           # <-- CHANGE THIS: your Node.js version

  # --- AWS (uncomment if using AWS) ---
  # AWS_REGION: us-east-1

  # --- Azure (uncomment if using Azure) ---
  # ARM_USE_OIDC: true

  # --- GCP (uncomment if using GCP) ---
  # GOOGLE_PROJECT: my-project-id

# Note 22: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  # =============================================
  # PREVIEW — runs on every Pull Request
  # =============================================
  # Note 23: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  preview:
    # Note 24: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Pulumi Preview
    # Note 25: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Note 26: Control flow should stay readable; predictable branches reduce defects and simplify troubleshooting.
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'

    # Note 27: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      # Note 28: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # --- Cloud Authentication (uncomment ONE) ---

      # AWS OIDC
      # - name: AWS Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
      #   with:
      #     role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}
      #     aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}

      # Azure OIDC
      # - name: Azure Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: azure/login@v2
      #   with:
      #     client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
      #     tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
      #     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      # GCP Workload Identity
      # - name: GCP Auth (Workload Identity)
      #   uses: google-github-actions/auth@v2
      #   with:
      #     workload_identity_provider: ${{ secrets.GCP_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_PROVIDER }}
      #     service_account: ${{ secrets.GCP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT }}

      # Note 29: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Set up Node.js
        # Note 30: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        # Note 31: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          # Note 32: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          node-version: ${{ env.NODE_VERSION }}
          # Note 33: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          cache: npm
          # Note 34: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          cache-dependency-path: ${{ env.PULUMI_WORKING_DIR }}/package-lock.json

      # Note 35: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Install dependencies
        # Note 36: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        working-directory: ${{ env.PULUMI_WORKING_DIR }}
        # Note 37: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: npm ci

      # Note 38: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Pulumi Preview
        # Note 39: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: pulumi/actions@v5
        # Note 40: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          command: preview
          stack-name: ${{ env.PULUMI_STACK }}
          work-dir: ${{ env.PULUMI_WORKING_DIR }}
          comment-on-pr: true
          comment-on-summary: true
        env:
          PULUMI_ACCESS_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PULUMI_ACCESS_TOKEN }}

  # =============================================
  # DEPLOY — runs on push to main (after PR merge)
  # =============================================
  deploy:
    name: Pulumi Deploy
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'push'
    environment: production                     # <-- CHANGE THIS: requires GitHub environment approval

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # --- Cloud Authentication (uncomment ONE — same as preview) ---

      # AWS OIDC
      # - name: AWS Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
      #   with:
      #     role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}
      #     aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}

      # Azure OIDC
      # - name: Azure Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: azure/login@v2
      #   with:
      #     client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
      #     tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
      #     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      # GCP Workload Identity
      # - name: GCP Auth (Workload Identity)
      #   uses: google-github-actions/auth@v2
      #   with:
      #     workload_identity_provider: ${{ secrets.GCP_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_PROVIDER }}
      #     service_account: ${{ secrets.GCP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT }}

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ env.NODE_VERSION }}
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: ${{ env.PULUMI_WORKING_DIR }}/package-lock.json

      - name: Install dependencies
        working-directory: ${{ env.PULUMI_WORKING_DIR }}
        run: npm ci

      - name: Pulumi Deploy
        uses: pulumi/actions@v5
        with:
          command: up
          stack-name: ${{ env.PULUMI_STACK }}
          work-dir: ${{ env.PULUMI_WORKING_DIR }}
        env:
          PULUMI_ACCESS_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PULUMI_ACCESS_TOKEN }}

  gcp-gke:machineType: e2-medium
  gcp-gke:nodeCount: "3"

name: gcp-gke
description: GKE Autopilot cluster with Artifact Registry
