  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  paths:
    include:
      # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - terraform/**                # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to your Terraform configs

pr:
  # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  branches:
    include:
      # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - main
  paths:
    # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    include:
      - terraform/**                # <-- CHANGE THIS: match above

# Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
variables:
  TF_VERSION: "1.10.0"                             # <-- CHANGE THIS: pin to your version
  # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  TF_WORKING_DIR: "$(System.DefaultWorkingDirectory)/terraform/azure-aks"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
  azureServiceConnection: "my-azure-connection"     # <-- CHANGE THIS: Azure DevOps service connection name

  # Backend config for remote state (Azure Storage)
  # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  backendResourceGroup: "tfstate-rg"                # <-- CHANGE THIS
  backendStorageAccount: "tfstatesa"                # <-- CHANGE THIS
  # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  backendContainer: "tfstate"                       # <-- CHANGE THIS
  backendKey: "terraform.tfstate"                   # <-- CHANGE THIS

# Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
pool:
  vmImage: "ubuntu-latest"

# Note 13: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
stages:
  # =============================================
  # VALIDATE — format check + validation
  # =============================================
  - stage: Validate
    # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    displayName: "Terraform Validate"
    jobs:
      # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - job: Validate
        displayName: "Format & Validate"
        # Note 16: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        steps:
          - task: TerraformInstaller@1
            # Note 17: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            displayName: "Install Terraform $(TF_VERSION)"
            inputs:
              # Note 18: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              terraformVersion: $(TF_VERSION)

          - script: terraform fmt -check -diff
            # Note 19: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            displayName: "Terraform Format Check"
            workingDirectory: $(TF_WORKING_DIR)

          # Note 20: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          - script: terraform init -backend=false
            displayName: "Terraform Init (validation only)"
            # Note 21: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            workingDirectory: $(TF_WORKING_DIR)

          - script: terraform validate -no-color
            # Note 22: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            displayName: "Terraform Validate"
            workingDirectory: $(TF_WORKING_DIR)

  # =============================================
  # PLAN — runs on PR and main branch
  # =============================================
  # Note 23: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  - stage: Plan
    displayName: "Terraform Plan"
    # Note 24: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    dependsOn: Validate
    jobs:
      # Note 25: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - job: Plan
        displayName: "Generate Plan"
        # Note 26: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        steps:
          - task: TerraformInstaller@1
            # Note 27: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            displayName: "Install Terraform $(TF_VERSION)"
            inputs:
              # Note 28: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              terraformVersion: $(TF_VERSION)

          - task: AzureCLI@2
            # Note 29: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            displayName: "Terraform Init + Plan"
            inputs:
              # Note 30: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              azureSubscription: $(azureServiceConnection)
              scriptType: bash
              # Note 31: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              scriptLocation: inlineScript
              workingDirectory: $(TF_WORKING_DIR)
              # Note 32: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              addSpnToEnvironment: true  # Exposes ARM_CLIENT_ID, ARM_CLIENT_SECRET, etc.
              inlineScript: |
                # Export Azure credentials for Terraform
                # Note 33: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
                export ARM_CLIENT_ID=$servicePrincipalId
                export ARM_CLIENT_SECRET=$servicePrincipalKey
                # Note 34: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
                export ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)
                export ARM_TENANT_ID=$tenantId

                # Init with backend
                # Note 35: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
                terraform init -input=false \
                  -backend-config="resource_group_name=$(backendResourceGroup)" \
                  # Note 36: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
                  -backend-config="storage_account_name=$(backendStorageAccount)" \
                  -backend-config="container_name=$(backendContainer)" \
                  # Note 37: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
                  -backend-config="key=$(backendKey)"

                # Plan
                terraform plan -input=false -no-color -out=tfplan

                # Save human-readable plan for review
                # Note 38: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
                terraform show -no-color tfplan > $(Build.ArtifactStagingDirectory)/plan_output.txt

          - publish: $(Build.ArtifactStagingDirectory)/plan_output.txt
            # Note 39: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            artifact: terraform-plan
            displayName: "Publish Plan Output"

  # =============================================
  # APPLY — runs only on main branch, with approval
  # =============================================
  # Note 40: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  - stage: Apply
    displayName: "Terraform Apply"
    dependsOn: Plan
    condition: and(succeeded(), eq(variables['Build.SourceBranch'], 'refs/heads/main'))
    jobs:
      - deployment: Apply
        displayName: "Apply Infrastructure Changes"
        environment: production     # <-- CHANGE THIS: configure approval gates in Azure DevOps
        strategy:
          runOnce:
            deploy:
              steps:
                - checkout: self

                - task: TerraformInstaller@1
                  displayName: "Install Terraform $(TF_VERSION)"
                  inputs:
                    terraformVersion: $(TF_VERSION)

                - task: AzureCLI@2
                  displayName: "Terraform Init + Apply"
                  inputs:
                    azureSubscription: $(azureServiceConnection)
                    scriptType: bash
                    scriptLocation: inlineScript
                    workingDirectory: $(TF_WORKING_DIR)
                    addSpnToEnvironment: true
                    inlineScript: |
                      export ARM_CLIENT_ID=$servicePrincipalId
                      export ARM_CLIENT_SECRET=$servicePrincipalKey
                      export ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)
                      export ARM_TENANT_ID=$tenantId

                      terraform init -input=false \
                        -backend-config="resource_group_name=$(backendResourceGroup)" \
                        -backend-config="storage_account_name=$(backendStorageAccount)" \
                        -backend-config="container_name=$(backendContainer)" \
                        -backend-config="key=$(backendKey)"

                      terraform apply -input=false -auto-approve

# GitHub Actions — Environment Protection Rules

## What is a GitHub Environment?

A GitHub Environment is a named deployment target you configure in **Settings → Environments**.
Workflows reference it with `environment: <name>`, which makes the job pause and wait for the
protection rules to be satisfied before it runs.

The **key value**: your `secrets.PROD_DB_PASSWORD` in a job that references `environment: production`
is only available if all protection gates pass. Secrets are scoped to the environment, not the repo.

---

## How to configure (one-time, per repo)

1. Go to **Settings → Environments → New environment**
2. Name it to match exactly what workflows use (`production`, `staging`, etc.)
3. Configure protection rules:

| Rule | What it does | Recommended setting |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Required reviewers** | Pause until N people approve the deployment | 1–2 (prod), 0 (dev/staging) |
| **Wait timer** | Delay start N minutes after approval | 0–5 min (gives you time to cancel) |
| **Deployment branches** | Which branches can deploy to this env | `main` only for production |
| **Deployment tags** | Which tag patterns can deploy | `v*` for prod release tags |
| **Prevent self-review** | Reviewer cannot be the same person who triggered | Enable on production |

---

## Recommended environment matrix

```
┌─────────────┬──────────────┬───────────┬─────────────────────────┐
│ Environment │ Branch/Tag   │ Reviewers │ Notes                   │
├─────────────┼──────────────┼───────────┼─────────────────────────┤
│ development │ Any          │ 0         │ Auto-deploy on PR        │
│ staging     │ main         │ 0         │ Auto-deploy after merge  │
│ production  │ main / v*.*  │ 1–2       │ Manual approval required │
│ dr-failover │ main         │ 2         │ Ops team only            │
└─────────────┴──────────────┴───────────┴─────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Workflow template with environment gate

See the example below. The `environment:` key on a job is all that is needed in the YAML —
the protection rules are enforced server-side by GitHub.

```yaml
# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Staged deployment with environment gates
# WHEN TO USE: Any workflow that deploys to staging and production
# PREREQUISITES: Environments configured in Settings → Environments
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: ci/github-actions/_strategies/matrix-build.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

name: Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:           # allow manual trigger with environment selection
    inputs:
      target:
        description: "Target environment"
        required: true
        default: staging
        type: choice
        options: [staging, production]

jobs:
  # ── Build once, deploy everywhere ─────────────────────────────────
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write
      id-token: write
    outputs:
      image-digest: ${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}
      image-tag:    ${{ steps.meta.outputs.version }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Docker metadata
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        with:
          images: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}    # <-- CHANGE THIS
          tags: |
            type=sha,prefix=sha-
            type=semver,pattern={{version}}

      - name: Build and push
        id: build
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to:   type=gha,mode=max

  # ── Deploy to staging (no approval required) ──────────────────────
  deploy-staging:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging           # <-- matches environment name in Settings → Environments
    concurrency:
      group: staging-deploy
      cancel-in-progress: false    # never cancel an in-flight deployment

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Deploy to staging
        run: |
          echo "Deploying ${{ needs.build.outputs.image-tag }} to staging"
          # kubectl set image ... or helm upgrade ...   # <-- CHANGE THIS

  # ── Deploy to production (requires approval) ──────────────────────
  deploy-production:
    needs: deploy-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Note 1: The 'environment' key is what connects this job to the protection rules
    # configured in Settings → Environments. GitHub will pause the job here and
    # notify required reviewers before releasing the job to run.
    environment:
      name: production
      url: https://app.example.com   # <-- CHANGE THIS: shown in the deployment summary

    concurrency:
      group: production-deploy
      cancel-in-progress: false      # NEVER cancel in-progress production deploys

    permissions:
      contents: read
      id-token: write                # needed if using OIDC to cloud

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 2: Secrets scoped to the 'production' environment are ONLY available
      # inside a job that references environment: production. They are not accessible
      # to any other job, including deploy-staging.
      - name: Authenticate to cloud
        run: |
          echo "Using OIDC token to authenticate"
          # aws configure or az login --federated-token   # <-- CHANGE THIS

      - name: Deploy to production
        run: |
          echo "Deploying ${{ needs.build.outputs.image-tag }} to production"
          # kubectl set image ... or helm upgrade ...   # <-- CHANGE THIS

      - name: Notify on success
        if: success()
        uses: ./.github/workflows/notify-slack.yml    # <-- CHANGE THIS or remove
```

---

## Protecting secrets per environment

1. In **Settings → Environments → production**, add secrets (e.g. `KUBECONFIG`, `PROD_DB_URL`).
2. These secrets are NOT available to jobs targeting other environments.
3. Repository secrets are available everywhere — only use them for non-sensitive values.

```
Hierarchy (most to least restricted):
  Environment secrets   → only in jobs with matching environment:
  Repository secrets    → any job in any workflow
  Organization secrets  → any repo in the org (if policy allows)
```

---

## API / Terraform equivalent (for IaC teams)

GitHub Environments can be managed via the REST API or third-party Terraform providers.
The `github` Terraform provider supports `github_repository_environment` and
`github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` resources for GitOps-style environment config.

```hcl
resource "github_repository_environment" "production" {
  repository  = "my-repo"              # <-- CHANGE THIS
  environment = "production"

  reviewers {
    teams = [data.github_team.ops.id]  # <-- CHANGE THIS
  }

  deployment_branch_policy {
    protected_branches     = true
    custom_branch_policies = false
  }
}
```

---

## Related files

- `ci/github-actions/_strategies/release-please.yml` — triggers deployment after a release tag
- `ci/github-actions/_shared/reusable-docker-build.yml` — build step called from the deploy workflow
- `docs/decisions/` — record your environment topology as an ADR

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — PR Quality Gate
#   1. Conventional commit enforcer (commitlint)
#   2. PR size checker (blocks XL PRs, warns on L)
# WHEN TO USE: Add to every repo that uses release-please or
#   semantic-release. Run on every pull_request event.
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - commitlint.config.js at repo root (template in comments below)
#   - See docs/guides/conventional-commits.md for the commit format
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES:
#   - ci/github-actions/_strategies/release-please.yml
#   - ci/github-actions/_strategies/semantic-release.yml
#   - docs/guides/conventional-commits.md
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

name: PR Quality Gate

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened, edited]
    branches: [main, develop]      # <-- CHANGE THIS: branches you merge into

# Note 1: Only read access is needed — this workflow never writes back to the repo.
permissions:
  contents: read
  pull-requests: read

jobs:
  # ── Job 1: Conventional commit check ────────────────────────────────────
  # Validates every commit in the PR individually, plus the PR title
  # (useful when squash-merging — the PR title becomes the merge commit).
  commit-lint:
    name: Commit Convention Check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          # Note 2: fetch-depth 0 gives access to all commits in the PR branch
          # so commitlint can inspect every commit, not just the latest.
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Set up Node (for commitlint)
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "lts/*"

      - name: Install commitlint
        run: |
          npm install --no-save \
            @commitlint/cli \
            @commitlint/config-conventional

      # Note 3: We lint from the base branch to HEAD so every commit
      # in the PR is checked, not just the last one.
      - name: Lint commits in this PR
        run: |
          npx commitlint \
            --from ${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }} \
            --to   ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }} \
            --verbose

      # Also lint the PR title — used when squash-merging.
      # The PR title becomes the single merge commit message.
      - name: Lint PR title
        env:
          PR_TITLE: ${{ github.event.pull_request.title }}
        run: |
          echo "$PR_TITLE" | npx commitlint --verbose

  # ── Job 2: PR size check ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Blocks PRs that change too many lines, encourages incremental work.
  # Adjust thresholds to match your team's preference.
  pr-size:
    name: PR Size Check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Calculate changed lines
        id: size
        env:
          BASE_SHA: ${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }}
          HEAD_SHA: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}
        run: |
          # Count insertions + deletions, exclude generated/vendored files
          CHANGED=$(git diff --stat "$BASE_SHA" "$HEAD_SHA" \
            -- ':!*.lock' ':!**/vendor/**' ':!**/__generated__/**' ':!*.min.*' \
            | tail -1 \
            | grep -oP '\d+(?= insertion)' || echo 0)

          DELETED=$(git diff --stat "$BASE_SHA" "$HEAD_SHA" \
            -- ':!*.lock' ':!**/vendor/**' ':!**/__generated__/**' ':!*.min.*' \
            | tail -1 \
            | grep -oP '\d+(?= deletion)' || echo 0)

          TOTAL=$((CHANGED + DELETED))
          echo "total=$TOTAL" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "PR changes $TOTAL lines (additions + deletions, excluding generated files)"

      # Note 4: Size thresholds — adjust to your team's norms.
      #   XS  <  50 lines  — config tweak, doc update
      #   S   < 200 lines  — typical bug fix
      #   M   < 500 lines  — feature
      #   L   < 800 lines  — large feature (warn)
      #   XL  ≥ 800 lines  — block and require justification  # <-- CHANGE THIS threshold
      - name: Enforce size limit
        env:
          TOTAL: ${{ steps.size.outputs.total }}
          WARN_THRESHOLD: 500      # <-- CHANGE THIS
          FAIL_THRESHOLD: 800      # <-- CHANGE THIS
        run: |
          echo "Changed lines: $TOTAL"

          if [ "$TOTAL" -ge "$FAIL_THRESHOLD" ]; then
            echo ""
            echo "❌ PR is too large ($TOTAL lines ≥ $FAIL_THRESHOLD limit)."
            echo ""
            echo "Large PRs are hard to review safely and increase merge risk."
            echo "Please split this PR into smaller, focused changes."
            echo "If this is unavoidable (e.g. a migration), add the label"
            echo "'large-pr-approved' and a comment explaining why."
            exit 1
          fi

          if [ "$TOTAL" -ge "$WARN_THRESHOLD" ]; then
            echo ""
            echo "⚠️  PR is large ($TOTAL lines ≥ $WARN_THRESHOLD warning threshold)."
            echo "Consider splitting into smaller PRs for easier review."
          fi

          echo "✅ PR size is acceptable ($TOTAL lines)"

      # Note 5: Override gate — if the PR has the 'large-pr-approved' label,
      # skip the size check. Useful for migrations and large refactors.
      - name: Check for large-pr-approved label override
        if: failure()
        env:
          LABELS: ${{ toJson(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name) }}
        run: |
          if echo "$LABELS" | grep -q "large-pr-approved"; then
            echo "Label 'large-pr-approved' found — overriding size check."
            exit 0
          fi
          exit 1

# ============================================================
# commitlint.config.js — place at repo root
# ============================================================
# module.exports = {
#   extends: ['@commitlint/config-conventional'],
#   rules: {
#     'type-enum': [
#       2, 'always',
#       ['feat', 'fix', 'docs', 'style', 'refactor', 'perf',
#        'test', 'chore', 'ci', 'build', 'revert'],
#     ],
#     'subject-case':      [2, 'always', 'lower-case'],
#     'header-max-length': [2, 'always', 100],
#     'body-max-line-length': [1, 'always', 120],   // 1 = warning
#   },
# };

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Reusable Artifact Attestation
#   Provides SLSA provenance + SBOM + cosign image signing
#   for any container image built in this repo.
#
# WHEN TO USE: Call from any workflow that builds and pushes
#   a Docker image. Replaces the inline SBOM step that only
#   some workflows had, giving consistent coverage everywhere.
#
# WHAT THIS PROVIDES:
#   1. SBOM (SPDX-JSON) — attached as OCI referrer and uploaded
#      as a workflow artifact. Satisfies supply-chain audit requirements.
#   2. SLSA provenance attestation — signed by GitHub's OIDC token
#      via actions/attest-build-provenance. Meets SLSA Build L2.
#   3. cosign keyless signing — signs the image digest in Sigstore's
#      transparency log (Rekor). Verifiable without managing keys.
#
# SLSA levels in this template:
#   L1: Provenance exists (attestation written)
#   L2: Provenance is signed (GitHub OIDC token, Sigstore Rekor)
#   L3: Requires hermetic builds on GitHub-hosted runners with no
#       network access — not covered here; needs slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - Image already built and pushed (pass the digest as input)
#   - Calling job needs: id-token: write, attestations: write, packages: write
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES:
#   - ci/github-actions/dotnet/docker-publish.yml
#   - ci/github-actions/_shared/reusable-docker-build.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

name: Reusable Artifact Attestation

on:
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      image-ref:
        description: "Full image reference including digest, e.g. ghcr.io/org/app@sha256:abc123"
        required: true
        type: string
      image-digest:
        description: "Image digest (sha256:...) returned by docker/build-push-action"
        required: true
        type: string
      sbom-artifact-name:
        description: "Name for the uploaded SBOM artifact"
        required: false
        type: string
        default: sbom-${{ github.sha }}.spdx.json
    secrets:
      registry-token:
        description: "Token with push access to the registry (for cosign)"
        required: true

jobs:
  attest:
    name: Sign & Attest
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Note 1: These three permissions are ALL required.
    # id-token: write   — lets cosign get an OIDC token from GitHub, no keys needed
    # attestations: write — lets actions/attest-build-provenance write attestations
    # packages: write   — lets cosign push the signature/attestation to GHCR
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      attestations: write
      packages: write

    steps:
      # ── SBOM ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      # Note 2: anchore/sbom-action scans the image layers and produces an SPDX
      # JSON SBOM. It attaches it to the image as an OCI referrer (so it stays
      # with the image in the registry) AND uploads it as a workflow artifact.
      - name: Generate SBOM
        uses: anchore/sbom-action@v0
        with:
          image:         ${{ inputs.image-ref }}
          format:        spdx-json
          output-file:   sbom.spdx.json
          upload-artifact: true
          artifact-name: ${{ inputs.sbom-artifact-name }}

      # ── SLSA provenance attestation ────────────────────────────────────────
      # Note 3: actions/attest-build-provenance writes a signed SLSA provenance
      # attestation that records: the workflow that triggered the build, the
      # git ref + SHA, the repository, and the build runner. It is signed using
      # the job's OIDC token so no secrets are needed.
      # Verify with: gh attestation verify <image> --repo <owner/repo>
      - name: Attest build provenance
        uses: actions/attest-build-provenance@v1
        with:
          subject-name:   ${{ inputs.image-ref }}
          subject-digest: ${{ inputs.image-digest }}
          push-to-registry: true  # attaches attestation as OCI referrer in registry

      # ── cosign keyless image signing ────────────────────────────────────────
      # Note 4: cosign signs the image digest using Sigstore's keyless flow:
      #   1. Gets an OIDC token from GitHub (the job's id-token)
      #   2. Exchanges it for a short-lived certificate from Fulcio (Sigstore CA)
      #   3. Creates a signature and records it in Rekor (public transparency log)
      # No private keys are stored anywhere. The identity claim in the cert is
      # the GitHub Actions workflow URL, making it auditable.
      - name: Install cosign
        uses: sigstore/cosign-installer@v3

      - name: Log in to registry for cosign
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io                         # <-- CHANGE THIS if using ACR/ECR/Artifact Registry
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.registry-token }}

      - name: Sign image with cosign (keyless)
        env:
          COSIGN_EXPERIMENTAL: "1"   # enables keyless (OIDC) signing
        run: |
          cosign sign --yes \
            ${{ inputs.image-ref }}

          echo ""
          echo "Image signed. To verify on any machine:"
          echo "  cosign verify \\"
          echo "    --certificate-identity-regexp 'https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}' \\"
          echo "    --certificate-oidc-issuer     'https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com' \\"
          echo "    ${{ inputs.image-ref }}"

      # ── Verify (self-check) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
      # Note 5: Verify the signature immediately after signing as a sanity check.
      # This catches registry connectivity issues and misconfigured OIDC issuers
      # before the image is deployed.
      - name: Verify cosign signature
        env:
          COSIGN_EXPERIMENTAL: "1"
        run: |
          cosign verify \
            --certificate-identity-regexp "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" \
            --certificate-oidc-issuer     "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" \
            ${{ inputs.image-ref }}

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Reusable Docker Build & Push
# WHEN TO USE: Call from any workflow that needs to build and push images
# PREREQUISITES: Container registry accessible from GitHub Actions
# SECRETS NEEDED: registry-token (e.g., GITHUB_TOKEN for GHCR)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Inputs when calling this workflow
# RELATED FILES: ci/github-actions/dotnet/docker-publish.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Reusable Docker Build

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      image-name:
        required: true
        # Note 4: This declaration defines a reusable unit, which supports composition and makes behavior easier to test.
        type: string
      dockerfile:
        # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        required: false
        type: string
        # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        default: Dockerfile
      context:
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        required: false
        type: string
        # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        default: .
    secrets:
      # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      registry-token:
        required: true
    outputs:
      # Note 10: Expose the image digest so callers can pass it to the attest
      # reusable workflow (reusable-attest.yml) for SBOM + cosign signing.
      image-digest:
        description: "sha256 digest of the pushed image"
        value: ${{ jobs.docker-build.outputs.image-digest }}

# Note 11: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  docker-build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image-digest: ${{ steps.build-push.outputs.digest }}
    permissions:
      # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      contents: read
      packages: write

    # Note 13: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          # Note 16: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.registry-token }}

      # Note 17: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Set up Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      # Note 18: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Docker metadata
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        with:
          images: ghcr.io/${{ inputs.image-name }}
          tags: |
            type=sha
            type=ref,event=branch

      - name: Build and push
        id: build-push          # id required so outputs.digest is accessible
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: ${{ inputs.context }}
          file: ${{ inputs.dockerfile }}
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Reusable Slack Notification
# WHEN TO USE: Call from any workflow to send Slack alerts
# PREREQUISITES: Slack Incoming Webhook URL configured
# SECRETS NEEDED: slack-webhook (Slack Incoming Webhook URL)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Inputs when calling this workflow
# RELATED FILES: notifications/slack-notify.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Reusable Slack Notify

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      status:
        required: true
        # Note 4: This declaration defines a reusable unit, which supports composition and makes behavior easier to test.
        type: string  # success | failure | cancelled
      message:
        # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        required: false
        type: string
    # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    secrets:
      slack-webhook:
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        required: true

jobs:
  # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  notify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Note 9: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - name: Send Slack notification
        # Note 10: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v1
        with:
          # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          payload: |
            {
              # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              "text": "${{ inputs.status == 'success' && '✅' || '❌' }} *${{ github.repository }}* — ${{ inputs.message || github.workflow }} (${{ github.ref_name }})",
              "attachments": [{
                "color": "${{ inputs.status == 'success' && 'good' || 'danger' }}",
                "fields": [
                  { "title": "Run", "value": "${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}", "short": false }
                ]
              }]
            }
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.slack-webhook }}
          SLACK_WEBHOOK_TYPE: INCOMING_WEBHOOK

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Reusable Trivy Security Scan
# WHEN TO USE: Call after any Docker image build to scan for CVEs
# PREREQUISITES: Built Docker image available as image-ref
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Inputs when calling this workflow
# RELATED FILES: security/container-scanning/trivy-scan.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Reusable Security Scan

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      image-ref:
        required: true
        # Note 4: This declaration defines a reusable unit, which supports composition and makes behavior easier to test.
        type: string

jobs:
  # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  trivy-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Note 6: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - name: Run Trivy vulnerability scanner
        # Note 7: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@0.28.0
        with:
          # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          image-ref: ${{ inputs.image-ref }}
          format: "sarif"
          # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          output: trivy-results.sarif
          severity: "CRITICAL,HIGH"

      - name: Upload Trivy results to GitHub Security
        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
        with:
          sarif_file: trivy-results.sarif

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Matrix Build Strategy
# WHEN TO USE: Testing across multiple OS / runtime versions
# PREREQUISITES: Node.js project (adapt for other runtimes)
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Matrix values for your target platforms
# RELATED FILES: None
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Matrix Build Example

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main]

# Note 3: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  build:
    # Note 4: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Build (${{ matrix.os }} / ${{ matrix.node }})
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}

    # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false  # Don't cancel other matrix jobs on failure
      # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest]
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        node: ["18", "20"]
        exclude:
          # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          - os: macos-latest
            node: "18"  # Skip this combination

    # Note 9: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Setup Node ${{ matrix.node }}
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node }}

      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Monorepo Affected Services
# WHEN TO USE: Monorepos with multiple services in subdirectories
# PREREQUISITES: dorny/paths-filter action, per-service workflows
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Path filters and service names
# RELATED FILES: None
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Monorepo — Build Affected Services

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Note 4: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  detect-changes:
    # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      user-service: ${{ steps.filter.outputs.user-service }}
      order-service: ${{ steps.filter.outputs.order-service }}
      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      api-gateway: ${{ steps.filter.outputs.api-gateway }}

    steps:
      # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: dorny/paths-filter@v3
        # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        id: filter
        with:
          # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          filters: |
            user-service:
              # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              - 'services/user-service/**'
            order-service:
              # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              - 'services/order-service/**'
            api-gateway:
              # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
              - 'services/api-gateway/**'
              - 'shared/**'  # Shared changes affect gateway

  # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  build-user-service:
    needs: detect-changes
    if: needs.detect-changes.outputs.user-service == 'true'
    uses: ./.github/workflows/build-service.yml
    with:
      service: user-service

  build-order-service:
    needs: detect-changes
    if: needs.detect-changes.outputs.order-service == 'true'
    uses: ./.github/workflows/build-service.yml
    with:
      service: order-service
