# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Release Please (automated releases)
# WHEN TO USE: Automating changelog, version bumps, and GitHub Releases
# PREREQUISITES: Conventional Commits in your commit messages
# SECRETS NEEDED: NPM_TOKEN (if publishing to npm)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: release-type and publish steps
# RELATED FILES: None
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Release Please

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main]

# Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
permissions:
  contents: write
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  release-please:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Note 6: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - uses: googleapis/release-please-action@v4
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        id: release
        with:
          # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          release-type: node  # or: python, java, go, etc.
          # Reads conventional commits to bump version automatically

      # Only run on release (not on every push)
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        # Note 9: Control flow should stay readable; predictable branches reduce defects and simplify troubleshooting.
        if: ${{ steps.release.outputs.release_created }}

      - name: Publish to npm
        # Note 10: Control flow should stay readable; predictable branches reduce defects and simplify troubleshooting.
        if: ${{ steps.release.outputs.release_created }}
        run: |
          # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          npm ci
          npm publish
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — semantic-release (non-Node stacks)
# WHEN TO USE: Automated versioning + changelog for Python, Go,
#              .NET, Java, or Ruby repos where release-please is
#              not a natural fit. semantic-release runs on every
#              push to main and cuts a release directly (no PR).
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - Conventional Commits in every commit (see docs/guides/conventional-commits.md)
#   - .releaserc.json at repo root (example provided below)
#   - Node.js available in the runner (only for running semantic-release itself)
# SECRETS NEEDED: GITHUB_TOKEN (automatic), NPM_TOKEN only if publishing to npm
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES:
#   - ci/github-actions/_strategies/release-please.yml (alternative)
#   - ci/github-actions/_shared/pr-conventional-commit.yml (enforcer)
#   - docs/guides/conventional-commits.md
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

name: Semantic Release

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main             # <-- CHANGE THIS: add 'beta' or 'next' for pre-release channels

# Note 1: semantic-release needs write access to push the release commit,
# create the tag, and post the GitHub Release. id-token is only required
# if you add an OIDC-based cloud publish step below.
permissions:
  contents: write
  issues: write
  pull-requests: write
  id-token: write

jobs:
  release:
    name: Cut Release
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Note 2: Concurrency ensures only one release runs at a time on main.
    # cancel-in-progress: false is intentional — we never want to drop a release mid-flight.
    concurrency:
      group: semantic-release
      cancel-in-progress: false

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0          # full history needed for changelog generation
          persist-credentials: false  # use GITHUB_TOKEN from env, not git credential store

      # ── Language-specific build/test step ─────────────────────────────────
      # Run your existing build before cutting the release so a broken build
      # cannot produce a new version tag.

      # Python example
      - name: Set up Python
        if: false                 # <-- CHANGE THIS: remove this 'if: false' to enable
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
      - name: Python build & test
        if: false                 # <-- CHANGE THIS
        run: |
          pip install -e ".[dev]"
          pytest --tb=short

      # Go example
      - name: Set up Go
        if: false                 # <-- CHANGE THIS: remove this 'if: false' to enable
        uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version: "1.22"      # <-- CHANGE THIS
      - name: Go build & test
        if: false                 # <-- CHANGE THIS
        run: |
          go test ./... -race -count=1

      # .NET example
      - name: Set up .NET
        if: false                 # <-- CHANGE THIS: remove this 'if: false' to enable
        uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: "9.x"   # <-- CHANGE THIS
      - name: .NET build & test
        if: false                 # <-- CHANGE THIS
        run: |
          dotnet build --configuration Release
          dotnet test --no-build --verbosity normal
      # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

      # semantic-release is a Node package, but it works for any language repo.
      # It reads .releaserc.json, parses commits, and creates the GitHub Release.
      - name: Set up Node (for semantic-release only)
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "lts/*"

      - name: Install semantic-release
        run: |
          npm install --no-save \
            semantic-release \
            @semantic-release/commit-analyzer \
            @semantic-release/release-notes-generator \
            @semantic-release/changelog \
            @semantic-release/git \
            @semantic-release/github
          # Optional extras:                                  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          # @semantic-release/exec            — run arbitrary shell commands per lifecycle
          # @semantic-release/npm             — bump package.json version
          # semantic-release-pypi             — publish to PyPI
          # @semantic-release/slack-notify    — post Slack message on release

      - name: Run semantic-release
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          # NPM_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}              # <-- CHANGE THIS: uncomment for npm publish
          # PYPI_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PYPI_TOKEN }}            # <-- CHANGE THIS: uncomment for PyPI
        run: npx semantic-release

# ============================================================
# .releaserc.json — place this at the repo root
# ============================================================
# {
#   "branches": ["main"],
#
#   "Note 1": "commit-analyzer decides the version bump based on commit types",
#   "plugins": [
#     ["@semantic-release/commit-analyzer", {
#       "preset": "conventionalcommits",
#       "releaseRules": [
#         { "type": "feat",     "release": "minor" },
#         { "type": "fix",      "release": "patch" },
#         { "type": "perf",     "release": "patch" },
#         { "type": "revert",   "release": "patch" },
#         { "type": "docs",     "release": false   },
#         { "type": "style",    "release": false   },
#         { "type": "refactor", "release": false   },
#         { "type": "test",     "release": false   },
#         { "type": "chore",    "release": false   },
#         { "breaking": true,   "release": "major" }
#       ]
#     }],
#
#     "Note 2": "release-notes-generator builds the changelog content",
#     "@semantic-release/release-notes-generator",
#
#     "Note 3": "changelog plugin writes CHANGELOG.md",
#     ["@semantic-release/changelog", {
#       "changelogFile": "CHANGELOG.md"
#     }],
#
#     "Note 4": "git plugin commits the updated CHANGELOG.md and version file back to main",
#     ["@semantic-release/git", {
#       "assets": ["CHANGELOG.md", "pyproject.toml", "package.json"],
#       "message": "chore(release): ${nextRelease.version} [skip ci]\n\n${nextRelease.notes}"
#     }],
#
#     "Note 5": "github plugin creates the GitHub Release with release notes attached",
#     "@semantic-release/github"
#   ]
# }

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Angular Build & Test
# WHEN TO USE: Any Angular 17+ project
# PREREQUISITES: package.json with lint, test, and build scripts
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: docker/angular/Dockerfile
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Angular Build & Test

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Note 4: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  build-test:
    # Note 5: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Build & Test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Note 6: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          node-version: "20"
          # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          cache: "npm"

      - name: Install dependencies
        # Note 10: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: npm ci

      - name: Lint
        # Note 11: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Test
        # Note 12: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: npm run test -- --watch=false --browsers=ChromeHeadless --code-coverage

      - name: Build
        # Note 13: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: npm run build -- --configuration production

      - name: Upload build artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: angular-dist
          path: dist/
          retention-days: 7

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Lighthouse CI Audit
# WHEN TO USE: Angular/React apps needing performance score gating on PRs
# PREREQUISITES: .lighthouserc.json config file in repo root
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: config path and thresholds in .lighthouserc.json
# RELATED FILES: ci/github-actions/angular/build-test.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Lighthouse Audit

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Note 3: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  lighthouse:
    # Note 4: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Lighthouse CI
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Note 5: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          node-version: "20"
          # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          cache: "npm"

      - name: Install dependencies
        # Note 9: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: npm ci

      - name: Build
        # Note 10: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: npm run build -- --configuration production

      - name: Run Lighthouse CI
        # Note 11: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: treosh/lighthouse-ci-action@v11
        with:
          uploadArtifacts: true
          temporaryPublicStorage: true
          configPath: .lighthouserc.json

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — .NET Build & Test
# WHEN TO USE: Any ASP.NET Core / .NET 8+ project
# PREREQUISITES: None (self-contained)
# SECRETS NEEDED: None for CI / CODECOV_TOKEN for coverage upload
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: ci/github-actions/dotnet/sonar-scan.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: .NET Build & Test

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
env:
  DOTNET_VERSION: "8.0.x"

# Note 5: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  build-test:
    # Note 6: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Build & Test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Note 7: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        # Note 8: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup .NET
        # Note 9: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          dotnet-version: ${{ env.DOTNET_VERSION }}

      - name: Restore dependencies
        # Note 11: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: dotnet restore

      - name: Build
        # Note 12: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: dotnet build --no-restore --configuration Release

      - name: Test
        # Note 13: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        # Note 14: --collect:"XPlat Code Coverage" wires in the cross-platform coverage driver; results land in ./TestResults.
        run: dotnet test --no-build --configuration Release \
          --logger trx \
          --collect:"XPlat Code Coverage" \
          --results-directory ./TestResults

      # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Upload test results
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: test-results
          path: TestResults/

      - name: Upload coverage to Codecov
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v5
        with:
          directory: TestResults

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — .NET Docker Build & Publish
# WHEN TO USE: Publishing .NET container images to GHCR or ACR
# PREREQUISITES: Dockerfile in the repository
# SECRETS NEEDED: GITHUB_TOKEN (automatic for GHCR)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: docker/dotnet/Dockerfile.api
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: .NET Docker Build & Publish

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main]
    # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    tags: ["v*.*.*"]

env:
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }}

# Note 5: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  docker:
    # Note 6: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Build & Push Docker Image
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Note 7: Pass the image digest forward so the attest job can sign the exact
    # immutable digest rather than a mutable tag.
    outputs:
      image-digest: ${{ steps.build-push.outputs.digest }}
    # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write

    steps:
      # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Log in to Container Registry
        # Note 10: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Extract Docker metadata
        # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          images: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}
          # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          tags: |
            type=semver,pattern={{version}}
            type=semver,pattern={{major}}.{{minor}}
            type=sha,prefix=sha-
            type=raw,value=latest,enable=${{ github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' }}

      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Build and push
        id: build-push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: .
          file: docker/dotnet/Dockerfile.api                 # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to your Dockerfile
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

  # ── Attestation job — SBOM + SLSA provenance + cosign signing ──────────────
  # Note 9: Runs after the build job completes. Uses the reusable workflow that
  # consistently applies SBOM generation, SLSA provenance attestation, and
  # cosign keyless signing across all images in this repo.
  attest:
    name: Sign & Attest
    needs: docker
    uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-attest.yml      # <-- CHANGE THIS to your workflow path
    permissions:
      id-token:     write
      attestations: write
      packages:     write
    with:
      image-ref:    ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}@${{ needs.docker.outputs.image-digest }}
      image-digest: ${{ needs.docker.outputs.image-digest }}
      sbom-artifact-name: sbom-dotnet-${{ github.sha }}.spdx.json
    secrets:
      registry-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — .NET SonarQube/SonarCloud Scan
# WHEN TO USE: .NET projects needing code quality gates and PR decoration
# PREREQUISITES: SonarQube or SonarCloud project configured
# SECRETS NEEDED: SONAR_TOKEN
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: quality/sonar-project.properties
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: .NET SonarQube Scan

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Note 4: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  sonar:
    # Note 5: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: SonarQube Analysis
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Note 6: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0  # Full history required by SonarQube

      # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Setup .NET
        uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          dotnet-version: "8.0.x"

      # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Install SonarScanner
        run: dotnet tool install --global dotnet-sonarscanner

      # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Begin SonarQube analysis
        run: |
          # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          dotnet-sonarscanner begin \
            /k:"${{ vars.SONAR_PROJECT_KEY }}" \
            # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            /d:sonar.host.url="${{ vars.SONAR_HOST_URL }}" \
            /d:sonar.token="${{ secrets.SONAR_TOKEN }}" \
            # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            /d:sonar.cs.opencover.reportsPaths="**/coverage.opencover.xml"

      - name: Build
        run: dotnet build --configuration Release

      - name: Test with coverage
        run: dotnet test --collect:"XPlat Code Coverage;Format=opencover"

      - name: End SonarQube analysis
        run: dotnet-sonarscanner end /d:sonar.token="${{ secrets.SONAR_TOKEN }}"

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Go Build & Test
# WHEN TO USE: Any Go project with go.mod
# PREREQUISITES: go.mod and go.sum in project root
# SECRETS NEEDED: None / CODECOV_TOKEN for coverage upload
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: docker/go/Dockerfile
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Go Build & Test

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Note 4: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  build-test:
    # Note 5: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Build & Test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    strategy:
      matrix:
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        go-version: ["1.23", "1.24"]            # <-- CHANGE THIS: your supported Go versions

    steps:
      # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Go ${{ matrix.go-version }}
        # Note 9: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          go-version: ${{ matrix.go-version }}
          cache: true                            # Caches ~/go/pkg/mod and ~/.cache/go-build

      # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Download dependencies
        run: go mod download

      # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Verify dependencies
        run: go mod verify

      # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Lint with golangci-lint
        uses: golangci/golangci-lint-action@v6
        # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          version: latest
          # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          args: --timeout=5m

      - name: Vet
        # Note 16: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: go vet ./...

      - name: Test with coverage
        # Note 17: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: go test -v -race -coverprofile=coverage.out -covermode=atomic ./...

      - name: Check coverage threshold
        # Note 18: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: |
          COVERAGE=$(go tool cover -func=coverage.out | grep total | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/%//')
          # Note 19: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          echo "Total coverage: ${COVERAGE}%"
          THRESHOLD=70                           # <-- CHANGE THIS: your minimum coverage
          # Note 20: Control flow should stay readable; predictable branches reduce defects and simplify troubleshooting.
          if (( $(echo "$COVERAGE < $THRESHOLD" | bc -l) )); then
            echo "::error::Coverage ${COVERAGE}% is below threshold ${THRESHOLD}%"
            # Note 21: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
            exit 1
          fi

      # Note 22: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Upload coverage
        if: matrix.go-version == '1.24'          # Upload once, not per matrix entry
        # Note 23: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v5
        with:
          # Note 24: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          file: coverage.out

      - name: Build
        # Note 25: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: go build -v ./...

  # Build Docker image on main branch
  docker:
    # Note 26: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Docker Build
    needs: build-test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write
      attestations: write   # Required for anchore/sbom-action to attach SBOM as OCI referrer
      id-token: write       # Required for OIDC-based attestation signing

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Extract Docker metadata
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        with:
          images: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}
          tags: |
            type=sha,prefix=sha-
            type=raw,value=latest,enable=${{ github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' }}

      - name: Build and push
        id: build-push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: .
          file: docker/go/Dockerfile             # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to your Dockerfile
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

      # Generate a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) in SPDX format and
      # attach it to the image as an OCI referrer. Required for SLSA level 2+.
      - name: Generate SBOM
        uses: anchore/sbom-action@v0
        with:
          image: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}@${{ steps.build-push.outputs.digest }}
          format: spdx-json
          output-file: sbom.spdx.json
          upload-artifact: true
          artifact-name: sbom-go-${{ github.sha }}.spdx.json

# ============================================================

          format: spdx-json
          output-file: sbom.spdx.json
          upload-artifact: true
          artifact-name: sbom-go-${{ github.sha }}.spdx.json

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Java Build & Test (Maven + Gradle)
# WHEN TO USE: Java / Spring Boot projects
# PREREQUISITES: pom.xml (Maven) or build.gradle (Gradle)
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: docker/java/Dockerfile.springboot, docker/java/Dockerfile.gradle
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Java Build & Test

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Note 4: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  build-test-maven:
    # Note 5: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Maven Build & Test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Note 6: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Set up JDK 21
        uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          java-version: "21"
          # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          distribution: "temurin"
          cache: "maven"

      # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Build with Maven
        run: mvn -B package --file pom.xml

      # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Run tests
        run: mvn -B test

      # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Upload Surefire reports
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        # Note 13: Control flow should stay readable; predictable branches reduce defects and simplify troubleshooting.
        if: always()
        with:
          # Note 14: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
          name: surefire-reports
          path: target/surefire-reports/

  # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  build-test-gradle:
    name: Gradle Build & Test
    # Note 16: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      # Note 17: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up JDK 21
        uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with:
          java-version: "21"
          distribution: "temurin"
          cache: "gradle"

      - name: Build with Gradle
        run: ./gradlew build

      - name: Run tests
        run: ./gradlew test

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Python Build & Test
# WHEN TO USE: Any Python project with pytest
# PREREQUISITES: requirements.txt and requirements-dev.txt
# SECRETS NEEDED: None / CODECOV_TOKEN for coverage
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: quality/python/pyproject.toml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Python Build & Test

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Note 4: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  build-test:
    # Note 5: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Build & Test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    strategy:
      matrix:
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        python-version: ["3.12", "3.13"]

    steps:
      # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python ${{ matrix.python-version }}
        # Note 9: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
          cache: "pip"

      # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install -r requirements.txt
          # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          pip install -r requirements-dev.txt

      - name: Lint with ruff
        # Note 14: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: ruff check .

      - name: Format check with black
        # Note 15: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: black --check .

      - name: Type check with mypy
        run: mypy .

      - name: Test with pytest
        run: pytest --cov=. --cov-report=xml --cov-report=term-missing

      - name: Upload coverage
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v5
        with:
          file: coverage.xml

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Python Security Scan

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

    branches: [main]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

# Note 5: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  security:

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

        # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

      # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

        run: |
          # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

      # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

      # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

      # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Run pip-audit (dependency CVEs)
        run: pip-audit --format json --output pip-audit-report.json || true

      - name: Upload reports
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: security-reports
          path: |
            bandit-report.json
            pip-audit-report.json
