﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Ruby on Rails Build & Test
# WHEN TO USE: Any Rails 7+ project
# PREREQUISITES: Gemfile, Gemfile.lock, database.yml with test config
# SECRETS NEEDED: None / CODECOV_TOKEN for coverage upload
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: docker/ruby/Dockerfile.rails
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Rails Build & Test

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
env:
  RAILS_ENV: test

# Note 5: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  build-test:
    # Note 6: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Build & Test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    strategy:
      matrix:
        # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        ruby-version: ["3.3", "3.4"]             # <-- CHANGE THIS: your supported Ruby versions

    services:
      # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      postgres:                                   # <-- CHANGE THIS: remove if using SQLite
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: test
          # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
          POSTGRES_DB: app_test
        # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          --health-retries 5

      redis:                                      # <-- CHANGE THIS: remove if not using Redis
        # Note 16: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        image: redis:7-alpine
        ports:
          # Note 17: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          - 6379:6379
        options: >-
          # Note 18: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          --health-cmd "redis-cli ping"
          --health-interval 10s
          # Note 19: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5

    # Note 20: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    env:
      DATABASE_URL: postgres://test:test@localhost:5432/app_test  # <-- CHANGE THIS: match postgres service
      # Note 21: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      REDIS_URL: redis://localhost:6379/0

    steps:
      # Note 22: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Ruby ${{ matrix.ruby-version }}
        # Note 23: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: ruby/setup-ruby@v1
        with:
          # Note 24: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          ruby-version: ${{ matrix.ruby-version }}
          bundler-cache: true                     # Caches gems automatically

      # Note 25: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Set up Node.js (for asset pipeline)
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        # Note 26: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          node-version: "22"                     # <-- CHANGE THIS: remove if API-only
          # Note 27: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          cache: yarn                            # <-- CHANGE THIS: use npm if not using Yarn

      - name: Install JS dependencies
        # Note 28: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: yarn install --frozen-lockfile       # <-- CHANGE THIS: remove if API-only

      - name: Set up database
        # Note 29: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: |
          bundle exec rails db:schema:load

      # Note 30: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Lint with RuboCop
        run: bundle exec rubocop --parallel

      # Note 31: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Security audit with Brakeman
        run: bundle exec brakeman --no-pager --quiet

      # Note 32: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Run tests
        run: |
          bundle exec rails test                 # <-- CHANGE THIS: use `bundle exec rspec` for RSpec

      - name: Upload coverage
        if: matrix.ruby-version == '3.3'
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
        with:
          file: coverage/.resultset.json         # <-- CHANGE THIS: path depends on SimpleCov config

  # Build Docker image on main branch
  docker:
    name: Docker Build
    needs: build-test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Build and push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: .
          file: docker/ruby/Dockerfile.rails     # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to your Dockerfile
          push: true
          tags: |
            ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }}
            ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:latest

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Terraform Cost Estimation with Infracost
# WHEN TO USE: On every pull request that changes Terraform files.
#   Posts a monthly cost diff table as a PR comment so the team
#   can review cost impact before merging.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - Infracost API key from https://www.infracost.io/docs/
#   - Add INFRACOST_API_KEY to GitHub repository secrets
#   - Cloud credentials (AWS/Azure/GCP) available as GitHub secrets or OIDC role
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: ci/github-actions/terraform/plan-apply.yml
#                terraform/aws-eks/, terraform/azure-aks/
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

name: Terraform Cost Estimation

on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main        # <-- CHANGE THIS
    paths:
      - "terraform/**"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: adjust to your Terraform root

# Note 1: Read access to contents and write access to pull-requests are the minimum
# permissions needed: read code + post the cost comment on the PR.
permissions:
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write

# Note 2: One concurrent cost estimation job per branch avoids comment spam on
# rapidly-updated PRs. If a new push arrives while the previous run is in progress,
# the in-progress run is cancelled and the new one starts fresh.
concurrency:
  group: infracost-${{ github.head_ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  cost-estimation:
    name: Cost Estimation
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 20

    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 3: Infracost is a free, open-source CLI that reads a Terraform plan (or
      # HCL directly with --path) and prices each resource using cloud provider APIs.
      # The API key is used to pull up-to-date pricing data from infracost.io.
      # Without a key the job runs in degraded mode: known prices are used but
      # the comment will note which resources have estimated costs.
      - name: Setup Infracost
        uses: infracost/actions/setup@v3
        with:
          api-key: ${{ secrets.INFRACOST_API_KEY }}   # <-- CHANGE THIS: add to GitHub secrets

      # Note 4: We run Infracost against the base branch first so we can compute
      # the DIFF (cost change introduced by this PR) rather than the total cost.
      # This is far more useful in reviews: "this PR adds $12/month" is actionable;
      # "the infrastructure costs $3,400/month" is just noise.
      - name: Generate cost estimate for base branch
        run: |
          infracost breakdown \
            --path="${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}" \
            --terraform-var-file="${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}" \
            --format=json \
            --out-file=/tmp/infracost-base.json
        env:
          TF_WORKING_DIR: terraform/aws-eks    # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to your Terraform module
          TF_VAR_FILE: environments/production.tfvars   # <-- CHANGE THIS: or remove if unused
          # Note 5: Cloud credentials for Infracost to call pricing APIs.
          # For AWS: provide AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID + AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY or use OIDC.
          # For Azure: provide ARM_CLIENT_ID + ARM_CLIENT_SECRET + ARM_TENANT_ID + ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID.
          # For GCP: provide GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS pointing to a service account key file.
          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}        # <-- CHANGE THIS
          AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }} # <-- CHANGE THIS
          AWS_DEFAULT_REGION: us-east-1                               # <-- CHANGE THIS

      - name: Checkout PR branch
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}

      - name: Generate cost estimate for PR branch
        run: |
          infracost breakdown \
            --path="${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}" \
            --terraform-var-file="${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}" \
            --format=json \
            --out-file=/tmp/infracost-pr.json
        env:
          TF_WORKING_DIR: terraform/aws-eks              # <-- CHANGE THIS: same as above
          TF_VAR_FILE: environments/production.tfvars   # <-- CHANGE THIS: same as above
          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
          AWS_DEFAULT_REGION: us-east-1                  # <-- CHANGE THIS

      # Note 6: `infracost diff` computes the delta between base and PR estimates.
      # It outputs a markdown table with:
      #   - Resources that will be added (green +)
      #   - Resources that will be removed (red -)
      #   - Existing resources whose cost changes (e.g., instance resize)
      #   - A total monthly cost change (+/- $X/month)
      - name: Generate cost diff
        run: |
          infracost diff \
            --path=/tmp/infracost-pr.json \
            --compare-to=/tmp/infracost-base.json \
            --format=json \
            --out-file=/tmp/infracost-diff.json

      # Note 7: Post the diff as a PR comment. Infracost will update the comment
      # if the workflow runs again on the same PR (identified by the comment header).
      # The `--policy-path` flag lets you enforce cost gates (e.g., fail if diff > $500/month);
      # see infracost.io/docs/features/cost_policies for details.
      - name: Post cost diff to PR
        uses: infracost/actions/comment@v3
        with:
          path: /tmp/infracost-diff.json
          behavior: update    # Note 8: 'update' replaces the previous comment; 'new' posts a new one each time

      # Note 9: soft_fail: true means a missing API key or pricing error does NOT
      # fail the PR. Cost estimation is advisory; teams should not block deploys
      # purely on cost failures. If you want to enforce a budget gate, remove this
      # line and add --policy-path to the infracost diff command above.
      - name: Check cost policy (optional gate)
        run: |
          infracost comment github \
            --path=/tmp/infracost-diff.json \
            --repo=${{ github.repository }} \
            --pull-request=${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} \
            --github-token=${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} \
            --behavior=update \
            --log-level=warn || true   # <-- Remove '|| true' to make cost overruns block the PR

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Terraform Drift Detection
# WHEN TO USE: Detect when live infrastructure diverges from the
#   Terraform state (someone applied changes outside Terraform, a
#   cloud provider changed defaults, etc.). Runs daily on a schedule.
#   Opens a GitHub Issue automatically when drift is found.
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - Terraform state in a remote backend (S3, Azure, GCS)
#   - Cloud credentials as GitHub Actions OIDC secrets
#   - GitHub token with Issues write permission
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES:
#   ci/github-actions/terraform/plan-apply.yml
#   terraform/aws-eks/main.tf  (or whichever module to monitor)
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

name: Terraform Drift Detection

on:
  # Note 1: Schedule runs at 06:00 UTC daily — adjust to match your team's
  # working hours so the issue appears when someone is online to triage it.
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 6 * * 1-5"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: Mon-Fri 06:00 UTC (skip weekends)
  # Note 2: workflow_dispatch lets you trigger a manual drift check without
  # waiting for the schedule — useful after an incident or bulk change.
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      environment:
        description: "Environment to check for drift"
        required: true
        default: "production"
        type: choice
        options:
          - production
          - staging
          - development

env:
  TF_VERSION:     "1.10.0"          # <-- CHANGE THIS: keep in sync with plan-apply.yml
  TF_WORKING_DIR: "terraform/aws-eks"  # <-- CHANGE THIS: module under drift check
  TF_VAR_FILE:    "envs/prod.tfvars"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: or leave empty if not using var files
  AWS_REGION:     "us-east-1"          # <-- CHANGE THIS: cloud-specific, used for display only

permissions:
  contents: read
  issues:   write   # Note 3: Required to open a drift issue automatically
  id-token: write   # Note 4: Required for OIDC cloud authentication

# Note 5: Concurrency prevents two drift checks running simultaneously
# (e.g., manual trigger during scheduled run). The cancel-in-progress=false
# ensures the scheduled run finishes rather than being killed by a manual one.
concurrency:
  group: terraform-drift-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  # =============================================
  # DRIFT CHECK — plan with -detailed-exitcode
  # =============================================
  detect-drift:
    name: Detect Terraform Drift
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Note 6: output the drift status so the notify job can check it
    outputs:
      drift-detected: ${{ steps.plan.outputs.drift }}
      plan-output:    ${{ steps.plan.outputs.plan_text }}

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # ── Cloud Authentication (uncomment ONE) ──────────────────────────
      # - name: Azure Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: azure/login@v2
      #   with:
      #     client-id:       ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}    # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #     tenant-id:       ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
      #     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      # - name: AWS Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
      #   with:
      #     role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }} # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #     aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}

      # - name: GCP Auth (Workload Identity)
      #   uses: google-github-actions/auth@v2
      #   with:
      #     workload_identity_provider: ${{ secrets.GCP_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_PROVIDER }} # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #     service_account: ${{ secrets.GCP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT }}

      - name: Setup Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}
          # Note 7: wrapper=false gives us the raw exit code from terraform plan.
          # With wrapper=true, Terraform exit code 2 (drift) would be swallowed.
          terraform_wrapper: false

      - name: Terraform Init
        working-directory: ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}
        run: terraform init -input=false

      - name: Terraform Plan (drift check)
        id: plan
        working-directory: ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}
        # Note 8: Exit codes from `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode`:
        #   0 = no changes (no drift)
        #   1 = error
        #   2 = changes present (DRIFT DETECTED)
        # We capture the exit code manually and set step outputs.
        run: |
          PLAN_ARGS="-detailed-exitcode -input=false -no-color"
          if [ -n "${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}" ]; then
            PLAN_ARGS="${PLAN_ARGS} -var-file=${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}"
          fi

          set +e
          terraform plan ${PLAN_ARGS} 2>&1 | tee /tmp/plan.txt
          EXIT_CODE=${PIPESTATUS[0]}
          set -e

          # Store plan output in step output (truncated to 65535 chars — GH limit)
          PLAN_TEXT=$(cat /tmp/plan.txt | tail -c 60000)
          echo "plan_text<<EOF" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "${PLAN_TEXT}"   >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "EOF"            >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

          if [ "$EXIT_CODE" -eq 2 ]; then
            echo "drift=true"  >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "::warning::Drift detected in ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}"
            exit 0   # Note 9: Don't fail the step — let the notify job open the issue
          elif [ "$EXIT_CODE" -eq 1 ]; then
            echo "drift=false" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "::error::terraform plan failed"
            exit 1
          else
            echo "drift=false" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            echo "No drift detected."
          fi

  # =============================================
  # NOTIFY — open (or update) a GitHub Issue when drift is found
  # =============================================
  open-drift-issue:
    name: Open Drift Issue
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: detect-drift
    if: needs.detect-drift.outputs.drift-detected == 'true'

    steps:
      - uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          script: |
            const title = `[Drift] Terraform drift detected in ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}`;
            const body = [
              "## :warning: Terraform Drift Detected",
              "",
              `**Module:** \`${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}\``,
              `**Detected at:** ${new Date().toISOString()}`,
              `**Triggered by:** \`${{ github.event_name }}\``,
              `**Run:** ${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}`,
              "",
              "### What this means",
              "The live infrastructure no longer matches the Terraform state.",
              "This typically happens when:",
              "- Someone applied changes directly via the cloud console or CLI",
              "- A cloud provider changed default values or resource behaviour",
              "- A resource was manually deleted or recreated",
              "",
              "### How to resolve",
              "1. Review the plan output below",
              "2. Determine if the live change should be **imported** (`terraform import`) or **reverted** (re-run plan-apply)",
              "3. Apply the fix and close this issue",
              "",
              "### Plan output",
              "```",
              `${{ needs.detect-drift.outputs.plan-output }}`,
              "```",
            ].join("\n");

            // Note 10: Check for an existing open drift issue so we don't spam.
            // If one already exists, add a comment instead of opening a duplicate.
            const { data: issues } = await github.rest.issues.listForRepo({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo:  context.repo.repo,
              state: "open",
              labels: "terraform-drift",
            });

            const existing = issues.find(i => i.title === title);

            if (existing) {
              await github.rest.issues.createComment({
                owner:    context.repo.owner,
                repo:     context.repo.repo,
                issue_number: existing.number,
                body: `Drift still present as of ${new Date().toISOString()}.\n\nRun: ${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}`,
              });
              console.log(`Updated existing drift issue #${existing.number}`);
            } else {
              const { data: issue } = await github.rest.issues.create({
                owner:  context.repo.owner,
                repo:   context.repo.repo,
                title,
                body,
                labels: ["terraform-drift", "infrastructure"],   // <-- CHANGE THIS: labels must exist in your repo
              });
              console.log(`Opened drift issue #${issue.number}: ${issue.html_url}`);
            }

  # =============================================
  # CLOSE ISSUE — auto-close drift issue when drift is resolved
  # =============================================
  close-drift-issue:
    name: Close Resolved Drift Issue
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: detect-drift
    if: needs.detect-drift.outputs.drift-detected == 'false'

    steps:
      - uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          script: |
            const title = `[Drift] Terraform drift detected in ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}`;

            const { data: issues } = await github.rest.issues.listForRepo({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo:  context.repo.repo,
              state: "open",
              labels: "terraform-drift",
            });

            const existing = issues.find(i => i.title === title);

            if (existing) {
              await github.rest.issues.createComment({
                owner:    context.repo.owner,
                repo:     context.repo.repo,
                issue_number: existing.number,
                body: `Drift resolved as of ${new Date().toISOString()}. Closing automatically.\n\nRun: ${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}`,
              });
              await github.rest.issues.update({
                owner:  context.repo.owner,
                repo:   context.repo.repo,
                issue_number: existing.number,
                state:  "closed",
              });
              console.log(`Closed drift issue #${existing.number}`);
            } else {
              console.log("No open drift issue to close.");
            }

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Terraform Module Tests
# WHEN TO USE: Run native `terraform test` on every PR that touches
#   Terraform code. Fast plan-only tests run on PR; integration
#   tests (apply) are gated on manual trigger.
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - Terraform >= 1.6 (native test framework)
#   - test files in terraform/aws-eks/tests/*.tftest.hcl
#   - For integration tests: cloud credentials as Actions secrets
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES:
#   terraform/aws-eks/tests/unit.tftest.hcl
#   terraform/_testing/terratest/aws_eks_test.go
#   ci/github-actions/terraform/plan-apply.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

name: Terraform Module Tests

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - "terraform/**"
      - ".github/workflows/terraform-module-test.yml"
  # Note 1: workflow_dispatch lets you trigger integration tests on demand
  # without waiting for a PR. Pass integration_test=true to run apply tests.
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      integration_test:
        description: "Run integration tests (creates real cloud resources)"
        required: false
        default: "false"
        type: choice
        options:
          - "false"
          - "true"

env:
  TF_VERSION: "1.10.0"         # <-- CHANGE THIS: keep in sync with plan-apply.yml
  GO_VERSION:  "1.22"          # <-- CHANGE THIS: Go version for Terratest

jobs:
  # =============================================
  # UNIT TESTS — native terraform test, no cloud creds needed
  # =============================================
  terraform-unit-test:
    name: Terraform Unit Tests (${{ matrix.module }})
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        # Note 2: Add one entry per module that has a tests/ directory.
        # Each entry maps to a subdirectory under terraform/
        module:
          - aws-eks        # <-- CHANGE THIS: list your modules
          # - azure-aks
          # - gcp-gke

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}

      - name: Terraform Init — ${{ matrix.module }}
        working-directory: terraform/${{ matrix.module }}
        run: terraform init -input=false

      - name: Terraform Test (unit) — ${{ matrix.module }}
        working-directory: terraform/${{ matrix.module }}
        # Note 3: `terraform test` discovers all *.tftest.hcl files in the
        # tests/ directory automatically. The -filter flag can target one file.
        run: terraform test
        env:
          # Note 4: mock_provider handles auth for unit tests, but some modules
          # still need these env vars to initialise remote state backends.
          TF_CLI_ARGS_init: "-backend=false"

  # =============================================
  # INTEGRATION TESTS — Terratest + Go, creates real resources
  # Only on workflow_dispatch with integration_test=true
  # =============================================
  terratest-integration:
    name: Terratest Integration Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch' && github.event.inputs.integration_test == 'true'
    environment: test-account   # <-- CHANGE THIS: GitHub environment for cloud test credentials
    permissions:
      id-token: write   # OIDC
      contents: read

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 5: Uncomment ONE cloud auth block — same pattern as plan-apply.yml.
      # - name: AWS Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
      #   with:
      #     role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_TEST_ROLE_ARN }}   # <-- CHANGE THIS
      #     aws-region: us-east-1                              # <-- CHANGE THIS

      # - name: Azure Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: azure/login@v2
      #   with:
      #     client-id:       ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
      #     tenant-id:       ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
      #     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      - name: Setup Go
        uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version: ${{ env.GO_VERSION }}
          cache-dependency-path: terraform/_testing/terratest/go.sum

      - name: Install Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}
          terraform_wrapper: false  # Note 6: Must be false for Terratest to capture output

      - name: Run Terratest
        working-directory: terraform/_testing/terratest
        # Note 7: -timeout 60m keeps the job alive during the ~25 min cluster creation.
        # -run selects only the integration tests (not the fast plan-only ones).
        run: go test -v -timeout 60m -run TestEKSCluster ./...
        env:
          AWS_DEFAULT_REGION: us-east-1   # <-- CHANGE THIS

      - name: Upload test results
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: terratest-results
          path: terraform/_testing/terratest/test-results.xml
          retention-days: 30
          if-no-files-found: ignore

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Terraform Plan / Apply
# WHEN TO USE: Automating Terraform workflows with PR-based plan review
# PREREQUISITES: Cloud credentials configured via OIDC or secrets
# SECRETS NEEDED: Cloud auth (see provider-specific sections below)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: terraform/azure-aks/, terraform/aws-eks/, terraform/gcp-gke/
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Terraform Plan / Apply

# Note 2: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
on:
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    branches: [main]
    # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    paths:
      # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - "terraform/**"    # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to your Terraform configs
  # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    branches: [main]
    # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    paths:
      # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - "terraform/**"    # <-- CHANGE THIS: match above

# Prevent concurrent Terraform runs against the same state
# Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
concurrency:
  # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  group: terraform-${{ github.ref }}
  # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  cancel-in-progress: false   # Never cancel in-progress Terraform applies

# Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
permissions:
  # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  contents: read
  # Note 16: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull-requests: write        # For posting plan output as PR comment
  # Note 17: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  id-token: write             # For OIDC authentication to cloud providers

# Note 18: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
env:
  # Note 19: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  TF_VERSION: "1.10.0"                      # <-- CHANGE THIS: pin to your version
  # Note 20: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  TF_WORKING_DIR: "terraform/azure-aks"      # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to root module
  # Note 21: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  TF_VAR_FILE: ""                            # <-- CHANGE THIS: e.g., "environments/prod.tfvars"

  # --- Azure (uncomment if using Azure) ---
  # ARM_CLIENT_ID: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
  # ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
  # ARM_TENANT_ID: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
  # ARM_USE_OIDC: true

  # --- AWS (uncomment if using AWS) ---
  # AWS_REGION: us-east-1

  # --- GCP (uncomment if using GCP) ---
  # GOOGLE_PROJECT: my-project-id

# Note 22: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  # =============================================
  # PLAN — runs on every PR
  # =============================================
  # Note 23: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  plan:
    # Note 24: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
    name: Terraform Plan
    # Note 25: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Note 26: Control flow should stay readable; predictable branches reduce defects and simplify troubleshooting.
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'

    # Note 27: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      # Note 28: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # --- Cloud Authentication (uncomment ONE) ---

      # Azure OIDC
      # - name: Azure Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: azure/login@v2
      #   with:
      #     client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
      #     tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
      #     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      # AWS OIDC
      # - name: AWS Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
      #   with:
      #     role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}
      #     aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}

      # GCP Workload Identity
      # - name: GCP Auth (Workload Identity)
      #   uses: google-github-actions/auth@v2
      #   with:
      #     workload_identity_provider: ${{ secrets.GCP_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_PROVIDER }}
      #     service_account: ${{ secrets.GCP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT }}

      # Note 29: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Setup Terraform
        # Note 30: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        # Note 31: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          # Note 32: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}
          # Note 33: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          terraform_wrapper: true   # Enables stdout capture for PR comments

      # Note 34: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Terraform Format Check
        # Note 35: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        id: fmt
        # Note 36: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        working-directory: ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}
        # Note 37: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: terraform fmt -check -diff
        # Note 38: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        continue-on-error: true

      # Note 39: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Terraform Init
        # Note 40: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        id: init
        # Note 41: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        working-directory: ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}
        # Note 42: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: terraform init -input=false

      # Note 43: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Terraform Validate
        # Note 44: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        id: validate
        # Note 45: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        working-directory: ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}
        # Note 46: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: terraform validate -no-color

      # Note 47: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Terraform Plan
        # Note 48: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        id: plan
        # Note 49: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        working-directory: ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}
        # Note 50: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: |
          PLAN_ARGS="-input=false -no-color -out=tfplan"
          if [ -n "${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}" ]; then
            PLAN_ARGS="${PLAN_ARGS} -var-file=${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}"
          fi
          terraform plan ${PLAN_ARGS}
        continue-on-error: true

      # Post plan output as a PR comment for review
      - name: Comment Plan on PR
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const output = `#### 🧹 Terraform Format: \`${{ steps.fmt.outcome }}\`
            #### ⚙️ Terraform Init: \`${{ steps.init.outcome }}\`
            #### ✅ Terraform Validate: \`${{ steps.validate.outcome }}\`
            #### 📋 Terraform Plan: \`${{ steps.plan.outcome }}\`

            <details><summary>Show Plan Output</summary>

            \`\`\`terraform
            ${{ steps.plan.outputs.stdout }}
            \`\`\`

            </details>

            *Pushed by: @${{ github.actor }}, Action: \`${{ github.event_name }}\`*`;

            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: output
            })

      - name: Fail if Plan Failed
        if: steps.plan.outcome == 'failure'
        run: exit 1

  # =============================================
  # APPLY — runs only on push to main (after PR merge)
  # =============================================
  apply:
    name: Terraform Apply
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    environment: production   # <-- CHANGE THIS: requires environment approval in GitHub settings

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # --- Cloud Authentication (uncomment ONE — same as plan job) ---
      # - name: Azure Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: azure/login@v2
      #   with:
      #     client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
      #     tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
      #     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      # - name: AWS Login (OIDC)
      #   uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
      #   with:
      #     role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}
      #     aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}

      # - name: GCP Auth (Workload Identity)
      #   uses: google-github-actions/auth@v2
      #   with:
      #     workload_identity_provider: ${{ secrets.GCP_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_PROVIDER }}
      #     service_account: ${{ secrets.GCP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT }}

      - name: Setup Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}

      - name: Terraform Init
        working-directory: ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}
        run: terraform init -input=false

      - name: Terraform Apply
        working-directory: ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}
        run: |
          APPLY_ARGS="-input=false -auto-approve"
          if [ -n "${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}" ]; then
            APPLY_ARGS="${APPLY_ARGS} -var-file=${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}"
          fi
          terraform apply ${APPLY_ARGS}

  # =============================================
  # INFRACOST — posts cloud cost diff on every PR
  # Requires: INFRACOST_API_KEY secret (free at infracost.io)
  # =============================================
  infracost:
    name: Cost Estimation (Infracost)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    permissions:
      contents:      read
      pull-requests: write   # Note 1: Required to post the cost comment on the PR

    steps:
      - name: Checkout PR branch
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Infracost
        uses: infracost/actions/setup@v3
        with:
          # Note 2: Get a free API key at https://www.infracost.io/docs/#2-get-api-key
          api-key: ${{ secrets.INFRACOST_API_KEY }}   # <-- CHANGE THIS: add to repo secrets

      # Note 3: We generate cost breakdowns for both the base branch and the PR
      # branch, then diff them so the comment shows only what CHANGED in this PR.
      - name: Generate cost breakdown — base branch
        run: |
          git fetch origin ${{ github.base_ref }}
          git checkout ${{ github.base_ref }}
          INFRACOST_ARGS="--path ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }} --format json --out-file /tmp/infracost-base.json"
          if [ -n "${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}" ]; then
            INFRACOST_ARGS="${INFRACOST_ARGS} --terraform-var-file ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}/${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}"
          fi
          infracost breakdown ${INFRACOST_ARGS}

      - name: Generate cost breakdown — PR branch
        run: |
          git checkout ${{ github.head_ref }}
          INFRACOST_ARGS="--path ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }} --format json --out-file /tmp/infracost-pr.json"
          if [ -n "${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}" ]; then
            INFRACOST_ARGS="${INFRACOST_ARGS} --terraform-var-file ${{ env.TF_WORKING_DIR }}/${{ env.TF_VAR_FILE }}"
          fi
          infracost breakdown ${INFRACOST_ARGS}

      - name: Post cost diff as PR comment
        # Note 4: --behavior=update replaces a previous Infracost comment rather than
        # posting a new one on each commit push, keeping PR threads clean.
        run: |
          infracost diff \
            --path /tmp/infracost-pr.json \
            --compare-to /tmp/infracost-base.json \
            --format github-comment \
            --github-token ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} \
            --pull-request ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} \
            --repo ${{ github.repository }} \
            --behavior update

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitLab CI — Reusable Docker Build (Kaniko)
# WHEN TO USE: Building Docker images in GitLab CI (works in rootless runners)
# PREREQUISITES: Dockerfile in the repository
# SECRETS NEEDED: CI_REGISTRY_* (automatic in GitLab)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Override DOCKERFILE and IMAGE_TAG variables
# RELATED FILES: ci/gitlab-ci/dotnet/.gitlab-ci.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Reusable Docker Build job — uses Kaniko (no Docker-in-Docker required)
# Note 1: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
.docker-build:
  stage: package
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  image:
    name: gcr.io/kaniko-project/executor:v1.23.2-debug
    # Note 3: Docker instructions are layer-based; ordering these commands intentionally can improve cache reuse and build speed.
    entrypoint: [""]
  variables:
    # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    DOCKERFILE: Dockerfile
    IMAGE_TAG: $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:$CI_COMMIT_SHORT_SHA
  # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  script:
    - |
      # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      /kaniko/executor \
        --context "$CI_PROJECT_DIR" \
        # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        --dockerfile "$CI_PROJECT_DIR/$DOCKERFILE" \
        --destination "$IMAGE_TAG" \
        # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        --destination "$CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:latest" \
        --cache=true \
        --cache-repo "$CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE/cache"
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == "main"

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitLab CI — Reusable Slack/Teams Notification
# WHEN TO USE: Post-pipeline notifications to messaging platforms
# PREREQUISITES: SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL variable configured in GitLab
# SECRETS NEEDED: SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Webhook URL and message format
# RELATED FILES: notifications/slack-notify.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Reusable Slack notification job
# Note 1: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
.notify:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  stage: .post
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  image: alpine:latest
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  script:
    # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    - apk add --no-cache curl
    # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    - |
      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      STATUS="${CI_JOB_STATUS:-unknown}"
      COLOR="good"
      if [ "$STATUS" != "success" ]; then COLOR="danger"; fi
      curl -X POST "$SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL" \
        -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
        -d "{\"text\":\"Pipeline $STATUS for $CI_PROJECT_NAME ($CI_COMMIT_REF_NAME)\"}"
  when: always

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitLab CI — Reusable SAST Scan (Semgrep)
# WHEN TO USE: Static analysis on any codebase
# PREREQUISITES: None
# SECRETS NEEDED: None (optional: SEMGREP_APP_TOKEN for Semgrep Cloud)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Semgrep config rules if needed
# RELATED FILES: security/sast/semgrep.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Reusable SAST scan — Semgrep
# Note 1: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
.sast-scan:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  stage: scan
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  image: returntocorp/semgrep:latest
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  script:
    # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    - semgrep --config auto --json --output semgrep-report.json . || true
  # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - semgrep-report.json
    when: always

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitLab CI — Dynamic Pipeline Generation
# WHEN TO USE: Generate child pipeline YAML at runtime based on changed files
# PREREQUISITES: Python script at scripts/generate-pipeline.py
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Script path and generated pipeline structure
# RELATED FILES: ci/gitlab-ci/_strategies/parent-child-pipeline.yml
# MATURITY: Beta
# ============================================================

# Dynamic Pipeline — generate child pipeline YAML at runtime
# Note 1: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
stages:
  - generate
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  - trigger

generate-pipeline:
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  stage: generate
  image: python:3.12-slim
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  script:
    - python scripts/generate-pipeline.py > generated-pipeline.yml
  # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  artifacts:
    paths:
      # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - generated-pipeline.yml

trigger-generated:
  # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  stage: trigger
  trigger:
    # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    include:
      - artifact: generated-pipeline.yml
        job: generate-pipeline
    strategy: depend

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitLab CI — Parent-Child Pipeline (Monorepo)
# WHEN TO USE: Monorepos where each service has its own .gitlab-ci.yml
# PREREQUISITES: Per-service .gitlab-ci.yml files
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Service paths and pipeline includes
# RELATED FILES: ci/gitlab-ci/_strategies/dynamic-pipeline.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Parent-Child Pipeline example
# The parent triggers child pipelines per service
# Note 1: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
stages:
  - trigger

# Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
trigger-user-service:
  stage: trigger
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  trigger:
    include: services/user-service/.gitlab-ci.yml
    # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    strategy: depend
  rules:
    # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    - changes:
        - services/user-service/**

# Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
trigger-order-service:
  stage: trigger
  # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  trigger:
    include: services/order-service/.gitlab-ci.yml
    # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    strategy: depend
  rules:
    # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    - changes:
        - services/order-service/**

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Docker Compose — Annotated Base Template
# WHEN TO USE: Starting point for any new local dev environment
# PREREQUISITES: Docker and Docker Compose v2+
# SECRETS NEEDED: None (uses .env file)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Adapt services, ports, and volumes to your stack
# RELATED FILES: compose/dotnet-sqlserver/, compose/python-postgres-redis/
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================
#
# NOTE: The top-level 'version:' key is obsolete in Docker Compose v2+
# and has been intentionally omitted. See:
# https://docs.docker.com/compose/compose-file/04-version-and-name/

# Extension fields: define anchors to reuse across services
# Note 1: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
x-logging: &default-logging
  driver: "json-file"
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  options:
    max-size: "10m"
    # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    max-file: "3"

x-restart-policy: &restart-policy
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  restart: unless-stopped

services:
  # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  app:
    # Build from local Dockerfile
    build:
      # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      context: .          # Build context (directory sent to Docker daemon)
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      args:
        - BUILD_ENV=development  # ARGs available during build only
      # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      target: runtime     # Stop at a specific multi-stage target

    # Or pull a pre-built image:
    # image: myregistry.io/my-app:1.2.3

    container_name: my-app  # Avoid auto-generated names in dev

    # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"     # host:container — only expose what you need

    # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=development
      # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - DB_HOST=db      # Container names resolve as DNS on the same network

    # Load from .env file (never commit secrets)
    env_file:
      # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - .env

    volumes:
      # Named volume: managed by Docker, persists between restarts
      # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - app-data:/app/data
      # Bind mount: reflects local file changes instantly (great for dev)
      - ./src:/app/src:ro   # :ro = read-only inside container

    # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    depends_on:
      db:
        # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        condition: service_healthy  # Wait for healthcheck to pass

    healthcheck:
      # Note 16: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-qO-", "http://localhost:3000/health"]
      interval: 30s
      # Note 17: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      # Note 18: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      start_period: 20s  # Grace period before first check

    networks:
      # Note 19: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - app-net

    logging: *default-logging
    # Note 20: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    <<: *restart-policy

  db:
    # Note 21: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      # Note 22: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - POSTGRES_USER=${DB_USER:-appuser}
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=${DB_PASSWORD:?DB_PASSWORD must be set}  # Fails if not set
      # Note 23: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - POSTGRES_DB=${DB_NAME:-appdb}
    volumes:
      # Note 24: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - db-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./db/init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql:ro  # Run on first start
    # Note 25: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U ${DB_USER:-appuser}"]
      # Note 26: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
    networks:
      - app-net
    logging: *default-logging
    <<: *restart-policy

volumes:
  app-data:     # Persists across container restarts
  db-data:

networks:
  app-net:
    driver: bridge
    # For external network (e.g., shared across compose files):
    # external: true

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Docker Compose — .NET API + SQL Server + Seq
# WHEN TO USE: Local development for .NET applications with SQL Server
# PREREQUISITES: Docker and Docker Compose v2+
# SECRETS NEEDED: SQL_PASSWORD (in .env file)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: docker/dotnet/Dockerfile.api
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# .NET API + SQL Server + Seq (structured logging UI) — Local Dev Stack

# Note 1: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
services:
  api:
    # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    build:
      context: ../../
      # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      dockerfile: docker/dotnet/Dockerfile.api               # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to your Dockerfile
    ports:
      # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - "5000:8080"                                          # <-- CHANGE THIS: host:container port
    environment:
      # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development
      - ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection=Server=sqlserver;Database=AppDb;User Id=sa;Password=${SQL_PASSWORD};TrustServerCertificate=True
      # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - Serilog__WriteTo__Seq__ServerUrl=http://seq:5341
    depends_on:
      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      sqlserver:
        condition: service_healthy
    # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    networks:
      - app-net

  # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  sqlserver:
    image: mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server:2022-latest
    # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    environment:
      - ACCEPT_EULA=Y
      # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - SA_PASSWORD=${SQL_PASSWORD:-Dev@Password123!}
    ports:
      # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - "1433:1433"
    volumes:
      # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - sqlserver-data:/var/opt/mssql
    healthcheck:
      # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      test: /opt/mssql-tools18/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost -U sa -P "$$SA_PASSWORD" -C -Q "SELECT 1" || exit 1
      interval: 15s
      # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
      # Note 16: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      start_period: 30s
    networks:
      # Note 17: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - app-net

  # Seq — structured logging UI (view .NET Serilog logs)
  seq:
    image: datalust/seq:latest
    environment:
      - ACCEPT_EULA=Y
    ports:
      - "5341:5341"   # Ingestion API
      - "8081:80"     # Web UI — open http://localhost:8081
    volumes:
      - seq-data:/data
    networks:
      - app-net

volumes:
  sqlserver-data:
  seq-data:

networks:
  app-net:
    driver: bridge

# Copy this file to .env before running docker compose.
# Keep the Spring datasource credentials aligned with the PostgreSQL service credentials.

SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME=postgres
SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD=devpassword

POSTGRES_DB=appdb
POSTGRES_USER=postgres
POSTGRES_PASSWORD=devpassword

PGADMIN_DEFAULT_EMAIL=admin@example.com
PGADMIN_DEFAULT_PASSWORD=admin123

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Docker Compose Override — Spring Boot + pgAdmin
# WHEN TO USE: Local development when you also need a PostgreSQL admin UI
# PREREQUISITES: Docker and Docker Compose v2+
# SECRETS NEEDED: PGADMIN_DEFAULT_EMAIL, PGADMIN_DEFAULT_PASSWORD (in .env file)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: compose/java-postgres/docker-compose.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# pgAdmin debug override — combine with docker-compose.yml for local DB inspection

services:
  pgadmin:
    image: dpage/pgadmin4:latest
    ports:
      - "5050:80"
    environment:
      - PGADMIN_DEFAULT_EMAIL=${PGADMIN_DEFAULT_EMAIL}

    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      - app-net

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Docker Compose — Spring Boot + PostgreSQL
# WHEN TO USE: Local development for Spring Boot applications with PostgreSQL
# PREREQUISITES: Docker and Docker Compose v2+
# SECRETS NEEDED: SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME, SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD, POSTGRES_DB, POSTGRES_USER, POSTGRES_PASSWORD (in .env file)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked with # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: docker/java/Dockerfile.gradle
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Spring Boot API + PostgreSQL — Local Dev Stack

services:
  api:
    build:
      context: ../../
      dockerfile: docker/java/Dockerfile.gradle
      target: build
    command: gradle bootRun --no-daemon
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    environment:
      - SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL=jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/${POSTGRES_DB}
      - SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME=${SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME}
      - SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD=${SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD}
      - SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=local
      - JAVA_OPTS=-Xmx512m
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:8080/actuator/health || exit 1"]
      interval: 15s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 45s
    volumes:
      - ../../src/main:/app/src/main   # <-- CHANGE THIS if your Spring Boot sources live elsewhere
    networks:
      - app-net

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_DB=${POSTGRES_DB}
      - POSTGRES_USER=${POSTGRES_USER}
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U $$POSTGRES_USER -d $$POSTGRES_DB"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 10s
    networks:
      - app-net

volumes:
  postgres-data:

networks:
  app-net:
    driver: bridge

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Docker Compose — Microservices Example
# WHEN TO USE: Learning reference for multi-service local dev
# PREREQUISITES: Docker and Docker Compose v2+
# SECRETS NEEDED: None (dev-only passwords)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Service build contexts and environment variables
# RELATED FILES: compose/_templates/docker-compose.base.yml
# MATURITY: Beta
# ============================================================

# Microservices Example — Local Dev Stack
# Demonstrates: shared network, service discovery, API gateway pattern

# Note 1: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
services:
  # API Gateway / reverse proxy
  gateway:
    image: nginx:1.27-alpine
    # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    ports:
      - "8080:80"
    volumes:
      # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - ./gateway/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf:ro
    depends_on:
      - user-service
      # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - order-service
    networks:
      - app-net

  # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  user-service:
    build:
      context: ./user-service
    # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:devpassword@postgres:5432/usersdb
    depends_on:
      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - app-net

  order-service:
    build:
      # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      context: ./order-service
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:devpassword@postgres:5432/ordersdb
      # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - MESSAGE_BROKER=amqp://guest:guest@rabbitmq:5672/
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        condition: service_healthy
      rabbitmq:
        condition: service_healthy
    # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    networks:
      - app-net

  postgres:
    # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_USER=postgres
      # Note 14: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=devpassword
      - POSTGRES_MULTIPLE_DATABASES=usersdb,ordersdb
    volumes:
      # Note 15: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres"]
      # Note 16: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
    # Note 17: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    networks:
      - app-net

  rabbitmq:
    # Note 18: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    image: rabbitmq:3.13-management-alpine
    ports:
      - "15672:15672"  # management UI
    # Note 19: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    healthcheck:
      test: rabbitmq-diagnostics -q ping
      interval: 15s
      # Note 20: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 5
    networks:
      # Note 21: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - app-net

volumes:
  postgres-data:

# Note 22: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
networks:
  app-net:
    driver: bridge

# Python API + PostgreSQL + Redis — Local Dev Stack

# Note 1: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
services:
  api:
    build:
      # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

      dockerfile: docker/python/Dockerfile.fastapi
    ports:
      # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - "8000:8000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:-devpassword}@postgres:5432/appdb
      # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379/0
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        # Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
    # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    volumes:
      - ./app:/app   # hot reload in dev
    networks:
      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - app-net

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

      - POSTGRES_DB=appdb
      - POSTGRES_USER=postgres
      # Note 9: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:-devpassword}
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    volumes:
      - postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      retries: 5
    networks:
      - app-net

  # Note 13: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
