              runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/loki/oomkilleddetected # <-- CHANGE THIS

          # ── PanicDetected ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
          # Fires immediately (for: 0m) when any production log line indicates a
          # fatal runtime error.  Covered patterns:
          #   - "panic:"         Go runtime panics and application-level panic() calls
          #   - "SIGSEGV"        Segmentation fault — memory safety violation in native code
          #   - "fatal error:"   Go runtime fatal errors (e.g. "concurrent map writes")
          # These indicate a process crash or imminent crash and always warrant
          # immediate investigation.
          - alert: PanicDetected
            expr: |
              sum(
                count_over_time(
                  {namespace="production"}
                    |~ "(?i)(panic:|SIGSEGV|fatal error:)"
                  [5m]
                )
              ) by (namespace, app, pod) > 0
            for: 0m
            labels:
              severity: critical
              ruler: loki
            annotations:
              summary: >-
                Panic or fatal error detected in {{ $labels.app }}
                (pod: {{ $labels.pod }}, namespace: {{ $labels.namespace }})
              description: >-
                A log line matching "panic:", "SIGSEGV", or "fatal error:" was
                found in pod {{ $labels.pod }} (app: {{ $labels.app }}, namespace:
                {{ $labels.namespace }}). The process has crashed or is in an
                unrecoverable state. Collect a stack trace immediately:
                  kubectl logs {{ $labels.pod }} -n {{ $labels.namespace }} --previous
              runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/loki/panicdetected # <-- CHANGE THIS

# loki-stack chart values (grafana/loki-stack 2.10.x)
# Installs both Loki and Promtail from a single values file.
#
# Install:
#   helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts
#   helm install loki grafana/loki-stack --version 2.10.2 -n monitoring -f values.yaml
#
# Single binary (monolithic) mode is used here.  It runs all Loki components in one process
# and is appropriate for clusters producing up to roughly 200 GB of logs per day.
# For higher volumes, use the standalone loki chart in distributed mode:
# https://grafana.com/docs/loki/latest/setup/install/helm/

# ─── Loki ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

loki:
  enabled: true

  config:
    auth_enabled: false # Single-tenant mode. Set true and pass X-Scope-OrgID headers for multi-tenancy.

    server:
      http_listen_port: 3100
      grpc_listen_port: 9095

    common:
      path_prefix: /var/loki
      replication_factor: 1 # Single-binary needs only one replica; raise for HA deployments.
      storage:
        # Filesystem storage is simple and requires no external dependencies.
        # For production durability, switch to an object storage backend:
        #   S3:    type: s3    — add bucketnames, region, endpoint
        #   GCS:   type: gcs   — add bucket_name
        #   Azure: type: azure — add account_name, account_key, container
        # <-- CHANGE THIS to object storage before running in production
        filesystem:
          chunks_directory: /var/loki/chunks
          rules_directory: /var/loki/rules

    schema_config:
      configs:
        - from: "2024-01-01"
          store: tsdb           # TSDB is the recommended index type for Loki 2.8+.
          object_store: filesystem # <-- CHANGE THIS to match your storage backend.
          schema: v13
          index:
            prefix: index_
            period: 24h

    ingester:
      # WAL persists un-flushed chunks to disk so in-flight data survives a pod restart.
      wal:
        enabled: true
        dir: /var/loki/wal
      lifecycler:
        ring:
          kvstore:
            store: inmemory
          replication_factor: 1
      chunk_encoding: snappy    # Snappy gives fast compression with moderate ratio — good default.
      chunk_target_size: 1048576 # 1 MiB target keeps chunks predictably sized for filesystem storage.
      chunk_idle_period: 30m    # Flush idle chunks after 30 minutes to bound in-memory usage.
      max_chunk_age: 1h         # Force-flush any chunk older than 1 hour regardless of idle state.

    compactor:
      working_directory: /var/loki/compactor
      # retention_enabled must be true in the compactor to honour retention_period below.
      retention_enabled: true
      retention_delete_delay: 2h    # Wait 2 hours after the retention window before deleting chunks.
      retention_delete_worker_count: 150

    limits_config:
      # 30-day retention matches the Prometheus retention set in observability/prometheus/values.yaml
      # so incident timelines stay consistent across metrics and logs.
      retention_period: 720h         # 720h = exactly 30 days.
      ingestion_rate_mb: 16          # Per-tenant ingestion ceiling in MB/s.
      ingestion_burst_size_mb: 32    # Burst allowance above the sustained rate.
      # max_query_length adds a 1-hour buffer beyond the 30-day retention window so range
      # queries ending exactly at "now - 30d" do not hit the boundary mid-request.
      max_query_length: 721h
      max_streams_per_user: 10000    # Prevents a noisy namespace from exhausting the ingester.
      per_stream_rate_limit: 3MB
      per_stream_rate_limit_burst: 15MB
      reject_old_samples: true
      reject_old_samples_max_age: 168h # Drop lines older than 7 days at ingest time.

    query_range:
      split_queries_by_interval: 24h # Split long queries into 1-day chunks to bound fan-out.
      cache_results: true

    storage_config:
      tsdb_shipper:
        active_index_directory: /var/loki/index
        cache_location: /var/loki/index_cache
        cache_ttl: 24h

    chunk_store_config:
      chunk_cache_config:
        embedded_cache:
          enabled: true
          max_size_mb: 500 # In-process cache reduces repeated storage reads under normal query load.

  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: 500m
      memory: 1Gi
    limits:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: 4Gi # 4 GiB ceiling absorbs query spikes without OOM-killing on a mid-size cluster.

  persistence:
    enabled: true
    size: 50Gi # Sized to hold 30 days of compressed logs for a cluster with moderate log volume.
    # storageClassName: "" # <-- CHANGE THIS to an SSD-backed storage class in production

  podLabels:
    app: loki
    monitoring: "true" # Opt in to the additionalScrapeConfigs job in prometheus/values.yaml.

# ─── Promtail ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

promtail:
  enabled: true

  # Promtail runs as a DaemonSet (one pod per node). Requests are intentionally low because
  # Promtail spends most of its time blocked on inotify file events rather than doing CPU work.
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: 50m    # Minimal CPU; file-tail work is almost entirely I/O-wait, not CPU.
      memory: 64Mi
    limits:
      cpu: 200m
      memory: 256Mi # Cap memory so Promtail cannot crowd out application pods on a busy node.

  config:
    clients:
      - url: http://loki:3100/loki/api/v1/push # Points to the Loki service in the same namespace.

    snippets:
      # extraScrapeConfigs appends to the default Kubernetes pod scrape job rather than replacing
      # it, so the default CRI log format stage and label relabelling are preserved.
      extraScrapeConfigs: |
        # Collect system logs from each node (/var/log/*.log covers kubelet, kernel, systemd).
        - job_name: system
          static_configs:
            - targets:
                - localhost
              labels:
                job: varlogs
                __path__: /var/log/*.log
          pipeline_stages:
            - regex:
                # Parse syslog-style "Mon DD HH:MM:SS host process: message" lines.
                expression: '^(?P<timestamp>\w+\s+\d+ \d+:\d+:\d+) (?P<host>\S+) (?P<process>\S+): (?P<message>.*)$'
            - labels:
                host:
                process:

      pipelineStages:
        # Stage 1: strip the CRI prefix (timestamp, stream, flags) added by containerd/CRI-O.
        - cri: {}

        # Stage 2: try to parse the log line as JSON.
        # Covers FastAPI / uvicorn structured logs and any Python app using python-json-logger or structlog.
        # Fields that do not exist in a line are silently ignored, so this stage is safe to run globally.
        - json:
            expressions:
              level: level       # Severity used by structlog, python-json-logger, and the OTel SDK.
              time: time         # ISO-8601 timestamp emitted by uvicorn's JSON access log formatter.
              message: message   # Primary log message body.
              trace_id: trace_id # OTel trace correlation ID (populated when the OTel sidecar is running).
              logger: logger     # Logger name, useful for filtering by component within an application.

        # Stage 3: for plain-text log lines (no JSON), extract level via regex.
        # Handles .NET (ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT), Java (Logback/Log4j), and generic bracketed formats.
        - match:
            # Select lines that did not produce a level from the JSON stage above.
            selector: '{app=~".+"} |~ "^[^{]"'
            stages:
              - regex:
                  # Matches formats:
                  #   .NET:    "2024-01-01 12:00:00.000 +00:00 [INF] Message"
                  #   Java:    "2024-01-01 12:00:00.000  INFO --- [main] Message"
                  #   Generic: "INFO: Message" or "[ERROR] Message"
                  expression: '(?i)(?:^|\s)\[?(?P<level>TRACE|DEBUG|INFO|WARN(?:ING)?|ERROR|CRITICAL|FATAL)\]?[\s:]'
              - labels:
                  level:

        # Stage 4: parse nginx access log format into structured fields.
        # The selector targets both standalone nginx containers and the ingress-nginx pods.
        - match:
            selector: '{app=~"nginx|ingress.*"}'
            stages:
              - regex:
                  expression: >-
                    ^(?P<remote_addr>\S+) - (?P<remote_user>\S+) \[(?P<time_local>[^\]]+)\]
                    "(?P<method>\S+) (?P<path>\S+) (?P<protocol>\S+)"
                    (?P<status>\d+) (?P<body_bytes_sent>\d+)
                    "(?P<http_referer>[^"]*)" "(?P<http_user_agent>[^"]*)"
              - labels:
                  method:
                  status:

        # Stage 5: normalise the level label to lowercase so LogQL label matchers work
        # consistently regardless of whether the app emits "INFO", "Info", or "info".
        - template:
            source: level
            template: '{{ ToLower .Value }}'

        # Stage 6: promote level and trace_id to indexed Loki stream labels.
        # Keep the label count small — each unique label combination creates a new stream.
        # namespace, pod, container, and node are added by the default Kubernetes SD relabelling.
        - labels:
            level:
            trace_id: # Promote so log panels can link to Tempo/Jaeger traces via the derived field.

        # Stage 7: drop debug-level lines in production namespaces.
        # Debug logs are typically 3-5x higher volume than info+warn+error combined, so dropping
        # them at collection time significantly reduces ingestion cost and storage pressure.
        - match:
            selector: '{namespace=~"production|prod"}'
            stages:
              - drop:
                  expression: '(?i)level=debug|"level":"debug"|\[debug\]'
                  drop_counter_reason: debug_dropped_in_production

  # Mount the node's /var/log directory so Promtail can collect system logs.
  extraVolumes:
    - name: varlog
      hostPath:
        path: /var/log

  extraVolumeMounts:
    - name: varlog
      mountPath: /var/log
      readOnly: true

  tolerations:
    # Run Promtail on control-plane nodes too so system logs are never missed.
    - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
      operator: Exists
      effect: NoSchedule

  serviceMonitor:
    # Expose Promtail's own metrics to Prometheus so scrape lag, drop counts, and
    # pipeline errors are visible without manual scrape configuration.
    enabled: true
    labels:
      monitoring: "true"

# ─── Loki Ruler ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#
# The ruler evaluates LogQL alert rules on a schedule and forwards firing alerts
# to Alertmanager, where they appear alongside Prometheus alerts on the same
# routing tree.
#
# Prerequisites:
#   1. Apply the rules ConfigMap first (or in the same Helm sync):
#        kubectl apply -f observability/loki/loki-ruler-alerts.yaml
#   2. The ConfigMap is mounted at /var/loki/rules/fake/ — "fake" is the synthetic
#      tenant name used in single-tenant mode (auth_enabled=false).
#   3. Set alertmanager_url below to match your Alertmanager service FQDN.
#
# This block extends loki.config defined earlier in this file. The Helm chart
# deep-merges the config keys, so only the new ruler section is added here.
loki:
  config:
    ruler:
      # Store rule files on the local filesystem, read from the mounted ConfigMap.
      # For distributed/microservices mode, switch to an object storage backend:
      #   storage: { type: s3, s3: { ... } }
      storage:
        type: local
        local:
          # This path is also used by common.storage.filesystem.rules_directory above.
          # The sub-directory "fake" is Loki's default org ID for single-tenant deployments.
          directory: /var/loki/rules

      # <-- CHANGE THIS to the Alertmanager service in your monitoring namespace.
      alertmanager_url: http://kube-prometheus-stack-alertmanager.monitoring:9093

      # Send alerts to Alertmanager even when only one Loki replica is running.
      enable_alertmanager_v2: true

      # Poll the rules directory every minute for configuration changes.
      # Lower values mean faster rule propagation; the minimum supported is 15s.
      poll_interval: 1m

      # Enable the remote-write path so ruler evaluation results can also be written
      # back to Prometheus as recording rules (set to false if not needed).
      enable_api: true

  # Mount the loki-ruler-config ConfigMap into the Loki pod so the ruler can read
  # the alert rules without requiring a pod restart on rule changes.
  extraVolumes:
    - name: loki-ruler-rules
      configMap:
        name: loki-ruler-config

  extraVolumeMounts:
    - name: loki-ruler-rules
      # /var/loki/rules/fake/ — "fake" is the single-tenant org ID.
      mountPath: /var/loki/rules/fake
      readOnly: true

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: otel-collector-config
  namespace: default  # <-- CHANGE THIS to match the namespace of the Deployment being patched
data:
  config.yaml: |
    # ─── Receivers ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    receivers:
      otlp:
        protocols:
          grpc:
            # Port 4317 — preferred SDK transport. Apps inject OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT
            # pointing to localhost:4317 because the collector runs as a sidecar.
            endpoint: "0.0.0.0:4317"
          http:
            # Port 4318 — HTTP fallback for SDKs or proxies that cannot use gRPC.
            endpoint: "0.0.0.0:4318"

      prometheus:
        config:
          scrape_configs:
            # Self-scrape: collect the collector's own pipeline metrics (spans received,
            # export failures, queue depth) and forward them to Prometheus.
            - job_name: otel-collector-self
              static_configs:
                - targets: ["localhost:8888"]

    # ─── Processors ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    processors:
      memory_limiter:
        # Prevent the collector from OOM-killing the pod by refusing new data when
        # memory usage approaches the container limit. Set to 80% of the 512Mi limit.
        # This must be the FIRST processor in every pipeline.
        check_interval: 1s
        limit_mib: 409      # 80% of the 512Mi container limit (512 * 0.8 = 409).
        spike_limit_mib: 128 # Allow a short burst above limit before hard-rejecting.

      batch:
        # Batching is critical for export performance. Without it, every span triggers
        # a separate RPC call to the backend, which saturates connections and inflates
        # per-span cost. With batching, the collector amortises connection overhead
        # across many spans in a single request.
        timeout: 5s         # Wait up to 5 seconds to fill a batch before flushing.
        send_batch_size: 1000     # Flush when 1000 items are queued, even before the timeout.
        send_batch_max_size: 1500 # Hard ceiling per batch to bound individual request size.

      resource:
        # Stamp every span and metric with cluster-level attributes sourced from the
        # Downward API env vars injected by the sidecar patch. This means the application
        # SDK does not need to set these attributes itself.
        attributes:
          - key: k8s.cluster.name
            # <-- CHANGE THIS: set a static value matching your cluster name, or inject
            # it via an additional env var in collector-sidecar.yaml
            value: "my-cluster"
            action: upsert
          - key: k8s.namespace.name
            value: "${K8S_NAMESPACE}"   # Injected by the Downward API in the sidecar patch.
            action: upsert
          - key: k8s.pod.name
            value: "${K8S_POD_NAME}"    # Injected by the Downward API in the sidecar patch.
            action: upsert
          - key: k8s.node.name
            value: "${K8S_NODE_NAME}"   # Injected by the Downward API in the sidecar patch.
            action: upsert

      filter/exclude_health:
        # Drop traces generated by Kubernetes liveness and readiness probes.
        # These are high-frequency, low-value requests that would inflate trace volume
        # and obscure meaningful latency distributions in dashboards.
        # The paths match the probe endpoints defined in cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml.
        traces:
          span:
            - 'attributes["http.target"] == "/health"'
            - 'attributes["http.target"] == "/health/ready"'
            - 'attributes["http.target"] == "/health/live"'
            - 'attributes["url.path"] == "/health"'
            - 'attributes["url.path"] == "/health/ready"'
            - 'attributes["url.path"] == "/health/live"'

    # ─── Exporters ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    exporters:
      otlp/tempo:
        # Export traces to Tempo using OTLP gRPC.
        # Set TEMPO_ENDPOINT in the sidecar env to the in-cluster Tempo gRPC address.
        # <-- CHANGE THIS: default assumes Tempo is installed in the monitoring namespace
        endpoint: "${TEMPO_ENDPOINT}"
        tls:
          insecure: true  # Set false and configure cert paths when Tempo is behind TLS.

      prometheusremotewrite:
        # Push collector self-metrics and any OTLP metrics to Prometheus via remote-write.
        # Set PROMETHEUS_REMOTE_WRITE_URL in the sidecar env.
        # <-- CHANGE THIS: default assumes kube-prometheus-stack in the monitoring namespace
        endpoint: "${PROMETHEUS_REMOTE_WRITE_URL}"
        tls:
          insecure_skip_verify: false  # <-- CHANGE THIS to false and supply a CA cert in production

      debug:
        # The debug exporter prints spans to the collector's stdout. It is set to "basic"
        # verbosity (one summary line per batch) so it can remain in the pipeline without
        # flooding logs. To inspect individual spans during troubleshooting, change
        # verbosity to "detailed" and redeploy, then revert before committing.
        verbosity: basic

    # ─── Extensions ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    extensions:
      health_check:
        # Exposes /ready and /live on port 13133 for the readiness probe in the sidecar patch.
        endpoint: "0.0.0.0:13133"
      pprof:
        # Go pprof profiling endpoint, useful when diagnosing collector CPU spikes.
        endpoint: "localhost:1777"

    # ─── Service ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    service:
      extensions: [health_check, pprof]
      pipelines:
        traces:
          # Order matters: memory_limiter must be first to protect the process,
          # resource stamps attributes, filter removes noise, batch amortises export cost.
          receivers:  [otlp]
          processors: [memory_limiter, resource, filter/exclude_health, batch]
          exporters:  [otlp/tempo, debug]

        metrics:
          receivers:  [otlp, prometheus]
          processors: [memory_limiter, batch]
          exporters:  [prometheusremotewrite, debug]

      telemetry:
        logs:
          # "warn" keeps the collector's own log output quiet in production.
          # Switch to "debug" temporarily when diagnosing pipeline issues.
          level: warn
        metrics:
          address: "0.0.0.0:8888"

# Add this sidecar to any Deployment that needs distributed tracing.
#
# This is a strategic merge patch. Apply it to an existing Deployment with:
#
#   kubectl patch deployment <name> -n <namespace> \
#     --patch-file observability/opentelemetry/collector-sidecar.yaml
#
# Or add it as a Kustomize patchesStrategicMerge entry:
#
#   patchesStrategicMerge:
#     - ../../observability/opentelemetry/collector-sidecar.yaml
#
# The patch merges by container name ("otel-collector"), so it is safe to apply
# repeatedly and will not duplicate the sidecar on subsequent patches.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   1. Apply the collector ConfigMap first:
#        kubectl apply -f observability/opentelemetry/collector-config.yaml
#   2. Set TEMPO_ENDPOINT and PROMETHEUS_REMOTE_WRITE_URL in the ConfigMap or
#      as Kubernetes Secret values before the pod starts.
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: app  # <-- CHANGE THIS to match the target Deployment name
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: otel-collector
          # Pin to a specific version. The -contrib image includes all receiver/exporter
          # plugins including the Kubernetes attributes and Prometheus remote-write exporters.
          # Check https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-collector-releases for updates.
          # Review and bump this version quarterly to pick up security patches and new features.
          image: otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib:0.91.0
          args:
            - "--config=/etc/otel/config.yaml"
          ports:
            # OTLP gRPC — the preferred transport for SDK-to-collector communication.
            # Lower overhead than HTTP for high-throughput trace export.
            - name: otlp-grpc
              containerPort: 4317
              protocol: TCP
            # OTLP HTTP — used by SDKs or proxies that cannot speak gRPC (e.g. browser agents).
            - name: otlp-http
              containerPort: 4318
              protocol: TCP
            # Collector self-metrics scraped by Prometheus via the monitoring: "true" label.
            - name: metrics
              containerPort: 8888
              protocol: TCP
            # Health check extension — used by the readiness probe below.
            - name: health
              containerPort: 13133
              protocol: TCP
          env:
            # Downward API values injected so the resource processor can stamp every span
            # and metric with the pod's own namespace and name without hard-coding them.
            - name: K8S_NAMESPACE
              valueFrom:
                fieldRef:
                  fieldPath: metadata.namespace
            - name: K8S_POD_NAME
              valueFrom:
                fieldRef:
                  fieldPath: metadata.name
            - name: K8S_NODE_NAME
              valueFrom:
                fieldRef:
                  fieldPath: spec.nodeName
            # Tempo endpoint — inject via a Kubernetes Secret or ConfigMap.
            # See docs/guides/secrets-management.md for the recommended approach.
            - name: TEMPO_ENDPOINT
              value: "http://tempo:4317"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
            # Prometheus remote-write URL — set to the in-cluster Prometheus endpoint.
            - name: PROMETHEUS_REMOTE_WRITE_URL
              value: "http://kube-prometheus-stack-prometheus.monitoring.svc:9090/api/v1/write"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          readinessProbe:
            # The health check extension exposes /ready on port 13133.
            # Kubernetes waits for this before routing traffic or marking the pod Ready,
            # which prevents the app container from sending spans before the collector is up.
            httpGet:
              path: /
              port: 13133
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          resources:
            requests:
              # 100m CPU is enough for the collector's normal serialisation and batching work
              # on a pod producing a few hundred spans per second.
              cpu: 100m
              # 128Mi covers the batch processor's in-memory queue at the default 1000-item limit.
              memory: 128Mi
            limits:
              # 500m CPU headroom absorbs bursts when many spans arrive simultaneously.
              cpu: 500m
              # 512Mi limit is the ceiling the memory_limiter processor is tuned to (80% = 409Mi).
              # If the collector is regularly hitting this, increase both the limit and memory_limiter.
              memory: 512Mi
          securityContext:
            # Mirror the base deployment's security posture so the sidecar does not weaken
            # the pod's overall security profile.
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
            runAsNonRoot: true
            runAsUser: 10001  # otel-collector-contrib uses UID 10001 in its official image
            capabilities:
              drop: ["ALL"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: otel-collector-config
              mountPath: /etc/otel
              readOnly: true
            # Writable temp space so the collector can buffer spans during transient export failures.
            - name: otel-tmp
              mountPath: /tmp
      volumes:
        - name: otel-collector-config
          configMap:
            name: otel-collector-config  # Created by collector-config.yaml in this directory.
        - name: otel-tmp
          emptyDir: {}

# OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation environment variables for .NET (ASP.NET Core 6+)
#
# INSTALLATION:
# Add the following NuGet packages to your project:
#
#   dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting
#   dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Instrumentation.AspNetCore
#   dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Instrumentation.Http
#   dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Exporter.OpenTelemetryProtocol
#
# Then register tracing in Program.cs:
#
#   builder.Services.AddOpenTelemetry()
#       .WithTracing(b => b
#           .AddAspNetCoreInstrumentation()
#           .AddHttpClientInstrumentation()
#           .AddOtlpExporter())
#       .WithMetrics(b => b
#           .AddAspNetCoreInstrumentation()
#           .AddOtlpExporter());
#
# Alternatively, use the OpenTelemetry .NET Auto-Instrumentation agent (zero-code):
#   https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-dotnet-instrumentation
#
# USAGE:
# Kubernetes — add to your Deployment env section:
#   envFrom:
#     - configMapRef:
#         name: otel-dotnet-env
# Docker Compose — add to your service:
#   env_file:
#     - observability/opentelemetry/env-vars/dotnet.env
#
OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=my-dotnet-service
# OTEL_SERVICE_NAME: uniquely identifies this service in traces and metrics.
# <-- CHANGE THIS to your service name (e.g. "order-api", "payment-worker")

OTEL_SERVICE_VERSION=1.0.0
# <-- CHANGE THIS to your deployed version, ideally injected by CI from the image tag

OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:4317
# Points to the OTel collector sidecar running in the same pod on port 4317 (gRPC).

OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL=grpc
# grpc is the preferred transport: lower overhead and supports streaming compared to http/protobuf.

OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER=parentbased_traceidratio
# parentbased_traceidratio respects the sampling decision of the parent span when one exists
# (e.g. an upstream service), and applies the ratio below for root spans it originates.

OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG=0.1
# 10% sampling in production balances observability coverage against storage cost.
# A busy service producing 1000 req/s at 10% yields 100 stored traces per second,
# which is sufficient to detect latency regressions and error patterns.
# Set to 1.0 in dev/staging so every request is traced for easier debugging.
# Override per environment via values.dev.yaml / values.prod.yaml.

OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES=deployment.environment=$(ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT)
# Appends the ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT value (Development/Staging/Production) as a resource
# attribute so traces can be filtered by environment in Tempo/Grafana dashboards.

OTEL_DOTNET_AUTO_TRACES_ADDITIONAL_SOURCES=*
# Enables instrumentation for all ActivitySource names in the application.
# Narrow this to specific source names (comma-separated) if you want to exclude third-party libs.

OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER=otlp
# Export runtime and ASP.NET Core metrics via OTLP to the collector.

OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER=otlp
# Forward structured logs via OTLP so log entries carry the same trace_id as the active span,
# enabling log-to-trace correlation in Grafana without additional configuration.

OTEL_PROPAGATORS=tracecontext,baggage
# W3C TraceContext ensures trace IDs propagate correctly across HTTP calls to other services.

# OpenTelemetry environment variables for Java (Spring Boot / eclipse-temurin)
#
# INSTALLATION:
# The Java agent is a single fat JAR that instruments the JVM at startup using bytecode
# injection — no code changes are required. Add it to your Docker image:
#
# In docker/java/Dockerfile.springboot, add after the COPY app.jar line:
#
#   ADD https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-java-instrumentation/releases/download/v2.1.0/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar /app/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar
#
# Then update the ENTRYPOINT to load the agent before the application JAR:
#
#   ENTRYPOINT ["java", \
#     "-javaagent:/app/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar", \
#     "-XX:+UseContainerSupport", \
#     "-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0", \
#     "-jar", "app.jar"]
#
# NOTE: The agent must appear before -jar. Using ADD with an HTTPS URL works in most
# environments, but prefer COPY from a local file when building in air-gapped CI systems
# to avoid network dependencies during image build.
#
# Check for the latest release at:
#   https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-java-instrumentation/releases
#
# USAGE:
# Kubernetes — add to your Deployment env section:
#   envFrom:
#     - configMapRef:
#         name: otel-java-env
# Docker Compose — add to your service:
#   env_file:
#     - observability/opentelemetry/env-vars/java.env
#
OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=my-java-service
# <-- CHANGE THIS to your service name (e.g. "order-service", "inventory-api")

OTEL_SERVICE_VERSION=1.0.0
# <-- CHANGE THIS to your deployed version, ideally injected by CI from the image tag

OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:4317
# Points to the OTel collector sidecar in the same pod on the gRPC port.

OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL=grpc
# grpc is the preferred transport for the Java agent's OTLP exporter.

OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER=parentbased_traceidratio
# Respects the parent span's sampling decision and applies the ratio below for root spans.

OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG=0.1
# 10% sampling in production. Adjust based on observed trace volume and storage budget.
# Spring Boot Actuator health check probes are already filtered at the collector level
# (see collector-config.yaml filter/exclude_health), so even 10% of health traffic
# is suppressed before reaching the Tempo backend.

OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES=deployment.environment=production
# <-- CHANGE THIS: inject the real environment name from a ConfigMap or Helm values file.

OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER=otlp
# Export JVM metrics (GC pause, heap usage, thread count) and Spring Actuator metrics via OTLP.

OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER=otlp
# Forward log records via OTLP. The agent bridges SLF4J/Logback records so they carry
# the active trace_id and span_id for log-trace correlation in Grafana.

OTEL_PROPAGATORS=tracecontext,baggage
# W3C TraceContext ensures trace IDs propagate through RestTemplate, WebClient, and Feign clients.

OTEL_JAVAAGENT_ENABLED=true
# Explicit flag to enable the agent. Set to false in unit tests or local runs where
# the sidecar collector is not available to avoid connection errors on startup.

OTEL_JAVAAGENT_LOGGING=generic
# "generic" routes agent internal logs through SLF4J so they appear in the application
# log stream alongside application messages. Alternative: "jul" (java.util.logging).

# OpenTelemetry environment variables for Python (FastAPI / uvicorn)
#
# INSTALLATION:
# Add the following packages to requirements.txt:
#
#   opentelemetry-distro>=0.43b0
#   opentelemetry-exporter-otlp-proto-grpc>=1.22.0
#
# Then bootstrap auto-instrumentation (downloads all instrumentors for detected libraries):
#
#   opentelemetry-bootstrap -a install
#
# RUNNING:
# Wrap your uvicorn command with opentelemetry-instrument:
#
#   opentelemetry-instrument uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --workers 4
#
# In Dockerfile.fastapi, replace the CMD line with:
#   CMD ["opentelemetry-instrument", "uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000", "--workers", "4"]
#
# The opentelemetry-instrument wrapper reads the env vars below at startup and patches
# FastAPI, requests, httpx, SQLAlchemy, and other libraries automatically.
#
# USAGE:
# Kubernetes — add to your Deployment env section:
#   envFrom:
#     - configMapRef:
#         name: otel-python-env
# Docker Compose — add to your service:
#   env_file:
#     - observability/opentelemetry/env-vars/python.env
#
OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=my-python-service
# <-- CHANGE THIS to your service name (e.g. "recommendation-api", "data-processor")

OTEL_SERVICE_VERSION=1.0.0
# <-- CHANGE THIS to your deployed version, ideally injected by CI from the image tag

OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:4317
# Points to the OTel collector sidecar in the same pod on the gRPC port.

OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL=grpc
# grpc is the preferred transport for Python's OTLP exporter.

OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER=parentbased_traceidratio
# Respects the parent span's sampling decision and applies the ratio below for root spans.

OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG=0.1
# 10% sampling in production. Sufficient to capture statistical patterns across high-throughput
# FastAPI endpoints without storing every trace. Set to 1.0 in dev/staging.

OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES=deployment.environment=production
# <-- CHANGE THIS: inject the real environment name from a ConfigMap or Helm values file
# so Grafana dashboards can filter traces by environment.

OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER=otlp
# Export Python runtime metrics and custom application metrics via OTLP.

OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER=otlp
# Forward log records via OTLP so they carry the active trace_id for log-trace correlation.

OTEL_PROPAGATORS=tracecontext,baggage
# W3C TraceContext propagation ensures trace IDs flow through httpx, requests, and aiohttp calls.

OTEL_PYTHON_LOG_CORRELATION=true
# Injects trace_id and span_id into every Python log record, which Promtail then promotes
# as a Loki label for cross-linking logs and traces in Grafana.

# cert-manager-alerts.yaml
#
# PrometheusRule for monitoring TLS certificate lifecycle events exposed by cert-manager.
#
# Prerequisites
# -------------
# 1. cert-manager >= 1.8 must be running with the metrics endpoint enabled.
#    The controller, cainjector, and webhook expose /metrics on port 9402 by default.
#
# 2. A ServiceMonitor (or PodMonitor) must exist in the monitoring namespace that
#    scrapes the cert-manager controller pods.  If you installed cert-manager via Helm,
#    enable it with:
#      --set prometheus.enabled=true \
#      --set prometheus.servicemonitor.enabled=true \
#      --set prometheus.servicemonitor.prometheusInstance=kube-prometheus-stack
#
# 3. The flag --enable-certificate-owner-ref is NOT required for metrics, but if you
#    rely on automatic secret cleanup you should enable it in the cert-manager values.
#
# 4. Apply this file to the cluster:
#      kubectl apply -f observability/prometheus/alerts/cert-manager-alerts.yaml
#
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: cert-manager-alerts
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    release: kube-prometheus-stack
    app: kube-prometheus-stack
spec:
  groups:
    - name: cert-manager.rules
      rules:

        # ── CertificateExpiringIn14Days ──────────────────────────────────────
        # Early-warning: gives the team two weeks to investigate renewal failures
        # before the certificate actually expires and traffic is disrupted.
        - alert: CertificateExpiringIn14Days
          expr: >-
            (
              certmanager_certificate_expiration_timestamp_seconds
              - time()
            ) < 1209600
          for: 1h
          labels:
            severity: warning
            release: kube-prometheus-stack
            app: kube-prometheus-stack
          annotations:
            summary: >-
              Certificate {{ $labels.name }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }}
              expires in less than 14 days.
            description: >-
              Certificate {{ $labels.name }} (namespace: {{ $labels.namespace }},
              issuer: {{ $labels.issuer_name }}) will expire in
              {{ printf "%.1f" (div (sub $value 0.0) 86400.0) }} days.
              Verify that cert-manager has successfully renewed the certificate
              and that the issuer ({{ $labels.issuer_kind }}/{{ $labels.issuer_name }})
              is reachable.
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/certmanager/certificateexpiring # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # ── CertificateExpiringIn7Days ───────────────────────────────────────
        # Critical escalation: one week remaining is the last comfortable window
        # for manual intervention if automated renewal has silently failed.
        # Page the on-call engineer — certificate expiry causes hard outages.
        - alert: CertificateExpiringIn7Days
          expr: >-
            (
              certmanager_certificate_expiration_timestamp_seconds
              - time()
            ) < 604800
          for: 1h
          labels:
            severity: critical
            release: kube-prometheus-stack
            app: kube-prometheus-stack
          annotations:
            summary: >-
              CRITICAL: Certificate {{ $labels.name }} in namespace
              {{ $labels.namespace }} expires in less than 7 days.
            description: >-
              Certificate {{ $labels.name }} (namespace: {{ $labels.namespace }},
              issuer: {{ $labels.issuer_kind }}/{{ $labels.issuer_name }}) will
              expire in {{ printf "%.1f" (div (sub $value 0.0) 86400.0) }} days.
              Automated renewal has not completed in time. Check cert-manager
              controller logs and issuer status immediately:
                kubectl describe certificate {{ $labels.name }} -n {{ $labels.namespace }}
                kubectl describe certificaterequest -n {{ $labels.namespace }}
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/certmanager/certificateexpiring # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # ── CertificateExpired ───────────────────────────────────────────────
        # The certificate is already past its NotAfter timestamp.
        # TLS handshakes will fail for any workload using this secret.
        # Fire immediately (for: 0m) — do not wait for a confirmation window.
        - alert: CertificateExpired
          expr: >-
            certmanager_certificate_expiration_timestamp_seconds - time() < 0
          for: 0m
          labels:
            severity: critical
            release: kube-prometheus-stack
            app: kube-prometheus-stack
          annotations:
            summary: >-
              Certificate {{ $labels.name }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }}
              has EXPIRED.
            description: >-
              Certificate {{ $labels.name }} (namespace: {{ $labels.namespace }},
              issuer: {{ $labels.issuer_kind }}/{{ $labels.issuer_name }}) expired
              {{ printf "%.0f" (div (sub 0.0 $value) 86400.0) }} day(s) ago.
              Any service using this certificate's Secret will be serving an
              invalid TLS certificate. Rotate or force-renew immediately:
                kubectl delete certificaterequest -n {{ $labels.namespace }} \
                  -l cert-manager.io/certificate-name={{ $labels.name }}
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/certmanager/certificateexpired # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # ── CertificateRenewalFailed ─────────────────────────────────────────
        # Detect certificates whose Ready condition has been False for >30 minutes.
        # This fires before expiry and covers cases where cert-manager is cycling
        # (e.g. ACME DNS challenge failures, rate limits, issuer misconfigurations).
        # The metric label condition="False" is set by cert-manager when the
        # Certificate object's Ready condition transitions to False.
        - alert: CertificateRenewalFailed
          expr: >-
            max by (namespace, name, issuer_name, issuer_kind) (
              certmanager_certificate_ready_status{condition="False"} == 1
            )
          for: 30m
          labels:
            severity: warning
            release: kube-prometheus-stack
            app: kube-prometheus-stack
          annotations:
            summary: >-
              Certificate {{ $labels.name }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }}
              has been in a non-Ready state for more than 30 minutes.
            description: >-
              Certificate {{ $labels.name }} (namespace: {{ $labels.namespace }},
              issuer: {{ $labels.issuer_kind }}/{{ $labels.issuer_name }}) has
              had a Ready=False condition for over 30 minutes, indicating that
              renewal or issuance is failing. Common causes: ACME challenge
              failure, DNS propagation delay, issuer rate-limit, or missing
              ClusterIssuer/Issuer. Investigate with:
                kubectl describe certificate {{ $labels.name }} -n {{ $labels.namespace }}
                kubectl get certificaterequest -n {{ $labels.namespace }}
                kubectl logs -n cert-manager deploy/cert-manager
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/certmanager/certificaterenewalfailed # <-- CHANGE THIS

apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: deployment-alerts
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  groups:
    - name: deployment.rules
      rules:
        - alert: DeploymentReplicasMismatch
          # Compare desired replicas to currently available replicas and alert when the gap persists.
          expr: >-
            kube_deployment_status_replicas_available{deployment!=""}
            <
            kube_deployment_spec_replicas{deployment!=""}
          for: 10m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: A deployment has fewer available replicas than it requested.
            description: Deployment {{ $labels.deployment }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} has had fewer available replicas than desired for more than 10 minutes.
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/deploymentreplicasmismatch # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - alert: DeploymentRolloutStuck
          # Detect deployments that still report unavailable replicas well past the normal rollout window.
          expr: >-
            kube_deployment_status_replicas_unavailable{deployment!=""} > 0
          for: 15m
          labels:
            severity: critical
          annotations:
            summary: A deployment rollout appears stuck because replicas remain unavailable.
            description: Deployment {{ $labels.deployment }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} still has unavailable replicas after 15 minutes.
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/deploymentrolloutstuck # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - alert: HpaMaxReplicasReached
          # Flag HPAs that are already at their configured ceiling and may need manual review.
          expr: >-
            kube_horizontalpodautoscaler_status_current_replicas{horizontalpodautoscaler!=""}
            >=
            kube_horizontalpodautoscaler_spec_max_replicas{horizontalpodautoscaler!=""}
          for: 10m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: An HPA has reached its configured maximum replica count.
            description: HPA {{ $labels.horizontalpodautoscaler }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} has been at max replicas long enough to warrant scaling review.
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/hpamaxreplicasreached # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - alert: HpaMetricsMissing
          # Detect HPAs whose ScalingActive condition is false, which often points to metrics-server or target metric issues.
          expr: >-
            max by (namespace, horizontalpodautoscaler) (
              kube_horizontalpodautoscaler_status_condition{condition="ScalingActive", status="false", horizontalpodautoscaler!=""}
            ) > 0
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: critical
          annotations:
            summary: An HPA cannot retrieve the metrics it needs to make scaling decisions.
            description: HPA {{ $labels.horizontalpodautoscaler }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} cannot read scaling metrics, which often indicates a metrics-server or metric pipeline issue.
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/hpametricsmissing # <-- CHANGE THIS

apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: pod-alerts
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  groups:
    - name: pod.rules
      rules:
        - alert: PodCrashLoopBackOff
          # Detect pods whose containers restarted more than five times in the last 15 minutes.
          expr: >-
            sum by (namespace, pod) (
              increase(kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{pod!=""}[15m])
            ) > 5
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: critical
          annotations:
            summary: Pod restart count is increasing fast enough to indicate a crash loop.
            description: Pod {{ $labels.pod }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} restarted more than five times in the last 15 minutes.
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/podcrashloopbackoff # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - alert: PodOOMKilled
          # Detect containers whose most recent termination reason was OOMKilled.
          expr: >-
            max by (namespace, pod) (
              kube_pod_container_status_last_terminated_reason{reason="OOMKilled", pod!=""}
            ) > 0
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: A container has been terminated by the kernel because it exceeded its memory limit.
            description: Pod {{ $labels.pod }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} recorded an OOMKilled termination.
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/podoomkilled # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - alert: PodNotReady
          # Alert when the pod Ready condition stays false across the full five-minute window.
          expr: >-
            max by (namespace, pod) (
              kube_pod_status_ready{condition="true", pod!=""} == 0
            ) > 0
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: A pod has remained not ready for longer than the expected rollout window.
            description: Pod {{ $labels.pod }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} has been in a not-ready state for more than five minutes.
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/podnotready # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - alert: ContainerHighCPU
          # Compare five-minute CPU usage rate to each container CPU limit and alert above 80 percent.
          expr: >-
            (
              sum by (namespace, pod, container) (
                rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{container!="", image!="", pod!=""}[5m])
              )
              /
              max by (namespace, pod, container) (
                kube_pod_container_resource_limits{resource="cpu", unit="core", container!="", pod!=""}
              )
            ) > 0.80
          for: 10m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: A container is sustaining CPU usage above 80 percent of its declared limit.
            description: Pod {{ $labels.pod }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} has a container running above 80 percent of its CPU limit for 10 minutes.
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/containerhighcpu # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - alert: ContainerHighMemory
          # Compare working-set memory to each container memory limit and alert above 85 percent.
          expr: >-
            (
              max by (namespace, pod, container) (
                container_memory_working_set_bytes{container!="", image!="", pod!=""}
              )
              /
              max by (namespace, pod, container) (
                kube_pod_container_resource_limits{resource="memory", unit="byte", container!="", pod!=""}
              )
            ) > 0.85
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: A container is sustaining memory usage above 85 percent of its declared limit.
            description: Pod {{ $labels.pod }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} has a container using more than 85 percent of its memory limit for five minutes.
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/containerhighmemory # <-- CHANGE THIS

# =============================================================================
# SLO Recording Rules and Multi-Window Multi-Burn-Rate Alerts
# =============================================================================
#
# TEMPLATE: WHEN TO USE
#   Copy this file for each service you want to hold to an SLO. Replace the
#   metric names, SLO targets, and labels marked # <-- CHANGE THIS.
#   Apply with: kubectl apply -f slo-rules.yaml
#
# APPROACH — Multi-Window Multi-Burn-Rate (Google SRE Workbook, Chapter 5)
#   Rather than alerting when errors exceed a simple threshold, these rules
#   track how quickly you are burning through your monthly error budget.
#   A burn rate of 1× means you will exhaust the budget in exactly 30 days.
#   A burn rate of 14.4× means you burn the full month's budget in 2 hours.
#
#   Each alert fires when BOTH a long window (trend) AND a short window
#   (confirmation) exceed the same burn-rate threshold. The dual window
#   suppresses false pages from momentary spikes and ensures slow-burn
#   degradation is caught before it silently drains the budget.
#
#   Threshold and window pairs used here (for a 99.9% availability SLO):
#   ┌───────────────┬───────────────┬──────────────┬────────────┬────────────┐
#   │ Alert name    │ Long window   │ Short window │ Burn rate  │ Severity   │
#   ├───────────────┼───────────────┼──────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
#   │ SloFastBurn   │ 1h            │ 5m           │ 14.4×      │ critical   │
#   │ SloFastBurn   │ 6h            │ 30m          │ 6×         │ critical   │
#   │ SloSlowBurn   │ 1d            │ 2h           │ 3×         │ warning    │
#   │ SloSlowBurn   │ 3d            │ 6h           │ 1×         │ warning    │
#   └───────────────┴───────────────┴──────────────┴────────────┴────────────┘
#
# SLOTH / PYRRA COMPATIBILITY
#   If you adopt Sloth (https://sloth.dev) or Pyrra (https://github.com/pyrra-dev/pyrra)
#   to auto-generate these rules from a declarative SLO spec, you can delete this file
#   and replace it with a SLO Custom Resource. The recording rule naming used here
#   matches the Sloth output convention so migration is drop-in.
#
#   Example Sloth SLO spec (for reference — not applied by this file):
#
#     apiVersion: sloth.slok.dev/v1
#     kind: PrometheusServiceLevel
#     metadata:
#       name: my-service-availability
#       namespace: monitoring
#     spec:
#       service: "my-service"                     # <-- CHANGE THIS
#       slos:
#         - name: "requests-availability"
#           objective: 99.9                        # <-- CHANGE THIS
#           sli:
#             events:
#               errorQuery: >-
#                 sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service",code=~"5.."}[{{.window}}]))
#               totalQuery: >-
#                 sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[{{.window}}]))
#           alerting:
#             name: MyServiceAvailability
#             annotations:
#               runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/myserviceavailability
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE:
#   1. Replace every `job="my-service"` with your actual Prometheus job label.
#   2. Replace `http_requests_total` with your service's request-counter metric.
#   3. Replace `code=~"5.."` with the error-selector for your metric.
#   4. Adjust the `slo_target` annotation on every alert (0.999 = 99.9%).
#   5. Update runbook_url to your real runbook location.
#   6. Clone the entire file for each additional service.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - kube-prometheus-stack installed (observability/prometheus/values.yaml)
#   - Your service exposes a request counter with an error dimension
#     (e.g. http_requests_total{code=~"5.."} for HTTP, or a custom metric)
# =============================================================================

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Part 1 — Availability SLO: 99.9% of requests succeed
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#
# Error ratio = error requests / total requests.
# We record it at seven window widths because Prometheus cannot evaluate a
# rule like `rate(metric[6h])` inside a recording rule defined in the same group.
# Pre-computing these ratios makes the alert expressions instantaneous lookups.
#
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: slo-availability-my-service           # <-- CHANGE THIS
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    # This label makes kube-prometheus-stack pick up this PrometheusRule automatically
    # (matches ruleSelector in observability/prometheus/values.yaml).
    app: kube-prometheus-stack
    release: kube-prometheus-stack
spec:
  groups:

    # ── Recording rules — error ratios over multiple windows ──────────────
    - name: slo:my-service:availability:ratio_rate  # <-- CHANGE THIS (service name)
      interval: 30s
      rules:

        # Note 1: The 5m and 30m windows are the "short" confirmation windows.
        # They are evaluated frequently (every 30s) to detect the start of a burn quickly.
        - record: slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate5m
          expr: >-
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[5m]))  # <-- CHANGE THIS
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[5m]))               # <-- CHANGE THIS
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS: matches the job label above

        - record: slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate30m
          expr: >-
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[30m]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[30m]))
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # Note 2: The 1h and 6h windows are the primary "long" windows for fast burn detection.
        # 1h × 14.4× burn = 100% error budget consumed in 2 hours (page immediately).
        # 6h × 6× burn = budget consumed in 5 hours (page before half-day milestone).
        - record: slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate1h
          expr: >-
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[1h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[1h]))
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - record: slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate6h
          expr: >-
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[6h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[6h]))
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # Note 3: 2h and 1d windows drive the "warning" slow-burn alerts.
        # 1d × 3× burn = budget consumed in 10 days (ticket + investigate).
        - record: slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate2h
          expr: >-
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[2h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[2h]))
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - record: slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate1d
          expr: >-
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[1d]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[1d]))
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # Note 4: The 3d window is the lowest-sensitivity check.
        # 3d × 1× burn = the service is right on the 30-day SLO boundary — ticket before it tips.
        - record: slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate3d
          expr: >-
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[3d]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[3d]))
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS

    # ── Burn-rate alerts — four pairs covering the full burn-rate ladder ───
    - name: slo:my-service:availability:burnrate  # <-- CHANGE THIS
      rules:

        # ── CRITICAL: fast burn (14.4× over 1h, confirmed by 5m) ──────────
        # Note 5: This alert fires when the 1-hour error rate exceeds 14.4× the error budget.
        # For a 99.9% SLO: error_budget = 0.001; threshold = 14.4 × 0.001 = 0.0144.
        # It self-resets (no for: clause) because a 14.4× burn that stops is resolved.
        # The `and` operator requires BOTH windows to be over threshold — the 5m window
        # confirms the burn is ongoing and not a leftover artefact of a resolved spike.
        - alert: SloFastBurn
          expr: >-
            (
              slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate1h{service="my-service"}  # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (14.4 * (1 - 0.999))                                       # <-- CHANGE THIS: 1 - slo_target
            )
            and
            (
              slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate5m{service="my-service"}  # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (14.4 * (1 - 0.999))
            )
          labels:
            severity: critical
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: availability
          annotations:
            summary: "{{ $labels.service }}: high error rate burning budget at 14.4× (SLO: 99.9%)"
            description: >-
              The 1-hour error ratio for {{ $labels.service }} is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }},
              which exceeds 14.4× the allowed error budget. At this rate the full monthly error budget
              will be consumed in approximately 2 hours.
            slo_target: "0.999"                                                       # <-- CHANGE THIS
            burn_rate: "14.4"
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/slofastburn                     # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # ── CRITICAL: fast burn (6× over 6h, confirmed by 30m) ───────────
        # Note 6: This pair catches burns that started several hours ago but weren't caught
        # by the 14.4× threshold (e.g. a 7× burn that oscillates below 14.4×).
        # For a 99.9% SLO: threshold = 6 × 0.001 = 0.006.
        - alert: SloFastBurn
          expr: >-
            (
              slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate6h{service="my-service"}  # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (6 * (1 - 0.999))
            )
            and
            (
              slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate30m{service="my-service"} # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (6 * (1 - 0.999))
            )
          labels:
            severity: critical
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: availability
          annotations:
            summary: "{{ $labels.service }}: sustained error rate burning budget at 6× (SLO: 99.9%)"
            description: >-
              The 6-hour error ratio for {{ $labels.service }} is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }},
              exceeding 6× the allowed error budget. At this rate the monthly budget will be exhausted
              in approximately 5 hours.
            slo_target: "0.999"
            burn_rate: "6"
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/slofastburn   # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # ── WARNING: slow burn (3× over 1d, confirmed by 2h) ─────────────
        # Note 7: 3× burn does not warrant an immediate page but should generate a ticket.
        # At 3×, the budget is consumed in 10 days — enough time to schedule a fix,
        # but not enough to ignore until the next sprint cycle.
        - alert: SloSlowBurn
          expr: >-
            (
              slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate1d{service="my-service"}  # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (3 * (1 - 0.999))
            )
            and
            (
              slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate2h{service="my-service"}  # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (3 * (1 - 0.999))
            )
          labels:
            severity: warning
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: availability
          annotations:
            summary: "{{ $labels.service }}: error rate burning budget at 3× — create a ticket (SLO: 99.9%)"
            description: >-
              The 1-day error ratio for {{ $labels.service }} is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }},
              which is 3× the allowed error budget. If this rate persists the monthly budget will be
              fully consumed in approximately 10 days.
            slo_target: "0.999"
            burn_rate: "3"
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/sloslowburn   # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # ── WARNING: slow burn (1× over 3d, confirmed by 6h) ─────────────
        # Note 8: 1× burn means the service is exactly on the boundary of meeting its SLO.
        # Three days of sustained 1× burn consumes 10% of the monthly budget — still recoverable
        # but a signal that something in the environment needs attention.
        - alert: SloSlowBurn
          expr: >-
            (
              slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate3d{service="my-service"}  # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (1 * (1 - 0.999))
            )
            and
            (
              slo:service_errors_total:ratio_rate6h{service="my-service"}  # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (1 * (1 - 0.999))
            )
          labels:
            severity: warning
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: availability
          annotations:
            summary: "{{ $labels.service }}: error rate matches budget burn rate exactly — review soon (SLO: 99.9%)"
            description: >-
              The 3-day error ratio for {{ $labels.service }} is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }},
              which is at the boundary of the allowed error budget. If unchanged over 30 days the
              SLO will be exactly missed.
            slo_target: "0.999"
            burn_rate: "1"
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/sloslowburn   # <-- CHANGE THIS

---
# =============================================================================
# Part 2 — Latency SLO: 99% of requests complete in under 500 ms
# =============================================================================
#
# Latency SLOs require a histogram metric (native histogram or classic _bucket).
# The error ratio here is: requests that exceeded the latency target / total requests.
# Replace `http_request_duration_seconds` with your histogram metric name.
# Replace `0.5` (500 ms) with your latency target in seconds.
#
# <-- CHANGE THIS: if your service does not expose a request-duration histogram,
# this entire section can be removed. Latency SLOs require histogram metrics.
#
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: slo-latency-my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    app: kube-prometheus-stack
    release: kube-prometheus-stack
spec:
  groups:

    # ── Recording rules — latency "error" ratios (requests > target) ──────
    - name: slo:my-service:latency:ratio_rate   # <-- CHANGE THIS
      interval: 30s
      rules:

        # Note 9: For latency SLOs the "error" is a request that missed the target.
        # We compute: (requests exceeding the threshold) / (total requests).
        # With a classic histogram: requests that DID meet the target are in the ≤ bucket;
        # requests that missed are total minus that bucket.
        - record: slo:service_latency:ratio_rate5m
          expr: >-
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[5m]))  # <-- CHANGE THIS
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[5m]))             # <-- CHANGE THIS
            )
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: latency

        - record: slo:service_latency:ratio_rate30m
          expr: >-
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[30m]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[30m]))
            )
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: latency

        - record: slo:service_latency:ratio_rate1h
          expr: >-
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[1h]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[1h]))
            )
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: latency

        - record: slo:service_latency:ratio_rate6h
          expr: >-
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[6h]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[6h]))
            )
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: latency

        - record: slo:service_latency:ratio_rate1d
          expr: >-
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[1d]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[1d]))
            )
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: latency

        - record: slo:service_latency:ratio_rate3d
          expr: >-
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[3d]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[3d]))
            )
          labels:
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: latency

    # ── Burn-rate alerts (99% latency SLO, error budget = 1%) ────────────
    - name: slo:my-service:latency:burnrate   # <-- CHANGE THIS
      rules:

        - alert: SloLatencyFastBurn
          # Note 10: Same multi-window logic as availability but targeting the latency ratio.
          # For a 99% SLO: error_budget = 0.01; 14.4× threshold = 0.144.
          expr: >-
            (
              slo:service_latency:ratio_rate1h{service="my-service", slo="latency"}   # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (14.4 * (1 - 0.99))
            )
            and
            (
              slo:service_latency:ratio_rate5m{service="my-service", slo="latency"}   # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (14.4 * (1 - 0.99))
            )
          labels:
            severity: critical
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: latency
          annotations:
            summary: "{{ $labels.service }}: high latency burning budget at 14.4× (SLO: 99% < 500ms)"
            description: >-
              The 1-hour slow-request ratio for {{ $labels.service }} is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }},
              exceeding 14.4× the allowed latency budget. At this rate the monthly budget will be
              consumed in approximately 2 hours.
            slo_target: "0.99"     # <-- CHANGE THIS
            burn_rate: "14.4"
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/slolatencyfastburn   # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - alert: SloLatencyFastBurn
          expr: >-
            (
              slo:service_latency:ratio_rate6h{service="my-service", slo="latency"}   # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (6 * (1 - 0.99))
            )
            and
            (
              slo:service_latency:ratio_rate30m{service="my-service", slo="latency"}  # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (6 * (1 - 0.99))
            )
          labels:
            severity: critical
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: latency
          annotations:
            summary: "{{ $labels.service }}: sustained latency burning budget at 6× (SLO: 99% < 500ms)"
            description: >-
              The 6-hour slow-request ratio for {{ $labels.service }} is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }},
              exceeding 6× the allowed latency budget.
            slo_target: "0.99"     # <-- CHANGE THIS
            burn_rate: "6"
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/slolatencyfastburn   # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - alert: SloLatencySlowBurn
          expr: >-
            (
              slo:service_latency:ratio_rate1d{service="my-service", slo="latency"}   # <-- CHANGE THIS
              > (3 * (1 - 0.99))
            )
            and
            (
              slo:service_latency:ratio_rate2h
              > (3 * (1 - 0.99))
            )
          labels:
            severity: warning
            service: my-service   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            slo: latency
          annotations:
            summary: "{{ $labels.service }}: latency burning budget at 3× — create a ticket (SLO: 99% < 500ms)"
            description: >-
              The 1-day slow-request ratio for {{ $labels.service }} is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
              If this rate persists the monthly latency budget will be consumed in approximately 10 days.
            slo_target: "0.99"     # <-- CHANGE THIS
            burn_rate: "3"
            runbook_url: https://runbooks.example.com/slolatencyslowburn   # <-- CHANGE THIS

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Prometheus SLO — Availability
# WHEN TO USE: You want multi-window burn-rate alerting for
#   a request-success-rate SLO (i.e., fraction of non-5xx requests).
#
# SLO TARGET: 99.9% availability  (error budget = 43m 49s per 30 days)
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE:
#   - SLO_TARGET annotation (default: 0.999)     # <-- CHANGE THIS
#   - job label filter (default: "my-service")   # <-- CHANGE THIS
#   - namespace label in metadata                # <-- CHANGE THIS
#   - Burn-rate thresholds if SLO target changes # <-- CHANGE THIS
#
# RELATED FILES: observability/prometheus/slos/README.md
#                observability/prometheus/slos/latency-slo.yaml
#                observability/prometheus/alerts/slo-rules.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: PrometheusRule is a CRD provided by the Prometheus Operator
# (kube-prometheus-stack). It is automatically discovered by any Prometheus
# instance with the matching label selector.
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: availability-slo                        # <-- CHANGE THIS: use your service name, e.g. payments-availability-slo
  namespace: monitoring                          # <-- CHANGE THIS: namespace where Prometheus Operator runs
  labels:
    # Note 2: The Prometheus Operator watches for PrometheusRule resources that
    # match the ruleSelector on the Prometheus CR. kube-prometheus-stack sets
    # this to `release: kube-prometheus-stack` by default.
    release: kube-prometheus-stack               # <-- CHANGE THIS: match your Prometheus Operator release name
    app: kube-prometheus-stack
  annotations:
    # Note 3: Document the SLO metadata in annotations for human reference.
    # These are not used by Prometheus but appear in alert notifications.
    SLO_TARGET: "0.999"                          # <-- CHANGE THIS: e.g. "0.995" for 99.5%
    SLO_WINDOW: "30d"
    SERVICE: "my-service"                        # <-- CHANGE THIS: your service name
spec:
  groups:

    # ── Recording Rules ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # Note 4: Recording rules pre-compute the error ratio over multiple time windows.
    # This makes alert evaluation fast (single metric lookup instead of two-range joins)
    # and makes the values available in Grafana dashboards.
    #
    # The metric name convention `slo:sli_error:ratio_rate<window>` is used by
    # the standard SLO dashboards (Grafana ID 14348, Sloth, Pyrra).
    #
    # Formula: (5xx requests / total requests) over the window
    # "error" = any 5xx response (server-side fault, not client error)
    - name: availability-slo-recording
      interval: 30s                              # <-- CHANGE THIS: how often rules are evaluated
      rules:
        # Note 5: Each recording rule covers one of the burn-rate windows.
        # Having all windows pre-computed means alert rules do just a simple comparison,
        # keeping alert evaluation latency under 1 second.
        - record: slo:sli_error:ratio_rate5m
          expr: |
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[5m]))  # <-- CHANGE THIS: job label and metric name
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[5m]))               # <-- CHANGE THIS
          labels:
            slo: availability
            service: my-service                  # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - record: slo:sli_error:ratio_rate30m
          expr: |
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[30m]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[30m]))
          labels:
            slo: availability
            service: my-service                  # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - record: slo:sli_error:ratio_rate1h
          expr: |
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[1h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[1h]))
          labels:
            slo: availability
            service: my-service

        - record: slo:sli_error:ratio_rate2h
          expr: |
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[2h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[2h]))
          labels:
            slo: availability
            service: my-service

        - record: slo:sli_error:ratio_rate6h
          expr: |
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[6h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[6h]))
          labels:
            slo: availability
            service: my-service

        - record: slo:sli_error:ratio_rate1d
          expr: |
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[1d]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[1d]))
          labels:
            slo: availability
            service: my-service

        - record: slo:sli_error:ratio_rate3d
          expr: |
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service", code=~"5.."}[3d]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="my-service"}[3d]))
          labels:
            slo: availability
            service: my-service

    # ── Alerting Rules ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # Note 6: The three alert tiers implement the multi-window burn rate strategy
    # from the Google SRE Workbook. See observability/prometheus/slos/README.md
    # for the full explanation.
    #
    # Thresholds are derived from: burn_rate × (1 - SLO_TARGET)
    #   - 14× burn = (1 - 0.999) × 14 = 0.014  → exhausts budget in ~50h
    #   - 6×  burn = (1 - 0.999) × 6  = 0.006  → exhausts budget in ~5 days
    #   - 3×  burn = (1 - 0.999) × 3  = 0.003  → exhausts budget in ~10 days
    #
    # If you change SLO_TARGET you MUST recalculate these thresholds.        # <-- CHANGE THIS
    - name: availability-slo-alerts
      rules:

        # ── Critical: Fast Burn (page immediately) ─────────────────────
        # Note 7: Two-window condition: BOTH windows must exceed the threshold.
        # The short window (5m) catches the onset of the incident quickly.
        # The long window (1h) prevents alerting on a brief spike that will
        # self-resolve without exhausting significant budget.
        - alert: SLOAvailabilityFastBurnCritical
          expr: |
            (
