              slo:sli_error:ratio_rate5m{service="my-service"} > (14 * (1 - 0.999))  # <-- CHANGE THIS: service label and SLO target
              and
              slo:sli_error:ratio_rate1h{service="my-service"} > (14 * (1 - 0.999))  # <-- CHANGE THIS
            )
          for: 2m
          labels:
            severity: critical
            slo: availability
            service: my-service                  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          annotations:
            summary: "SLO availability fast burn — critical ({{ $labels.service }})"
            description: >
              The error rate for {{ $labels.service }} is burning the availability error budget
              at 14× the acceptable rate. At this pace the entire 30-day budget will be
              exhausted in approximately 50 hours.
              Current 5m error ratio: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            runbook_url: "https://your-wiki.example.com/runbooks/slo-availability"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
            dashboard_url: "https://grafana.example.com/d/slo-availability"         # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # ── Warning: Medium Burn (ticket, investigate today) ───────────
        # Note 8: This tier catches burns that are fast enough to exhaust the budget
        # within 5 days but not fast enough to warrant an immediate page.
        # Use 6h + 3d windows to smooth out noise from normal traffic variance.
        - alert: SLOAvailabilityFastBurnWarning
          expr: |
            (
              slo:sli_error:ratio_rate30m{service="my-service"} > (6 * (1 - 0.999))  # <-- CHANGE THIS
              and
              slo:sli_error:ratio_rate6h{service="my-service"}  > (6 * (1 - 0.999))  # <-- CHANGE THIS
            )
          for: 15m
          labels:
            severity: warning
            slo: availability
            service: my-service                  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          annotations:
            summary: "SLO availability burn elevated — warning ({{ $labels.service }})"
            description: >
              The error rate for {{ $labels.service }} is burning the availability error budget
              at 6× the acceptable rate. At this pace the entire 30-day budget will be
              exhausted in approximately 5 days.
              Current 30m error ratio: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            runbook_url: "https://your-wiki.example.com/runbooks/slo-availability"  # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # ── Warning: Slow Burn (ticket, investigate this week) ─────────
        # Note 9: This tier catches a sustained low-level error rate that would
        # silently exhaust the error budget over 10+ days without any alert.
        # The very long windows (6h AND 3d) mean this alert only fires for
        # genuinely persistent issues, not transient deployments or restarts.
        - alert: SLOAvailabilitySlowBurnWarning
          expr: |
            (
              slo:sli_error:ratio_rate6h{service="my-service"} > (3 * (1 - 0.999))   # <-- CHANGE THIS
              and
              slo:sli_error:ratio_rate3d{service="my-service"} > (3 * (1 - 0.999))   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            )
          for: 1h
          labels:
            severity: warning
            slo: availability
            service: my-service                  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          annotations:
            summary: "SLO availability slow burn — warning ({{ $labels.service }})"
            description: >
              The error rate for {{ $labels.service }} has been burning the availability error
              budget at 3× the acceptable rate for over 6 hours. This is a sustained degradation.
              At this pace the 30-day error budget will be exhausted in approximately 10 days.
              Current 6h error ratio: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            runbook_url: "https://your-wiki.example.com/runbooks/slo-availability"  # <-- CHANGE THIS

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Prometheus SLO — Latency
# WHEN TO USE: You want multi-window burn-rate alerting for
#   a request-latency SLO (fraction of requests below a threshold).
#
# SLO TARGET: 99% of requests complete in < 500ms
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE:
#   - SLO_TARGET annotation (default: 0.99)               # <-- CHANGE THIS
#   - LATENCY_THRESHOLD_SECONDS annotation (default: 0.5) # <-- CHANGE THIS
#   - job label filter (default: "my-service")            # <-- CHANGE THIS
#   - histogram bucket le value to match threshold        # <-- CHANGE THIS
#   - namespace label in metadata                         # <-- CHANGE THIS
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   Your service must expose an http_request_duration_seconds histogram
#   with bucket boundaries that include your threshold.
#   Standard Prometheus client histograms include: .005, .01, .025, .05, .1,
#   .25, .5, 1, 2.5, 5, 10. For a 500ms SLO use le="0.5".
#   For a 200ms SLO use le="0.25" (closest bucket below 200ms).
#
# RELATED FILES: observability/prometheus/slos/README.md
#                observability/prometheus/slos/availability-slo.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: PrometheusRule is a CRD provided by the Prometheus Operator.
# Discovered automatically by Prometheus instances matching the label selector.
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: latency-slo                              # <-- CHANGE THIS: use your service name, e.g. payments-latency-slo
  namespace: monitoring                          # <-- CHANGE THIS
  labels:
    release: kube-prometheus-stack               # <-- CHANGE THIS: match your Prometheus Operator release name
    app: kube-prometheus-stack
  annotations:
    SLO_TARGET: "0.99"                           # <-- CHANGE THIS: fraction of requests that must meet the latency target
    LATENCY_THRESHOLD_SECONDS: "0.5"             # <-- CHANGE THIS: P99 latency threshold in seconds
    SERVICE: "my-service"                        # <-- CHANGE THIS
spec:
  groups:

    # ── Recording Rules ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # Note 2: Latency SLOs measure the fraction of requests BELOW a threshold,
    # using the histogram bucket for that threshold.
    #
    # Formula for the "good request" ratio:
    #   rate(histogram_bucket{le="0.5"}[window]) / rate(histogram_count[window])
    #
    # This is the inverse of the error ratio used in the availability SLO:
    #   error_ratio = 1 - good_ratio
    # We record the error ratio (1 - good) to keep the same direction as
    # the availability SLO and reuse the same burn-rate thresholds.
    - name: latency-slo-recording
      interval: 30s
      rules:
        # Note 3: `le="0.5"` selects the 500ms histogram bucket.
        # This bucket counts all requests that completed in ≤ 500ms.
        # Change `le` to match your latency threshold.       # <-- CHANGE THIS: le bucket
        - record: slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate5m
          expr: |
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[5m]))  # <-- CHANGE THIS: job and le
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[5m]))              # <-- CHANGE THIS: job
            )
          labels:
            slo: latency
            service: my-service                  # <-- CHANGE THIS

        - record: slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate30m
          expr: |
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[30m]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[30m]))
            )
          labels:
            slo: latency
            service: my-service

        - record: slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate1h
          expr: |
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[1h]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[1h]))
            )
          labels:
            slo: latency
            service: my-service

        - record: slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate2h
          expr: |
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[2h]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[2h]))
            )
          labels:
            slo: latency
            service: my-service

        - record: slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate6h
          expr: |
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[6h]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[6h]))
            )
          labels:
            slo: latency
            service: my-service

        - record: slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate1d
          expr: |
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[1d]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[1d]))
            )
          labels:
            slo: latency
            service: my-service

        - record: slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate3d
          expr: |
            1 - (
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="my-service", le="0.5"}[3d]))
              /
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="my-service"}[3d]))
            )
          labels:
            slo: latency
            service: my-service

    # ── Alerting Rules ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # Note 4: Burn-rate thresholds for a 99% SLO differ from a 99.9% SLO
    # because the error budget is larger (1% vs 0.1%).
    #
    # Thresholds: burn_rate × (1 - 0.99) = burn_rate × 0.01
    #   - 14× burn = 0.14  → exhausts budget in ~50h
    #   - 6×  burn = 0.06  → exhausts budget in ~5 days
    #   - 3×  burn = 0.03  → exhausts budget in ~10 days
    #                                                # <-- CHANGE THIS if SLO_TARGET changes
    - name: latency-slo-alerts
      rules:

        # ── Critical: Fast Burn (page immediately) ─────────────────────
        # Note 5: Two-window condition prevents false positives from brief spikes.
        # At 14× burn rate, 50h of error budget will be consumed — page immediately.
        - alert: SLOLatencyFastBurnCritical
          expr: |
            (
              slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate5m{service="my-service"}  > (14 * (1 - 0.99))  # <-- CHANGE THIS: service and SLO target
              and
              slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate1h{service="my-service"}  > (14 * (1 - 0.99))  # <-- CHANGE THIS
            )
          for: 2m
          labels:
            severity: critical
            slo: latency
            service: my-service                  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          annotations:
            summary: "SLO latency fast burn — critical ({{ $labels.service }})"
            description: >
              P99 latency for {{ $labels.service }} is burning the latency error budget
              at 14× the acceptable rate. The threshold is 500ms.              # <-- CHANGE THIS: threshold in message
              At this pace the entire 30-day budget will be exhausted in approximately 50 hours.
              Current 5m latency error ratio: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            runbook_url: "https://your-wiki.example.com/runbooks/slo-latency"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
            dashboard_url: "https://grafana.example.com/d/slo-latency"         # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # ── Warning: Medium Burn (ticket, investigate today) ───────────
        - alert: SLOLatencyFastBurnWarning
          expr: |
            (
              slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate30m{service="my-service"} > (6 * (1 - 0.99))   # <-- CHANGE THIS
              and
              slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate6h{service="my-service"}  > (6 * (1 - 0.99))   # <-- CHANGE THIS
            )
          for: 15m
          labels:
            severity: warning
            slo: latency
            service: my-service                  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          annotations:
            summary: "SLO latency burn elevated — warning ({{ $labels.service }})"
            description: >
              P99 latency for {{ $labels.service }} is burning the latency error budget
              at 6× the acceptable rate (threshold: 500ms).                   # <-- CHANGE THIS: threshold in message
              At this pace the entire 30-day budget will be exhausted in approximately 5 days.
              Current 30m latency error ratio: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            runbook_url: "https://your-wiki.example.com/runbooks/slo-latency"  # <-- CHANGE THIS

        # ── Warning: Slow Burn (ticket, investigate this week) ─────────
        # Note 6: Slow burn for latency often indicates a gradual database or
        # downstream service degradation. The 6h+3d windows confirm it is
        # a sustained trend, not a temporary deployment artifact.
        - alert: SLOLatencySlowBurnWarning
          expr: |
            (
              slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate6h{service="my-service"} > (3 * (1 - 0.99))    # <-- CHANGE THIS
              and
              slo:sli_latency_error:ratio_rate3d{service="my-service"} > (3 * (1 - 0.99))    # <-- CHANGE THIS
            )
          for: 1h
          labels:
            severity: warning
            slo: latency
            service: my-service                  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          annotations:
            summary: "SLO latency slow burn — warning ({{ $labels.service }})"
            description: >
              P99 latency for {{ $labels.service }} has been above the 500ms threshold # <-- CHANGE THIS: threshold
              at 3× the acceptable error rate for over 6 hours. Sustained degradation detected.
              At this pace the 30-day error budget will be exhausted in approximately 10 days.
              Current 6h latency error ratio: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            runbook_url: "https://your-wiki.example.com/runbooks/slo-latency"  # <-- CHANGE THIS

# kube-prometheus-stack values for chart 55.x
# Production teams should layer environment-specific overrides on top of this file
# instead of editing shared defaults directly.

prometheus:
  prometheusSpec:
    retention: 30d # Keeps one month of metrics for trend analysis and incident review.
    scrapeInterval: 30s # Matches the health check intervals in base deployment closely enough without oversampling them.
    evaluationInterval: 30s
    serviceMonitorSelectorNilUsesHelmValues: false # Allows ServiceMonitors outside the chart's namespace and release labels to be discovered.
    serviceMonitorSelector: {}
    serviceMonitorNamespaceSelector: {}
    podMonitorSelectorNilUsesHelmValues: false
    podMonitorSelector: {}
    podMonitorNamespaceSelector: {}
    ruleSelectorNilUsesHelmValues: false # Lets Prometheus pick up PrometheusRule resources managed outside the Helm release.
    ruleSelector: {}
    ruleNamespaceSelector: {}
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: 500m
        memory: 2Gi
      limits:
        cpu: "2"
        memory: 6Gi
    storageSpec:
      volumeClaimTemplate:
        spec:
          accessModes:
            - ReadWriteOnce
          resources:
            requests:
              storage: 50Gi # Sized for 30 days of cluster plus app metrics in a small-to-mid production cluster.
    # Discover Services that explicitly opt in with monitoring: "true".
    additionalScrapeConfigs:
      - job_name: kubernetes-service-endpoints-opt-in
        kubernetes_sd_configs:
          - role: endpoints
        relabel_configs:
          - source_labels:
              - __meta_kubernetes_service_label_monitoring
            action: keep
            regex: "true"
          - source_labels:
              - __meta_kubernetes_service_annotation_prometheus_io_scheme
            action: replace
            regex: (https?)
            target_label: __scheme__
          - source_labels:
              - __meta_kubernetes_service_annotation_prometheus_io_path
            action: replace
            regex: (.+)
            target_label: __metrics_path__
          - source_labels:
              - __address__
              - __meta_kubernetes_service_annotation_prometheus_io_port
            action: replace
            regex: ([^:]+)(?::\d+)?;(\d+)
            replacement: $1:$2
            target_label: __address__
          - action: labelmap
            regex: __meta_kubernetes_service_label_(.+)
          - source_labels:
              - __meta_kubernetes_namespace
            target_label: namespace
          - source_labels:
              - __meta_kubernetes_service_name
            target_label: service

    # Common production additions teams often enable later:
    # thanos:
    #   objectStorageConfig:
    #     existingSecret:
    #       name: thanos-objstore-config # <-- CHANGE THIS
    #       key: objstore.yml
    # remoteWrite:
    #   - url: https://remote-write.example.com/api/v1/write # <-- CHANGE THIS
    #     basicAuth:
    #       username:
    #         name: remote-write-auth # <-- CHANGE THIS
    #         key: username
    #       password:
    #         name: remote-write-auth # <-- CHANGE THIS
    #         key: password

alertmanager:
  enabled: true
  alertmanagerSpec:
    replicas: 2
    retention: 120h
    additionalArgs:
      - --config.expand-env
    # Source these credentials from Kubernetes Secrets or an external secret controller.
    # See docs/guides/secrets-management.md for the recommended secret-management patterns.
    containers:
      - name: alertmanager
        env:
          - name: SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL
            valueFrom:
              secretKeyRef:
                name: observability-alertmanager-secrets # <-- CHANGE THIS
                key: slack-webhook-url
          - name: PAGERDUTY_KEY
            valueFrom:
              secretKeyRef:
                name: observability-alertmanager-secrets # <-- CHANGE THIS
                key: pagerduty-key
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: 200m
        memory: 512Mi
      limits:
        cpu: 1
        memory: 1Gi
  config:
    global:
      resolve_timeout: 5m
    route:
      receiver: slack-warnings
      group_by:
        - alertname
        - namespace
        - severity
      group_wait: 30s
      group_interval: 5m
      repeat_interval: 4h
      routes:
        - receiver: pagerduty-critical
          matchers:
            - severity="critical"
        - receiver: slack-warnings
          matchers:
            - severity="warning"
    receivers:
      - name: slack-warnings
        slack_configs:
          - api_url: ${SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL}
            channel: '#platform-alerts' # <-- CHANGE THIS
            send_resolved: true
            title: >-
              [{{ .Status | toUpper }}] {{ .CommonLabels.alertname }} in {{ .CommonLabels.namespace }}
            text: >-
              {{ range .Alerts -}}
              *Summary:* {{ .Annotations.summary }}
              *Description:* {{ .Annotations.description }}
              *Severity:* {{ .Labels.severity }}
              *Runbook:* {{ .Annotations.runbook_url }}
              {{ end }}
      - name: pagerduty-critical
        pagerduty_configs:
          - routing_key: ${PAGERDUTY_KEY}
            send_resolved: true
            severity: '{{ .CommonLabels.severity }}'
            description: >-
              {{ .CommonLabels.alertname }} in {{ .CommonLabels.namespace }}
            client: Alertmanager
            client_url: http://alertmanager-operated.monitoring.svc:9093 # <-- CHANGE THIS
            details:
              summary: '{{ .CommonAnnotations.summary }}'
              description: '{{ .CommonAnnotations.description }}'
              namespace: '{{ .CommonLabels.namespace }}'
              alertname: '{{ .CommonLabels.alertname }}'

grafana:
  enabled: true
  defaultDashboardsEnabled: true
  admin:
    existingSecret: grafana-admin-credentials # <-- CHANGE THIS
    userKey: admin-user
    passwordKey: admin-password
  persistence:
    enabled: true
    accessModes:
      - ReadWriteOnce
    size: 10Gi # Enough room for dashboards, plugins, and retained Grafana state without over-allocating by default.
  ingress:
    enabled: true
    ingressClassName: nginx
    hosts:
      - grafana.example.com # <-- CHANGE THIS
    path: /
    pathType: Prefix
  sidecar:
    dashboards:
      enabled: true
      searchNamespace: ALL
      label: grafana_dashboard
      labelValue: "1"
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: 100m
      memory: 256Mi
    limits:
      cpu: 500m
      memory: 1Gi

# Grafana datasource ConfigMap for Tempo.
# The Grafana datasource sidecar picks this up automatically via the grafana_datasource: "1" label.
#
# PREREQUISITE: The Grafana Helm release must have the datasource sidecar enabled.
# Add the following to observability/prometheus/values.yaml under grafana: and then upgrade:
#
#   grafana:
#     sidecar:
#       datasources:
#         enabled: true
#         searchNamespace: ALL
#         label: grafana_datasource
#         labelValue: "1"
#
# This file also configures trace-to-logs correlation: clicking a trace span in Grafana
# Explore navigates directly to the correlated log lines in Loki using the trace_id field.
# The Loki datasource ConfigMap in observability/loki/grafana-datasource.yaml performs
# the reverse link (log line → trace), so both directions work out of the box.
#
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: tempo-grafana-datasource
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    # Tells the Grafana datasource sidecar to load this as a provisioned datasource.
    grafana_datasource: "1"
data:
  tempo-datasource.yaml: |-
    apiVersion: 1
    datasources:
      - name: Tempo
        type: tempo
        access: proxy
        # Points to the Tempo service installed by the Helm chart in the same namespace.
        url: http://tempo:3200
        uid: tempo   # Stable UID referenced by the Loki datasource for trace-to-logs linking.
        isDefault: false   # Prometheus is the default for alerting panels.
        version: 1
        editable: false    # Prevent UI drift from the provisioned config.
        jsonData:
          # ── TraceQL / Search ───────────────────────────────────────────────
          httpMethod: GET   # Tempo query API uses GET for search and trace fetch.

          # ── Service Graph ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
          # When metricsGenerator is enabled in values.yaml, Tempo writes span-derived
          # service graph metrics to Prometheus. Point the serviceMap datasource at
          # the same Prometheus instance that receives those remote_write metrics.
          serviceMap:
            datasourceUid: prometheus   # Must match the uid of your Prometheus datasource.

          # ── Node Graph ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
          nodeGraph:
            enabled: true   # Renders a service dependency graph for a selected trace.

          # ── Trace to Logs (Tempo → Loki) ───────────────────────────────────
          # When a user opens a trace in Grafana Explore, clicking any span shows a
          # "Logs for this span" button. This config routes that click to Loki.
          tracesToLogsV2:
            # <-- CHANGE THIS: set to the uid of your Loki datasource
            datasourceUid: loki
            # Span start/end window expanded by ±1 s to capture log lines emitted
            # in the same millisecond but with slight clock skew.
            spanStartTimeShift: "-1s"
            spanEndTimeShift: "1s"
            # Filter Loki log lines to those carrying the trace_id extracted from the span.
            filterByTraceID: true
            filterBySpanID: false   # Span-level filtering is too narrow; trace-level is more useful.
            customQuery: false      # Use the auto-generated query rather than a custom LogQL expression.
            tags:
              # Map OTel span attributes to Loki label matchers.
              # These must match the labels Promtail promotes in observability/loki/values.yaml.
              - key: service.name
                value: app   # The Loki label that Promtail sets from the pod app= label.
              - key: k8s.namespace.name
                value: namespace

          # ── Trace to Metrics (Tempo → Prometheus) ──────────────────────────
          # When metricsGenerator is enabled, clicking a span can show the corresponding
          # Prometheus RED metrics for that service in the same time window.
          tracesToMetrics:
            datasourceUid: prometheus
            spanStartTimeShift: "-2m"
            spanEndTimeShift: "2m"
            tags:
              - key: service.name
                value: job   # The Prometheus job label derived from the service name.
            queries:
              - name: "Request rate"
                query: "rate(traces_spanmetrics_calls_total{$$__tags}[5m])"
              - name: "Error rate"
                query: "rate(traces_spanmetrics_calls_total{$$__tags,status_code=\"STATUS_CODE_ERROR\"}[5m])"
              - name: "P99 latency"
                query: "histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(traces_spanmetrics_duration_milliseconds_bucket{$$__tags}[5m])) by (le))"

          # ── Loki Search (Logs → Traces) ────────────────────────────────────
          # When a user navigates from a Loki log line that contains a trace_id,
          # this maps the trace_id back to Tempo for the reverse correlation.
          lokiSearch:
            datasourceUid: loki

          # ── Search (TraceQL query editor) ──────────────────────────────────
          search:
            hide: false   # Show the TraceQL search tab in Explore.

# grafana/tempo chart values (grafana/tempo 1.9.x — monolithic / single-binary mode)
#
# Install:
#   helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts
#   helm install tempo grafana/tempo --version 1.9.0 -n monitoring -f values.yaml
#
# Monolithic mode runs all Tempo components (distributor, ingester, querier, compactor,
# query-frontend) in a single process. This is the right starting point for:
#   - clusters generating up to ~10 000 spans/second
#   - teams without object storage set up yet
# For higher volumes, switch to the grafana/tempo-distributed chart:
# https://grafana.com/docs/tempo/latest/setup/helm-chart/
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE when adapting this file:
#   block_retention      — how long to keep trace data (default 72 h; increase for compliance)
#   storage.trace.backend — switch from local to s3/gcs/azure before running in production
#   resources            — raise limits as ingestion volume grows
#   metricsGenerator     — enable if you want span-derived RED metrics in Prometheus

# =============================================================================
# Tempo configuration (written to /conf/tempo.yaml inside the container)
# =============================================================================

tempo:
  # Google's anonymous feature-usage reporting. Disable in air-gapped or regulated environments.
  reportingEnabled: false

  server:
    http_listen_port: 3200   # Query API and Grafana datasource target.
    grpc_listen_port: 9095   # Used by intra-component communication when running distributed.
    log_level: warn          # warn keeps logs quiet in production; change to info when debugging.

  # ─── Storage ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  storage:
    trace:
      # Filesystem storage is the simplest option and requires no external dependencies.
      # For production durability, switch to an object storage backend:
      #   S3:     backend: s3    — add s3.bucket, s3.region, s3.access_key, s3.secret_key
      #   GCS:    backend: gcs   — add gcs.bucket_name
      #   Azure:  backend: azure — add azure.container_name, azure.account_name, azure.account_key
      # <-- CHANGE THIS to object storage before running in production
      backend: local
      local:
        path: /var/tempo/traces
      wal:
        # The write-ahead log persists un-flushed blocks so that an in-flight trace
        # survives a pod restart without being lost entirely.
        path: /var/tempo/wal

  # ─── Compactor ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  compactor:
    compaction:
      # block_retention controls how long trace data is kept.
      # 72 h (3 days) balances storage cost against incident response time.
      # Postmortem workflows that require longer windows should raise this.
      # <-- CHANGE THIS: increase (e.g. 168h = 7d) for regulated environments
      block_retention: 72h
      # Compact blocks smaller than 100 MiB into larger ones to reduce object-store operations
      # and improve query fan-out performance.
      max_block_bytes: 104857600   # 100 MiB
      max_compaction_objects: 6000000

  # ─── Ingester ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ingester:
    # Flush a block to storage after 5 minutes of inactivity rather than holding it
    # in memory indefinitely. Shorter values use less memory but increase write amplification.
    trace_idle_period: 10s
    max_block_duration: 5m
    max_block_bytes: 104857600   # 100 MiB — flush early if a block grows to 100 MiB

  # ─── Querier ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  querier:
    max_concurrent_queries: 20   # Limit parallel backend reads to prevent storage saturation.
    frontend_worker:
      # Single-binary: the frontend and querier live in the same process, so point at localhost.
      frontend_address: tempo:9095

  # ─── Query Frontend ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  query_frontend:
    search:
      # 0 = no duration cap on search queries. Apply a cap in multi-tenant setups to bound cost.
      max_duration: 0             # <-- CHANGE THIS: set e.g. 168h in shared tenancy deployments
      default_result_limit: 20   # Default page size for trace-search API calls.
      max_result_limit: 0        # 0 = no cap; the frontend enforces pagination client-side.

  # ─── Distributor / Receivers ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Configure the protocols Tempo accepts. OTLP gRPC (4317) and HTTP (4318) are the
  # standard ports used by the OTel collector sidecar in observability/opentelemetry/
  # and by all OTel SDK language integrations.
  distributor:
    receivers:
      otlp:
        protocols:
          grpc:
            # Matches the otlp/tempo exporter endpoint in observability/opentelemetry/collector-config.yaml.
            endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4317
          http:
            endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4318
      # Uncomment if you also receive Jaeger-format traces (e.g. from legacy services):
      # jaeger:
      #   protocols:
      #     thrift_http:
      #       endpoint: 0.0.0.0:14268
      #     grpc:
      #       endpoint: 0.0.0.0:14250

  # ─── Metrics Generator (span → RED metrics) ───────────────────────────────
  # Disabled by default to keep storage and CPU cost predictable.
  # Enable to derive request rate, error rate, and duration percentiles directly
  # from spans — useful when your services do not yet expose Prometheus metrics.
  #
  # Enabling requires a remote_write target (Prometheus or Thanos).
  metricsGenerator:
    enabled: false    # <-- CHANGE THIS to true to activate span-derived metrics
    remoteWrite:
      []
      # - url: http://kube-prometheus-stack-prometheus:9090/api/v1/write  # <-- CHANGE THIS

# =============================================================================
# Kubernetes resource settings (outside the tempo config block)
# =============================================================================

# Persistent volume for WAL and local trace storage.
# In production on object storage, this can be sized smaller (WAL only ~ 2 Gi).
persistence:
  enabled: true
  size: 10Gi   # <-- CHANGE THIS: increase to 50+ Gi if retention is >3 days on filesystem
  # storageClassName: ""  # <-- CHANGE THIS to an SSD-backed class in production

resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 512Mi
  limits:
    cpu: "1"
    memory: 2Gi   # Raise to 4+ Gi for high-cardinality trace workloads (>5 000 spans/s)

# Label this pod so the additionalScrapeConfigs job in prometheus/values.yaml picks it up.
podLabels:
  app: tempo
  monitoring: "true"

# ─── Service Monitor ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Let kube-prometheus-stack scrape Tempo's own metrics from :3200/metrics.
serviceMonitor:
  enabled: true
  interval: 30s
  additionalLabels: {}

# ─── Tempo Gateway (optional) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
# The gateway exposes a single entry-point for both reads and writes over HTTPS
# with basic auth. Disable in dev/test where mTLS is not required.
gateway:
  enabled: false   # <-- CHANGE THIS to true if you want multi-tenant basic-auth ingress

# ─── Multi-tenancy ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Disabled here for single-tenant operation (no X-Scope-OrgID header required).
# In multi-tenant setups (multiple teams sharing one Tempo instance) each request
# must carry an X-Scope-OrgID header and auth_enabled must be set to true.
# See https://grafana.com/docs/tempo/latest/operations/multitenancy/

# conftest configuration
# https://www.conftest.dev/configuration/

# Root directory that conftest searches for policy files.
# Override per invocation with --policy <path> when you only want
# to run a subset (e.g. only kubernetes/ or only terraform/).
policy: policy/conftest

# Emit results as a human-readable table in the terminal.
# Switch to "json" or "junit" for machine-readable CI output.
output: table

# Warnings do not cause a non-zero exit code by default.
# Set to true to make warnings fail the gate (useful in pre-merge CI).
fail-on-warn: false

# deny_latest_tag.rego
# Deny container images that use the :latest tag or have no tag at all.
# Pinning images to a specific digest or semver tag ensures reproducible
# deployments and prevents silent image drift between rollouts.
package main

import future.keywords.contains
import future.keywords.if
import future.keywords.in

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Collect containers from Deployment pods and bare Pods.
_containers(input_obj) := containers if {
	input_obj.kind == "Deployment"
	containers := input_obj.spec.template.spec.containers
} else := containers if {
	input_obj.kind == "Pod"
	containers := input_obj.spec.containers
}

# Return true when the image string has no tag or uses :latest.
_latest_or_untagged(image) if {
	not contains(image, ":")
}

_latest_or_untagged(image) if {
	endswith(image, ":latest")
}

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Rules
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

deny contains msg if {
	some container in _containers(input)
	_latest_or_untagged(container.image)
	msg := sprintf(
		"%s/%s: container %q uses image %q — pin to a specific version tag or digest (not :latest or untagged)",
		[input.kind, input.metadata.name, container.name, container.image],
	)
}

# deny_privileged.rego
# Deny containers that run with elevated Linux privileges.
# Privileged containers can escape the container boundary and compromise
# the host node. These checks mirror the Kubernetes Restricted Pod
# Security Standard (https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/).
package main

import future.keywords.contains
import future.keywords.if
import future.keywords.in

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Collect containers (and initContainers) from Deployments and bare Pods.
_all_containers(input_obj) := containers if {
	input_obj.kind == "Deployment"
	pod_spec := input_obj.spec.template.spec
	containers := array.concat(
		object.get(pod_spec, "containers", []),
		object.get(pod_spec, "initContainers", []),
	)
}

_all_containers(input_obj) := containers if {
	input_obj.kind == "Pod"
	containers := array.concat(
		object.get(input_obj.spec, "containers", []),
		object.get(input_obj.spec, "initContainers", []),
	)
}

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Rules
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 1. securityContext.privileged must not be true.
deny contains msg if {
	some container in _all_containers(input)
	container.securityContext.privileged == true
	msg := sprintf(
		"%s/%s: container %q has securityContext.privileged=true — remove or set to false",
		[input.kind, input.metadata.name, container.name],
	)
}

# 2. allowPrivilegeEscalation must not be true.
deny contains msg if {
	some container in _all_containers(input)
	container.securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation == true
	msg := sprintf(
		"%s/%s: container %q has securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation=true — set to false",
		[input.kind, input.metadata.name, container.name],
	)
}

# 3. runAsNonRoot must be explicitly set to true (i.e. runAsRoot must be false).
deny contains msg if {
	some container in _all_containers(input)
	not container.securityContext.runAsNonRoot == true
	msg := sprintf(
		"%s/%s: container %q does not set securityContext.runAsNonRoot=true — add it to prevent running as root",
		[input.kind, input.metadata.name, container.name],
	)
}

# require_labels.rego
# Deny Deployments missing required identification labels and Namespaces
# missing the environment label. Consistent labels enable cost attribution,
# network policy targeting, and observability dashboards to work correctly.
package main

import future.keywords.contains
import future.keywords.if

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Deployment rules — required: app, version
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

deny contains msg if {
	input.kind == "Deployment"
	not input.metadata.labels.app
	msg := sprintf(
		"Deployment/%s: missing required label 'app' — add metadata.labels.app to identify the workload",
		[input.metadata.name],
	)
}

deny contains msg if {
	input.kind == "Deployment"
	not input.metadata.labels.version
	msg := sprintf(
		"Deployment/%s: missing required label 'version' — add metadata.labels.version for traffic splitting and rollback tracking",
		[input.metadata.name],
	)
}

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Namespace rules — required: environment ∈ {dev, staging, production}
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

_valid_environments := {"dev", "staging", "production"}

deny contains msg if {
	input.kind == "Namespace"
	not input.metadata.labels.environment
	msg := sprintf(
		"Namespace/%s: missing required label 'environment' (must be one of: dev, staging, production)",
		[input.metadata.name],
	)
}

deny contains msg if {
	input.kind == "Namespace"
	env := input.metadata.labels.environment
	not _valid_environments[env]
	msg := sprintf(
		"Namespace/%s: label 'environment=%s' is not valid — must be one of: dev, staging, production",
		[input.metadata.name, env],
	)
}

# require_probes.rego
# Deny Deployment containers that are missing liveness or readiness probes
# when the Deployment runs more than one replica. Single-replica workloads
# are exempt because a failed probe would cause immediate downtime with no
# benefit from automatic restarts; multi-replica workloads can tolerate a
# pod being temporarily removed from the load-balancer or restarted.
package main

import future.keywords.contains
import future.keywords.if
import future.keywords.in

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

_multi_replica_deployment(input_obj) if {
	input_obj.kind == "Deployment"
	input_obj.spec.replicas > 1
}

_deployment_containers(input_obj) := containers if {
	input_obj.kind == "Deployment"
	containers := input_obj.spec.template.spec.containers
}

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Rules
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

deny contains msg if {
	_multi_replica_deployment(input)
	some container in _deployment_containers(input)
	not container.livenessProbe
	msg := sprintf(
		"Deployment/%s (replicas=%d): container %q is missing livenessProbe",
		[input.metadata.name, input.spec.replicas, container.name],
	)
}

deny contains msg if {
	_multi_replica_deployment(input)
	some container in _deployment_containers(input)
	not container.readinessProbe
	msg := sprintf(
		"Deployment/%s (replicas=%d): container %q is missing readinessProbe",
		[input.metadata.name, input.spec.replicas, container.name],
	)
}

# require_resources.rego
# Deny Deployment containers that are missing CPU or memory requests/limits.
# Without resource constraints the Kubernetes scheduler cannot make accurate
# placement decisions, and a runaway process can starve neighbouring workloads.
package main

import future.keywords.contains
import future.keywords.if
import future.keywords.in

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

_deployment_containers(input_obj) := containers if {
	input_obj.kind == "Deployment"
	containers := input_obj.spec.template.spec.containers
}

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Rules
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

deny contains msg if {
	some container in _deployment_containers(input)
	not container.resources.requests.cpu
	msg := sprintf(
		"Deployment/%s: container %q is missing resources.requests.cpu",
		[input.metadata.name, container.name],
	)
}

deny contains msg if {
	some container in _deployment_containers(input)
	not container.resources.requests.memory
	msg := sprintf(
		"Deployment/%s: container %q is missing resources.requests.memory",
		[input.metadata.name, container.name],
	)
}

deny contains msg if {
	some container in _deployment_containers(input)
	not container.resources.limits.cpu
	msg := sprintf(
		"Deployment/%s: container %q is missing resources.limits.cpu",
		[input.metadata.name, container.name],
	)
}

deny contains msg if {
	some container in _deployment_containers(input)
	not container.resources.limits.memory
	msg := sprintf(
		"Deployment/%s: container %q is missing resources.limits.memory",
		[input.metadata.name, container.name],
	)
}

# deny_public_s3.rego
# Deny aws_s3_bucket resources that expose data publicly via a permissive ACL.
# "public-read" and "public-read-write" make bucket contents visible or
# writable by anyone on the internet; use bucket policies with explicit
# IAM principals instead.
#
# Tested against Terraform plan JSON (conftest test <planfile>.json).
package main

import future.keywords.contains
import future.keywords.if
import future.keywords.in

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

_public_acls := {"public-read", "public-read-write"}

# Walk the Terraform plan's resource_changes to find aws_s3_bucket resources.
_s3_resource_changes[resource] if {
	some resource in input.resource_changes
	resource.type == "aws_s3_bucket"
}

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Rules
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

deny contains msg if {
	some resource in _s3_resource_changes
	acl := resource.change.after.acl
	_public_acls[acl]
	msg := sprintf(
		"aws_s3_bucket.%s: ACL is set to %q — public ACLs expose bucket contents to the internet; use bucket policies with explicit IAM principals instead",
		[resource.name, acl],
	)
}

# Disallows the :latest image tag (or images with no tag) in production namespaces.
#
# Production namespaces are identified by the label:  environment: production
# All other namespaces receive audit-mode warnings rather than hard blocks, because:
#   - Dev and staging teams may use mutable tags (e.g. :dev, :branch-name) during
#     active feature development before a release is cut.
#   - Enforcing in non-production namespaces would block fast-iteration workflows.
#
# Why :latest breaks rollback:
#   The :latest tag is mutable — it can point to a different image digest after every
#   push. If a bad release is deployed and you roll back to the previous Deployment
#   revision, Kubernetes will re-pull :latest which may still resolve to the broken
#   image. Pinning to an immutable digest (sha256:…) or a semantic version tag
#   (v1.2.3) guarantees that a rollback restores exactly the known-good binary.
#
# Reference: cd/kubernetes/_overlays/prod/kustomization.yaml
#   images:
#     - name: my-registry/my-app
#       newTag: v1.0.0   # Pin to immutable tag in production
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-latest-tag
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Latest Image Tag
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Image Management
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: high
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Container images must not use the ':latest' tag or omit a tag entirely in
      production namespaces. The ':latest' tag is mutable and breaks rollback because
      re-pulling the tag after a bad release may resolve to the same broken image.
      Pin images to a semantic version tag (e.g. v1.2.3) or an immutable digest
      (sha256:…). Production enforcement is scoped to namespaces labelled
      'environment: production'. Dev/staging namespaces receive audit warnings only.
spec:
  # Audit everywhere by default.
  # The validationFailureActionOverrides block below upgrades to Enforce for any
  # namespace carrying the label  environment: production.
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  validationFailureActionOverrides:
    - action: Enforce
      namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          environment: production
  background: true
  rules:
    # ── Rule 1: main containers ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    - name: check-container-image-tag
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Pod]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              namespaces:
                - kube-system
                - kube-public
                - kyverno
      validate:
        message: >-
          Container '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' uses image
          '{{ request.object.spec.containers[*].image }}' which has no tag or uses
          ':latest'. Mutable tags break rollback: if the tag is re-pushed with a
          broken image, rolling back the Deployment will re-pull the same broken
          image. Use a specific semantic version tag or an immutable digest:
            image: my-registry/my-app:v1.2.3
            # or
            image: my-registry/my-app@sha256:<digest>
          Reference: cd/kubernetes/_overlays/prod/kustomization.yaml
        pattern:
          spec:
            containers:
              - image: "*:*"
                (image): "!*:latest"

    # ── Rule 2: init containers ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    - name: check-init-container-image-tag
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Pod]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              namespaces:
                - kube-system
                - kube-public
                - kyverno
      preconditions:
        any:
          - key: "{{ request.object.spec.initContainers[] | length(@) }}"
            operator: GreaterThanOrEquals
            value: "1"
      validate:
        message: >-
          Init container image must specify an explicit version tag, not ':latest'
          or no tag. Pin to a semantic version tag or an immutable digest to ensure
          rollbacks restore the exact known-good binary.
            image: my-registry/init-tool:v2.0.1
        pattern:
          spec:
            initContainers:
              - image: "*:*"
                (image): "!*:latest"

# Start in audit mode to identify existing violations before enforcing.
#
# Switching to enforce without auditing first will block all Deployments, Services,
# and Namespaces that are missing the required labels — including system resources
# created by operators. Run `kubectl get policyreport -A` after deploying in audit
# mode and remediate violations before changing validationFailureAction to Enforce.
#
# Labels are required because:
#   - Prometheus ServiceMonitor selectors use `app` to discover scrape targets
#   - Grafana dashboards use `version` / `app.kubernetes.io/version` to filter by release
#   - The `environment` namespace label is used by Kyverno's own namespace selectors
#     (e.g. disallow-latest-tag.yaml) to apply stricter rules in production
#
# Reference: cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml (Deployment labels)
#            docs/decisions/ADR-001-folder-structure.md (organisational standards)
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Standard Labels on Deployments, Services, and Namespaces
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Metadata
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: low
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Deployments must carry 'app' and 'version' (or 'app.kubernetes.io/version') labels.
      Services must carry an 'app' label. Namespaces must carry an 'environment' label
      with a value of dev, staging, or production. These labels power Prometheus
      ServiceMonitor discovery, Grafana dashboard variable filtering, and Kyverno
      namespace-scoped policy overrides. Monitor violations with:
        kubectl get policyreport -A
spec:
  # Audit — not Enforce — so existing workloads are not blocked during roll-out.
  # Graduate to Enforce once `kubectl get policyreport -A` shows zero violations.
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    # ── Rule 1: Deployments must have app + version labels ───────────────────
    - name: check-deployment-labels
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Deployment]
      validate:
        message: >-
          Deployment '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' is missing required labels.
          Labels are used by Prometheus ServiceMonitor selectors and Grafana dashboards
          to identify and filter workloads by name and release version.
          Add to metadata.labels:
            app: <service-name>
            version: "1.0.0"            # or app.kubernetes.io/version: "1.0.0"
          Reference pattern: cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
        anyPattern:
          # Accept either the short 'version' key or the fully-qualified k8s label convention.
          - metadata:
              labels:
                app: "?*"
                version: "?*"
          - metadata:
              labels:
                app: "?*"
                app.kubernetes.io/version: "?*"

    # ── Rule 2: Namespaces must have an environment label ────────────────────
    - name: check-namespace-environment-label
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Namespace]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              names:
                - kube-system
                - kube-public
                - kube-node-lease
                - kyverno
                - monitoring
                - default
      validate:
        message: >-
          Namespace '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' must have an
          'environment' label with value 'dev', 'staging', or 'production'.
          This label is used by Kyverno policies (e.g. disallow-latest-tag.yaml)
          to apply stricter admission controls in production namespaces, and by
          Grafana dashboards to filter metrics by environment.
          Add to metadata.labels:
            environment: production   # or: dev | staging
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              environment: "dev | staging | production"

    # ── Rule 3: Services must have an app label ──────────────────────────────
    - name: check-service-app-label
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Service]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              namespaces:
                - kube-system
                - kube-public
                - monitoring
      validate:
        message: >-
          Service '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' must have an 'app' label.
          Prometheus ServiceMonitor resources use label selectors to discover Services
          to scrape. Without the 'app' label, metrics collection will silently break
          when a ServiceMonitor is added later.
          Add to metadata.labels:
            app: <service-name>
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: "?*"

# Audit only — enforcing would break many legacy workloads. Review violations in the
# Kyverno policy report before considering graduation to enforce mode.
#
# This policy targets Deployments with more than 1 replica because:
#   - Single-replica workloads (batch jobs, dev instances) have lower availability
#     requirements and may not yet have health endpoints.
#   - Multi-replica workloads are load-balanced; without a readinessProbe, a
#     restarting or slow-to-start container will receive traffic before it is ready,
#     causing errors for end users. Without a livenessProbe, a deadlocked container
#     will never be restarted automatically.
#
# Reference pattern: cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
#   readinessProbe — httpGet /health/ready:8080, periodSeconds: 10
#   livenessProbe  — httpGet /health/live:8080,  periodSeconds: 20
#
# Monitor audit violations with: kubectl get policyreport -A
# View details: kubectl describe polr -n <namespace> | grep -A 10 "require-liveness"
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-liveness-readiness
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Liveness and Readiness Probes for Multi-Replica Deployments
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Reliability
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Deployments with more than 1 replica must define both a livenessProbe and a
      readinessProbe on every container. Without a readinessProbe, Kubernetes routes
      traffic to containers before they are ready. Without a livenessProbe, deadlocked
      containers run indefinitely without being restarted. This policy is in audit mode
      to avoid blocking legacy workloads — review violations with:
        kubectl get policyreport -A
spec:
  # Audit only — see comment at the top of this file.
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    # ── Rule 1: containers must have a readinessProbe ────────────────────────
    - name: check-readiness-probe
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Deployment]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              namespaces:
                - kube-system
                - kube-public
      # Only apply to Deployments whose replica count is greater than 1.
      # Single-replica workloads are excluded to reduce noise for dev/batch workloads.
      preconditions:
        any:
          - key: "{{ request.object.spec.replicas }}"
            operator: GreaterThan
            value: 1
      validate:
        message: >-
          Deployment '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' has {{ request.object.spec.replicas }}
          replicas but at least one container is missing a readinessProbe.
          Without a readinessProbe, Kubernetes sends traffic to containers before they
          are ready, causing errors for clients during rolling updates.
          Add a readinessProbe to every container. Example (adjust path and port):
            readinessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /health/ready
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 5
              periodSeconds: 10
          Reference pattern: cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
        pattern:
          spec:
            template:
              spec:
                containers:
                  - readinessProbe:
                      (httpGet | tcpSocket | exec): "?*"

    # ── Rule 2: containers must have a livenessProbe ─────────────────────────
    - name: check-liveness-probe
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Deployment]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              namespaces:
                - kube-system
                - kube-public
      preconditions:
        any:
          - key: "{{ request.object.spec.replicas }}"
            operator: GreaterThan
            value: 1
      validate:
        message: >-
          Deployment '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' has {{ request.object.spec.replicas }}
          replicas but at least one container is missing a livenessProbe.
          Without a livenessProbe, a deadlocked or crashed container will not be
          automatically restarted, causing silent degradation in a multi-replica service.
          Add a livenessProbe to every container. Example (adjust path and port):
            livenessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /health/live
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 15
              periodSeconds: 20
          Reference pattern: cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
        pattern:
          spec:
            template:
              spec:
                containers:
                  - livenessProbe:
                      (httpGet | tcpSocket | exec): "?*"

# Enforces the pod-level and container-level security context demonstrated in:
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
#
# That file sets:
#   spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true
#   spec.containers[].securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
#
# This policy ensures every Pod in the cluster matches that baseline.
# Violations are blocked at admission so misconfigured workloads never start.
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-non-root
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Non-Root Containers
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: high
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Pods must run as a non-root user and containers must not allow privilege
      escalation. Running as root grants unnecessary OS capabilities and makes
      container escape vulnerabilities significantly more dangerous. Set
      spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true and
      spec.containers[].securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation: false.
      See cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml for the reference pattern.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Enforce
  background: true
  rules:
    # ── Rule 1: pod-level securityContext ────────────────────────────────────
    - name: check-pod-run-as-non-root
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Pod]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              namespaces:
                - kube-system   # System components may legitimately run as root.
                - kube-public   # Public config namespace; not a workload namespace.
                - monitoring    # kube-prometheus-stack node-exporter requires host access.
      validate:
        message: >-
          Pod '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' must set
          spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true.
          Running as root broadens the blast radius of any container escape.
          Reference pattern: cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
          Add to your pod spec:
            securityContext:
              runAsNonRoot: true
              runAsUser: 1000   # any non-zero UID
              fsGroup: 2000
        pattern:
          spec:
            securityContext:
              runAsNonRoot: true

    # ── Rule 2: container-level allowPrivilegeEscalation ─────────────────────
    - name: check-container-no-privilege-escalation
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Pod]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              namespaces:
                - kube-system
                - kube-public
                - monitoring
      validate:
        message: >-
          All containers in Pod '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' must set
          securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation: false.
          This prevents a process inside the container from gaining more privileges
          than its parent, limiting the impact of exploited vulnerabilities.
          Reference pattern: cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
          Add to every container:
            securityContext:
              allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
              capabilities:
                drop: ["ALL"]
        pattern:
          spec:
            containers:
              - securityContext:
                  allowPrivilegeEscalation: false

# Warns when containers do not use a read-only root filesystem.
#
# This policy uses 'warn' rather than 'enforce' because:
#   1. Some applications write to paths like /tmp, /var/run, or /app/cache at runtime.
#      These are legitimate write requirements that cannot always be eliminated immediately.
#   2. The correct migration path is to add an emptyDir volume for each writable path
#      (see the exception pattern in the message below), not to disable the policy.
#   3. Teams should migrate progressively: audit first, add emptyDir mounts, then
#      graduate to enforce once all workloads in the namespace are compliant.
#
# The reference deployment already uses this pattern correctly:
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml — readOnlyRootFilesystem: true with /tmp emptyDir
#   cd/helm/webapp/templates/deployment.yaml — same pattern for Helm-deployed workloads
#
# Monitor warnings with: kubectl get policyreport -A
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-readonly-filesystem
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Read-Only Root Filesystem
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Containers should set securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem: true to prevent
      attackers from writing malicious files to the container's root filesystem.
      Applications that need writable directories (e.g. /tmp) should mount emptyDir
      volumes instead of relying on a writable root filesystem. This policy is in
      warn mode to allow progressive adoption — violations are logged but not blocked.
      Monitor with: kubectl get policyreport -A
spec:
  # Warn — not Enforce. Violations are recorded in PolicyReports and surfaced in
  # the Kyverno admission webhook response as warnings, but the resource is admitted.
  # Change to Enforce once all workloads in a namespace are compliant.
  validationFailureAction: Warn
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: check-readonly-root-filesystem
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Pod]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              namespaces:
                - kube-system
                - kube-public
                - monitoring
      validate:
        message: >-
          Container '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' should set
          securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem: true.
          A writable root filesystem allows attackers to modify binaries and write
          persistence mechanisms if the container is compromised.
          If your application writes to /tmp or another path at runtime, mount an
          emptyDir volume instead of keeping the root filesystem writable:
            containers:
              - name: app
                securityContext:
                  readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
                volumeMounts:
                  - name: tmp
                    mountPath: /tmp
            volumes:
              - name: tmp
                emptyDir: {}
          Reference pattern: cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
        pattern:
          spec:
            containers:
              - securityContext:
                  readOnlyRootFilesystem: true

# Enforces the resource requests and limits demonstrated in:
#   cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
#
# That file sets:
#   resources.requests:  cpu: "100m",  memory: "128Mi"
#   resources.limits:    cpu: "500m",  memory: "512Mi"
#
# Use those values as a starting point; adjust per workload profile.
# Without limits, a runaway container can exhaust node resources and evict neighbours.
# Without requests, the scheduler cannot make accurate placement decisions.
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-resource-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Container Resource Requests and Limits
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Resource Management
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      All containers must declare CPU and memory requests and limits.
      Requests allow the scheduler to make accurate placement decisions;
      limits protect nodes from runaway processes. The reference values in
      cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml (requests: 100m/128Mi,
      limits: 500m/512Mi) are a reasonable starting point — tune to your
      workload's actual profile using VPA recommendations or load-test data.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Enforce
  background: true
  rules:
    # ── Rule 1: main containers — full requests + limits ─────────────────────
    - name: check-container-resource-requests-and-limits
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Pod]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              namespaces:
                - kube-system   # System DaemonSets and static pods have varied requirements.
      validate:
        message: >-
          Container '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' is missing resource requests
          or limits. Without them the scheduler cannot place the Pod accurately and
          a runaway process can evict neighbours on the same node.
          Add to every container in your Pod spec (adjust values to your workload):
            resources:
              requests:
                cpu: "100m"
                memory: "128Mi"
              limits:
                cpu: "500m"
                memory: "512Mi"
          Reference pattern: cd/kubernetes/_base/deployment.yaml
        pattern:
          spec:
            containers:
              - resources:
                  requests:
                    memory: "?*"
                    cpu: "?*"
                  limits:
                    memory: "?*"
                    cpu: "?*"

    # ── Rule 2: init containers — memory limits required, CPU limits optional ─
    #
    # CPU limits are intentionally excluded for init containers. An init container
    # runs to completion before the main container starts; if it is CPU-throttled
    # it can dramatically slow pod start-up (e.g. database migrations, key generation).
    # Memory limits are still required to prevent OOM conditions at init time.
    - name: check-init-container-memory-requests-and-limits
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Pod]
      exclude:
        any:
          - resources:
              namespaces:
                - kube-system
      preconditions:
        any:
          - key: "{{ request.object.spec.initContainers[] | length(@) }}"
            operator: GreaterThanOrEquals
            value: "1"
      validate:
        message: >-
          Init container '{{ request.object.metadata.name }}' must set
          resources.requests.memory, resources.requests.cpu, and
          resources.limits.memory. CPU limits are not required for init containers
          (throttling would unnecessarily slow pod start-up), but memory limits
          are mandatory to prevent OOM at initialisation time.
        pattern:
          spec:
            initContainers:
              - resources:
                  requests:
                    memory: "?*"
                    cpu: "?*"
                  limits:
                    memory: "?*"

﻿# EditorConfig — consistent coding style across editors and IDEs
# https://editorconfig.org

root = true

[*]
indent_style = space
indent_size = 2
end_of_line = lf
charset = utf-8
trim_trailing_whitespace = true
insert_final_newline = true

[*.{cs,csproj}]
indent_size = 4

[*.{py}]
indent_size = 4

[*.{java}]
indent_size = 4

[*.md]
trim_trailing_whitespace = false

[Makefile]
indent_style = tab

﻿<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<!-- .NET Test Run Settings — coverage configuration -->
<!-- Usage: dotnet test --settings .runsettings -->
<RunSettings>
  <DataCollectionRunSettings>
    <DataCollectors>
      <DataCollector friendlyName="XPlat Code Coverage">
        <Configuration>
          <Format>opencover,cobertura</Format>
          <Exclude>
            [*]*Migrations*
            [*]*Program
            [*]*Startup
            [*.Tests]*
          </Exclude>
          <ExcludeByAttribute>GeneratedCodeAttribute,CompilerGeneratedAttribute</ExcludeByAttribute>
          <IncludeTestAssembly>false</IncludeTestAssembly>
        </Configuration>
      </DataCollector>
    </DataCollectors>
  </DataCollectionRunSettings>

  <RunConfiguration>
    <ResultsDirectory>./TestResults</ResultsDirectory>
  </RunConfiguration>
</RunSettings>

﻿{
  "semi": true,
