}

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: flake8 Configuration
# WHEN TO USE: Python projects not yet migrated to ruff
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Exclusions and per-file-ignores
# RELATED FILES: quality/python/pyproject.toml (ruff config)
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

[flake8]
max-line-length = 100
extend-ignore = E203, W503
exclude =
    .git,
    __pycache__,
    venv,
    .venv,
    migrations,
    *.egg-info
per-file-ignores =
    tests/*: S101

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Python Project Configuration (pyproject.toml)
# WHEN TO USE: Python projects — covers ruff, black, mypy, pytest, coverage
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Adjust paths, versions, and rules for your project
# RELATED FILES: quality/python/.flake8
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Python project configuration
# ruff, black, mypy, pytest all configured here

[tool.ruff]
target-version = "py312"
line-length = 100

[tool.ruff.lint]
select = [
    "E",    # pycodestyle errors
    "W",    # pycodestyle warnings
    "F",    # pyflakes
    "I",    # isort
    "B",    # flake8-bugbear
    "C4",   # flake8-comprehensions
    "S",    # flake8-bandit (security)
    "UP",   # pyupgrade
]
ignore = ["S101"]  # Allow assert in tests

[tool.ruff.lint.per-file-ignores]
"tests/**/*.py" = ["S", "B"]

[tool.black]
line-length = 100
target-version = ["py311", "py312"]

[tool.mypy]
python_version = "3.12"
warn_return_any = true
warn_unused_configs = true
disallow_untyped_defs = true
disallow_incomplete_defs = true
check_untyped_defs = true
no_implicit_optional = true

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]
addopts = "-v --tb=short"

[tool.coverage.run]
source = ["."]
omit = ["tests/*", "venv/*", ".venv/*"]

[tool.coverage.report]
fail_under = 80
show_missing = true

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: SonarQube / SonarCloud Project Configuration
# WHEN TO USE: Any project using SonarQube for code quality
# PREREQUISITES: SonarQube or SonarCloud project created
# WHAT TO CHANGE: projectKey, projectName, language-specific report paths
# RELATED FILES: ci/github-actions/dotnet/sonar-scan.yml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# SonarQube Project Configuration Template
# Place at project root and configure in SonarQube / SonarCloud

# Note 1: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
sonar.projectKey=<PROJECT_KEY>
# Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
sonar.projectName=<PROJECT_NAME>
# Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
sonar.projectVersion=1.0

# Source encoding
# Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
sonar.sourceEncoding=UTF-8

# ── Language-specific settings ────────────────────────────────────────────
# .NET
# sonar.cs.opencover.reportsPaths=**/coverage.opencover.xml
# sonar.cs.vstest.reportsPaths=**/TestResults/*.trx

# JavaScript / TypeScript
# sonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths=coverage/lcov.info

# Python
# sonar.python.coverage.reportPaths=coverage.xml
# sonar.python.xunit.reportPath=test-results.xml

# ── Exclusions ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Note 5: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
sonar.exclusions=**/node_modules/**,**/dist/**,**/bin/**,**/obj/**,**/*.spec.*,**/migrations/**

# Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
sonar.coverage.exclusions=**/*Tests*/**,**/Program.cs,**/Startup.cs

# ── Quality Gate ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Configure Quality Gates in SonarQube UI:
# - Coverage >= 80%
# - Duplicated Lines <= 3%
# - Maintainability Rating = A
# - Reliability Rating = A
# - Security Rating = A

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: External Secrets Operator — AWS SecretStore (IRSA)
# WHEN TO USE: You are running on EKS and want ExternalSecrets to
#   pull secrets from AWS Secrets Manager using IAM Roles for
#   Service Accounts (IRSA) — no static AWS credentials in the cluster.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   1. External Secrets Operator installed:
#        helm repo add external-secrets https://charts.external-secrets.io
#        helm install external-secrets external-secrets/external-secrets \
#          -n external-secrets-system --create-namespace
#   2. IRSA role created with secretsmanager:GetSecretValue permission
#      and the trust policy allowing the ESO service account to assume it.
#      See: https://external-secrets.io/latest/provider/aws-secrets-manager/
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: secrets/external-secrets/example-external-secret.yaml
#                secrets/rotation/aws-rotation.yml
#                terraform/aws-eks/main.tf
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ClusterSecretStore
metadata:
  name: aws-secret-store                        # <-- CHANGE THIS: one store per AWS account/region pair
  annotations:
    description: "Pulls secrets from AWS Secrets Manager via IRSA"
spec:
  provider:
    aws:
      service: SecretsManager
      region: us-east-1                         # <-- CHANGE THIS: your AWS region

      # Note 1: IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) is the preferred authentication
      # method on EKS. The External Secrets Operator service account is annotated with
      # the IAM role ARN. No static AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID is stored in the cluster.
      # The role must have the following policy:
      #   secretsmanager:GetSecretValue
      #   secretsmanager:DescribeSecret
      auth:
        jwt:
          serviceAccountRef:
            name: external-secrets               # <-- CHANGE THIS: ESO service account name (usually "external-secrets")
            namespace: external-secrets-system   # <-- CHANGE THIS: namespace where ESO is installed

---
# Note 2: The ServiceAccount below is the ESO controller's service account.
# Annotating it with the IAM role ARN tells the EKS IRSA webhook to inject
# AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE and AWS_ROLE_ARN as environment variables,
# enabling the ESO pod to assume the role without any credentials.
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: external-secrets                        # <-- CHANGE THIS: must match spec.provider.aws.auth.jwt.serviceAccountRef.name above
  namespace: external-secrets-system            # <-- CHANGE THIS
  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/external-secrets-irsa"  # <-- CHANGE THIS: your IAM role ARN

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: External Secrets Operator — Azure SecretStore (Workload Identity)
# WHEN TO USE: You are running on AKS and want ExternalSecrets to
#   pull secrets from Azure Key Vault using Workload Identity
#   (formerly AAD Pod Identity v2) — no client secrets in the cluster.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   1. External Secrets Operator installed (see aws-secret-store.yaml)
#   2. AKS cluster has Workload Identity enabled:
#        az aks update --enable-oidc-issuer --enable-workload-identity \
#          -g <rg> -n <cluster>
#   3. Managed Identity created with Key Vault Secrets User role on your vault.
#   4. Federated credential configured linking the Managed Identity to the
#      ESO Kubernetes ServiceAccount in the namespace where ESO runs.
#      See: https://external-secrets.io/latest/provider/azure-key-vault/
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: secrets/external-secrets/example-external-secret.yaml
#                secrets/rotation/azure-rotation.yml
#                terraform/azure-aks/main.tf
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ClusterSecretStore
metadata:
  name: azure-secret-store                      # <-- CHANGE THIS
  annotations:
    description: "Pulls secrets from Azure Key Vault via Workload Identity"
spec:
  provider:
    azurekv:
      authType: WorkloadIdentity                # Note 1: WorkloadIdentity uses federated OIDC tokens — no client secret or certificate stored in the cluster

      # Note 2: The vaultUrl must be the full HTTPS URL of your Key Vault.
      # Find it in the Azure Portal: Key Vault → Overview → Vault URI
      vaultUrl: "https://my-keyvault.vault.azure.net"  # <-- CHANGE THIS

      serviceAccountRef:
        name: external-secrets                  # <-- CHANGE THIS: ESO service account name
        namespace: external-secrets-system      # <-- CHANGE THIS

---
# Note 3: The ServiceAccount must carry the azure.workload.identity/client-id
# annotation. The AKS Workload Identity mutating webhook injects the OIDC
# token into the ESO pod, which exchanges it for an Azure access token via
# the federated credential configured on the Managed Identity.
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: external-secrets                        # <-- CHANGE THIS
  namespace: external-secrets-system            # <-- CHANGE THIS
  annotations:
    azure.workload.identity/client-id: "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"  # <-- CHANGE THIS: Managed Identity client ID
  labels:
    azure.workload.identity/use: "true"

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: External Secrets Operator — ExternalSecret Example
# WHEN TO USE: After a ClusterSecretStore is configured (see the
#   aws/azure/gcp-secret-store.yaml files), this creates a
#   Kubernetes Secret that is automatically synced from the
#   upstream secret store.
#
# HOW IT WORKS:
#   ESO watches ExternalSecret resources. On every refreshInterval
#   it fetches the specified keys from the upstream store and writes
#   them into a Kubernetes Secret. If the upstream secret value
#   changes (e.g., after rotation), ESO updates the Kubernetes Secret
#   automatically. Pods pick up the new value on the next restart.
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: secrets/external-secrets/aws-secret-store.yaml
#                secrets/external-secrets/azure-secret-store.yaml
#                secrets/external-secrets/gcp-secret-store.yaml
#                secrets/rotation/
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: app-db-credentials                      # <-- CHANGE THIS: the name of the Kubernetes Secret to create
  namespace: default                            # <-- CHANGE THIS: namespace where the consuming pods run
spec:
  # Note 1: refreshInterval controls how often ESO checks the upstream store for
  # changes. A lower interval detects rotated secrets faster, but increases API
  # call volume to the secret store. 1 hour is a sensible default for most apps.
  # After a rotation event you can force an immediate sync with:
  #   kubectl annotate externalsecret app-db-credentials \
  #     force-sync=$(date +%s) --overwrite
  refreshInterval: 1h                           # <-- CHANGE THIS: balance freshness vs. API cost

  secretStoreRef:
    name: aws-secret-store                      # <-- CHANGE THIS: name of your ClusterSecretStore (aws/azure/gcp)
    kind: ClusterSecretStore

  target:
    name: app-db-credentials                    # <-- CHANGE THIS: Kubernetes Secret name (usually same as ExternalSecret name)
    creationPolicy: Owner                       # Note 2: Owner means ESO owns the Secret; it will be deleted if the ExternalSecret is deleted

  # Note 3: `data` maps individual keys from the upstream secret to keys in
  # the Kubernetes Secret. Use this when your upstream secret is a JSON object
  # and you want specific keys extracted.
  data:
    - secretKey: DB_HOST                        # Key in the Kubernetes Secret
      remoteRef:
        key: my-app/production/db               # <-- CHANGE THIS: path/name in Secrets Manager, Key Vault, or Secret Manager
        property: host                          # <-- CHANGE THIS: JSON key within the upstream secret value

    - secretKey: DB_PORT
      remoteRef:
        key: my-app/production/db              # <-- CHANGE THIS
        property: port                         # <-- CHANGE THIS

    - secretKey: DB_NAME
      remoteRef:
        key: my-app/production/db
        property: name

    - secretKey: DB_USER
      remoteRef:
        key: my-app/production/db
        property: username

    - secretKey: DB_PASSWORD
      remoteRef:
        key: my-app/production/db
        property: password

  # Note 4: Alternatively, use `dataFrom` to extract ALL keys from a JSON secret
  # at once. Uncomment the block below and remove the `data` block above.
  # dataFrom:
  #   - extract:
  #       key: my-app/production/db             # <-- CHANGE THIS
  #
  # The upstream secret must be a flat JSON object like:
  # { "DB_HOST": "...", "DB_PORT": "5432", ... }
  # All top-level keys are extracted and become Secret data keys.

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: External Secrets Operator — GCP SecretStore (Workload Identity)
# WHEN TO USE: You are running on GKE and want ExternalSecrets to
#   pull secrets from Google Secret Manager using GKE Workload Identity
#   — no service account key JSON stored in the cluster.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   1. External Secrets Operator installed (see aws-secret-store.yaml)
#   2. GKE cluster has Workload Identity enabled:
#        gcloud container clusters update <cluster> \
#          --workload-pool=<project>.svc.id.goog
#   3. GCP service account created with secretmanager.secretAccessor role.
#   4. IAM binding created linking the GCP SA to the Kubernetes SA:
#        gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding \
#          <gcp-sa>@<project>.iam.gserviceaccount.com \
#          --role roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser \
#          --member "serviceAccount:<project>.svc.id.goog[external-secrets-system/external-secrets]"
#      See: https://external-secrets.io/latest/provider/google-secrets-manager/
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: secrets/external-secrets/example-external-secret.yaml
#                secrets/rotation/gcp-rotation.yml
#                terraform/gcp-gke/ (if present)
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ClusterSecretStore
metadata:
  name: gcp-secret-store                        # <-- CHANGE THIS
  annotations:
    description: "Pulls secrets from Google Secret Manager via Workload Identity"
spec:
  provider:
    gcpsm:
      projectID: my-gcp-project                 # <-- CHANGE THIS: your GCP project ID

      # Note 1: WorkloadIdentity binds the Kubernetes ServiceAccount to a GCP
      # service account via the projected token volume. The ESO pod presents the
      # Kubernetes OIDC token to GCP STS and receives a short-lived access token.
      auth:
        workloadIdentity:
          clusterLocation: us-central1           # <-- CHANGE THIS: your GKE region
          clusterName: my-cluster                # <-- CHANGE THIS
          clusterProjectID: my-gcp-project       # <-- CHANGE THIS
          serviceAccountRef:
            name: external-secrets               # <-- CHANGE THIS
            namespace: external-secrets-system   # <-- CHANGE THIS

---
# Note 2: Annotate the Kubernetes ServiceAccount with the GCP service account
# email. GKE's Workload Identity admission controller uses this annotation to
# know which GCP service account to impersonate.
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: external-secrets                        # <-- CHANGE THIS
  namespace: external-secrets-system            # <-- CHANGE THIS
  annotations:
    iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account: "external-secrets@my-gcp-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"  # <-- CHANGE THIS

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Secret Rotation — AWS Secrets Manager
# WHEN TO USE: Rotate a secret in AWS Secrets Manager on a schedule
#   or on demand, then force the External Secrets Operator to sync the
#   new value and restart the affected Deployment.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - INFRA_AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID / INFRA_AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY in GitHub Secrets
#     (or use OIDC — see ci/github-actions/_shared/oidc-aws.yml)
#   - KUBECONFIG_BASE64: base64-encoded kubeconfig for the target cluster
#   - The secret must have rotation enabled in Secrets Manager (optional
#     for manual rotation, required for Lambda-based auto-rotation)
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: secrets/external-secrets/aws-secret-store.yaml
#                secrets/external-secrets/example-external-secret.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

name: Rotate AWS Secret

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 2 1 * *"        # <-- CHANGE THIS: rotate on the 1st of each month at 02:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      secret_name:
        description: "AWS Secrets Manager secret name/ARN to rotate"
        required: true
        default: "my-app/production/db"   # <-- CHANGE THIS
      environment:
        description: "Target environment"
        required: true
        default: "production"
        type: choice
        options:
          - production
          - staging
          - development

permissions:
  contents: read
  id-token: write             # Required for OIDC authentication to AWS

jobs:
  rotate:
    name: Rotate Secret
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    environment: ${{ github.event.inputs.environment || 'production' }}

    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 1: For OIDC-based auth (no static keys), replace this step with:
      # - name: Configure AWS credentials
      #   uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
      #   with:
      #     role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/github-actions-rotation
      #     aws-region: us-east-1
      - name: Configure AWS credentials
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          aws-access-key-id: ${{ secrets.INFRA_AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}         # <-- CHANGE THIS
          aws-secret-access-key: ${{ secrets.INFRA_AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }} # <-- CHANGE THIS
          aws-region: us-east-1                                              # <-- CHANGE THIS

      # Note 2: `rotate-secret` triggers the Secrets Manager rotation Lambda
      # that you configure on the secret. The Lambda must be configured separately
      # (see AWS docs for the SecretsManagerRotationTemplate Lambda blueprint).
      # If you are doing manual rotation (no Lambda), replace this step with
      # a script that generates a new value and calls `put-secret-value`.
      - name: Rotate secret
        run: |
          SECRET_NAME="${{ github.event.inputs.secret_name || 'my-app/production/db' }}"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          echo "Rotating secret: ${SECRET_NAME}"
          aws secretsmanager rotate-secret \
            --secret-id "${SECRET_NAME}" \
            --rotate-immediately

          # Wait for rotation to complete (Lambda-based rotation is async)
          echo "Waiting for rotation to complete..."
          for i in $(seq 1 12); do
            STATUS=$(aws secretsmanager describe-secret \
              --secret-id "${SECRET_NAME}" \
              --query "RotationRules" \
              --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "none")
            LAST=$(aws secretsmanager describe-secret \
              --secret-id "${SECRET_NAME}" \
              --query "LastRotatedDate" \
              --output text)
            echo "  Attempt ${i}: LastRotatedDate=${LAST}"
            if [ "$LAST" != "None" ]; then
              ROTATED_EPOCH=$(date -d "${LAST}" +%s 2>/dev/null || date -j -f "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S" "${LAST}" +%s)
              NOW=$(date +%s)
              AGE=$((NOW - ROTATED_EPOCH))
              if [ "${AGE}" -lt 120 ]; then
                echo "Rotation completed."
                break
              fi
            fi
            sleep 10
          done

      # Note 3: After the upstream secret is updated, force the External Secrets
      # Operator to refresh the Kubernetes Secret immediately instead of waiting
      # for the next refreshInterval cycle.
      - name: Setup kubectl
        run: |
          echo "${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG_BASE64 }}" | base64 -d > /tmp/kubeconfig  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          echo "KUBECONFIG=/tmp/kubeconfig" >> "$GITHUB_ENV"

      - name: Force ESO sync
        run: |
          NAMESPACE="default"                          # <-- CHANGE THIS
          EXTERNAL_SECRET_NAME="app-db-credentials"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: name of the ExternalSecret resource
          kubectl annotate externalsecret "${EXTERNAL_SECRET_NAME}" \
            force-sync="$(date +%s)" \
            --overwrite \
            -n "${NAMESPACE}"
          echo "ESO sync triggered for ${EXTERNAL_SECRET_NAME}"

      # Note 4: Restart the Deployment so pods pick up the new secret value
      # from environment variables or mounted secret volumes.
      - name: Restart affected deployment
        run: |
          NAMESPACE="default"         # <-- CHANGE THIS
          DEPLOYMENT="my-app"         # <-- CHANGE THIS: name of the Deployment to restart
          kubectl rollout restart deployment/"${DEPLOYMENT}" -n "${NAMESPACE}"
          kubectl rollout status deployment/"${DEPLOYMENT}" -n "${NAMESPACE}" \
            --timeout=5m
          echo "Deployment ${DEPLOYMENT} restarted successfully."

      - name: Notify on failure
        if: failure()
        run: |
          echo "::error::Secret rotation failed. Manual intervention may be required."
          echo "Secret: ${{ github.event.inputs.secret_name }}"
          # Add Slack/Teams/PagerDuty notification here if needed
          # See notifications/ directory for templates

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Secret Rotation — Azure Key Vault
# WHEN TO USE: Rotate a secret in Azure Key Vault on a schedule
#   or on demand, then force External Secrets Operator to sync the
#   new value and restart the affected Deployment.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET, AZURE_TENANT_ID,
#     AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID in GitHub Secrets
#     (or use OIDC — see ci/github-actions/_shared/ for examples)
#   - KUBECONFIG_BASE64: base64-encoded kubeconfig for the target cluster
#   - The service principal must have Key Vault Secrets Officer role
#     on the target Key Vault
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: secrets/external-secrets/azure-secret-store.yaml
#                secrets/external-secrets/example-external-secret.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

name: Rotate Azure Key Vault Secret

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 2 1 * *"        # <-- CHANGE THIS: rotate on the 1st of each month at 02:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      secret_name:
        description: "Key Vault secret name to rotate"
        required: true
        default: "db-password"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
      environment:
        description: "Target environment"
        required: true
        default: "production"
        type: choice
        options:
          - production
          - staging
          - development

permissions:
  contents: read
  id-token: write             # Required for OIDC authentication to Azure

jobs:
  rotate:
    name: Rotate Secret
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    environment: ${{ github.event.inputs.environment || 'production' }}

    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 1: For OIDC-based auth (no static credentials), replace this with:
      # - name: Azure Login
      #   uses: azure/login@v2
      #   with:
      #     client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
      #     tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
      #     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
      - name: Azure Login
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          creds: |
            {
              "clientId": "${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}",
              "clientSecret": "${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET }}",
              "tenantId": "${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}",
              "subscriptionId": "${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}"
            }

      # Note 2: Generate a new secret value. For database passwords, use
      # openssl rand -base64 to create a cryptographically random string.
      # For API keys that cannot be randomly generated, replace this with
      # a call to the upstream API's key-rotation endpoint.
      - name: Generate new secret value
        id: generate
        run: |
          NEW_VALUE=$(openssl rand -base64 32 | tr -d '=/+' | head -c 32)
          echo "new_value=${NEW_VALUE}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "Generated new secret value (not logged)"

      - name: Set new secret in Key Vault
        run: |
          VAULT_NAME="my-keyvault"                          # <-- CHANGE THIS: your Key Vault name
          SECRET_NAME="${{ github.event.inputs.secret_name || 'db-password' }}"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          az keyvault secret set \
            --vault-name "${VAULT_NAME}" \
            --name "${SECRET_NAME}" \
            --value "${{ steps.generate.outputs.new_value }}"
          echo "Secret '${SECRET_NAME}' updated in Key Vault '${VAULT_NAME}'"

      # Note 3: Force ESO sync so the Kubernetes Secret picks up the new value
      # immediately rather than waiting for the refreshInterval (default: 1h).
      - name: Setup kubectl
        run: |
          echo "${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG_BASE64 }}" | base64 -d > /tmp/kubeconfig  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          echo "KUBECONFIG=/tmp/kubeconfig" >> "$GITHUB_ENV"

      - name: Force ESO sync
        run: |
          NAMESPACE="default"                          # <-- CHANGE THIS
          EXTERNAL_SECRET_NAME="app-db-credentials"   # <-- CHANGE THIS
          kubectl annotate externalsecret "${EXTERNAL_SECRET_NAME}" \
            force-sync="$(date +%s)" \
            --overwrite \
            -n "${NAMESPACE}"
          echo "ESO sync triggered."

      # Note 4: If the rotated secret is a database password, you must update
      # the database itself as well. For managed databases (Azure Database for
      # PostgreSQL, SQL Database), add an `az` command here to reset the
      # admin/app password before restarting the pods.
      - name: Restart affected deployment
        run: |
          NAMESPACE="default"     # <-- CHANGE THIS
          DEPLOYMENT="my-app"     # <-- CHANGE THIS
          kubectl rollout restart deployment/"${DEPLOYMENT}" -n "${NAMESPACE}"
          kubectl rollout status deployment/"${DEPLOYMENT}" -n "${NAMESPACE}" \
            --timeout=5m
          echo "Deployment ${DEPLOYMENT} restarted successfully."

      - name: Azure Logout
        if: always()
        run: az logout

      - name: Notify on failure
        if: failure()
        run: |
          echo "::error::Secret rotation failed. Manual intervention may be required."

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Secret Rotation — GCP Secret Manager
# WHEN TO USE: Rotate a secret in GCP Secret Manager on a schedule
#   or on demand, then force External Secrets Operator to sync the
#   new value and restart the affected Deployment.
#
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - Workload Identity Federation configured for GitHub Actions
#     (see: https://github.com/google-github-actions/auth#workload-identity-federation)
#   - The GCP service account must have secretmanager.secretVersionAdder and
#     secretmanager.viewer roles on the target secret
#   - KUBECONFIG_BASE64: base64-encoded kubeconfig for the target cluster
#
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: secrets/external-secrets/gcp-secret-store.yaml
#                secrets/external-secrets/example-external-secret.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

name: Rotate GCP Secret

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 2 1 * *"        # <-- CHANGE THIS: rotate on the 1st of each month at 02:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      secret_name:
        description: "GCP Secret Manager secret ID to rotate"
        required: true
        default: "my-app-db-password"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
      environment:
        description: "Target environment"
        required: true
        default: "production"
        type: choice
        options:
          - production
          - staging
          - development

permissions:
  contents: read
  id-token: write             # Required for Workload Identity Federation

jobs:
  rotate:
    name: Rotate Secret
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    environment: ${{ github.event.inputs.environment || 'production' }}

    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Note 1: google-github-actions/auth exchanges the GitHub OIDC token for
      # a short-lived Google access token via Workload Identity Federation.
      # No GCP service account key JSON is stored in GitHub Secrets.
      - name: Authenticate to GCP
        uses: google-github-actions/auth@v2
        with:
          workload_identity_provider: "projects/123456789/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github/providers/github"  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          service_account: "secret-rotator@my-gcp-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"  # <-- CHANGE THIS

      - name: Setup gcloud CLI
        uses: google-github-actions/setup-gcloud@v2

      # Note 2: Generate a new random secret value. For non-random credentials
      # (API tokens, database admin passwords managed by the DB itself), replace
      # the openssl command with a call to the relevant provider's rotation API.
      - name: Generate new secret value
        id: generate
        run: |
          NEW_VALUE=$(openssl rand -base64 32 | tr -d '=/+' | head -c 32)
          echo "new_value=${NEW_VALUE}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "Generated new secret value (not logged)"

      # Note 3: GCP Secret Manager uses an immutable versioning model.
      # `gcloud secrets versions add` creates a new version (e.g., version/3).
      # The previous version (e.g., version/2) remains accessible until
      # explicitly disabled or destroyed. We disable the previous version
      # AFTER the new one is confirmed, providing a safe rollback window.
      - name: Add new secret version
        id: add_version
        run: |
          PROJECT_ID="my-gcp-project"                                          # <-- CHANGE THIS
          SECRET_NAME="${{ github.event.inputs.secret_name || 'my-app-db-password' }}"  # <-- CHANGE THIS

          # Get the current latest version before adding the new one
          PREV_VERSION=$(gcloud secrets versions list "${SECRET_NAME}" \
            --project="${PROJECT_ID}" \
            --filter="state=enabled" \
            --sort-by="~name" \
            --limit=1 \
            --format="value(name)" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

          echo "prev_version=${PREV_VERSION}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

          # Add new version
          printf '%s' "${{ steps.generate.outputs.new_value }}" | \
            gcloud secrets versions add "${SECRET_NAME}" \
              --project="${PROJECT_ID}" \
              --data-file=-

          echo "New version added for secret: ${SECRET_NAME}"

      - name: Disable previous secret version
        if: steps.add_version.outputs.prev_version != ''
        run: |
          PROJECT_ID="my-gcp-project"       # <-- CHANGE THIS
          SECRET_NAME="${{ github.event.inputs.secret_name || 'my-app-db-password' }}"
          PREV_VERSION="${{ steps.add_version.outputs.prev_version }}"

          echo "Disabling previous version: ${PREV_VERSION}"
          gcloud secrets versions disable "${PREV_VERSION}" \
            --project="${PROJECT_ID}" \
            --secret="${SECRET_NAME}"
          echo "Previous version disabled (not destroyed; available for rollback)."

      # Note 4: Force ESO sync — adds the `force-sync` annotation to the
      # ExternalSecret resource. ESO detects the annotation change and re-reads
      # the secret from GCP Secret Manager immediately.
      - name: Setup kubectl
        run: |
          echo "${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG_BASE64 }}" | base64 -d > /tmp/kubeconfig  # <-- CHANGE THIS
          echo "KUBECONFIG=/tmp/kubeconfig" >> "$GITHUB_ENV"

      - name: Force ESO sync
        run: |
          NAMESPACE="default"                          # <-- CHANGE THIS
          EXTERNAL_SECRET_NAME="app-db-credentials"   # <-- CHANGE THIS
          kubectl annotate externalsecret "${EXTERNAL_SECRET_NAME}" \
            force-sync="$(date +%s)" \
            --overwrite \
            -n "${NAMESPACE}"
          echo "ESO sync triggered."

      - name: Restart affected deployment
        run: |
          NAMESPACE="default"     # <-- CHANGE THIS
          DEPLOYMENT="my-app"     # <-- CHANGE THIS
          kubectl rollout restart deployment/"${DEPLOYMENT}" -n "${NAMESPACE}"
          kubectl rollout status deployment/"${DEPLOYMENT}" -n "${NAMESPACE}" \
            --timeout=5m
          echo "Deployment ${DEPLOYMENT} restarted successfully."

      - name: Notify on failure
        if: failure()
        run: |
          echo "::error::Secret rotation failed. Manual intervention may be required."

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Trivy Container Scan

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 6 * * *"   # Daily scan

# Note 5: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
jobs:
  trivy:
    # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build image
        # Note 8: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        run: docker build -t my-app:scan .

      - name: Run Trivy (table output)
        # Note 9: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@0.28.0
        with:
          # Note 10: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          image-ref: my-app:scan
          format: table
          # Note 11: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
          exit-code: "1"
          severity: "CRITICAL"

      # Note 12: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Run Trivy (SARIF upload)
        if: always()
        # Note 13: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@0.28.0
        with:
          image-ref: my-app:scan
          format: sarif
          output: trivy-results.sarif

        if: always()
        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
        with:
          sarif_file: trivy-results.sarif

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Checkov IaC Security Scan
# WHEN TO USE: Advisory scanning across Terraform, Kubernetes, Helm, Compose, and Docker assets
# PREREQUISITES: Infrastructure-as-code files committed in the scanned directories
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Trigger branches, scan directories, and soft-fail behaviour
# RELATED FILES: security/iac-scanning/tfsec.yml, security/iac-scanning/README.md
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Checkov IaC Security Scan

on:
  # Note 2: Push scans keep the default branch's security signal current.
  push:
    branches: [main]  # <-- CHANGE THIS: scan your protected branch
  # Note 3: Pull request scans surface findings before infrastructure changes merge.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]  # <-- CHANGE THIS: match your review branch policy
  # Note 4: Scheduled scans catch drift when templates are not changing daily.
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 8 * * 1"  # <-- CHANGE THIS: weekly Monday 08:00 UTC

# Note 5: SARIF upload requires security-events; contents: read is enough for checkout.
permissions:
  contents: read
  security-events: write

jobs:
  # Note 6: Matrix execution scans each IaC surface independently so one parser error does not hide unrelated findings.
  checkov:
    name: Checkov (${{ matrix.label }})
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false  # Keep all scan roots reporting even if one directory is malformed.
      matrix:
        include:  # <-- CHANGE THIS: add or remove IaC roots for your repository
          - label: terraform
            directory: terraform
            category: terraform
          - label: kubernetes
            directory: cd/kubernetes
            category: kubernetes
          - label: helm
            directory: cd/helm
            category: helm
          - label: compose
            directory: compose
            category: compose
          - label: docker
            directory: docker
            category: docker
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Run Checkov
        # Note 7: Checkov gives broad policy coverage across Terraform, Kubernetes, Helm, Compose, and Docker definitions.
        uses: bridgecrewio/checkov-action@master
        with:
          directory: ${{ matrix.directory }}
          output_format: sarif  # SARIF is required for GitHub code scanning ingestion.
          output_file_path: checkov-results.sarif  # Write a predictable artifact name for the upload step.
          soft_fail: true  # <-- CHANGE THIS: set false to make findings block merges

      - name: Upload SARIF
        # Note 8: Always upload the report so advisory findings still reach the Security tab.
        if: always()
        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
        with:
          sarif_file: checkov-results.sarif
          category: checkov-${{ matrix.category }}  # Split alert streams by IaC surface in the Security tab.

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — tfsec Terraform Security Scan
# WHEN TO USE: Provider-aware Terraform misconfiguration scanning for cloud infrastructure modules
# PREREQUISITES: Terraform root modules under terraform/
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Trigger branches and Terraform directories in the matrix
# RELATED FILES: security/iac-scanning/checkov.yml, security/iac-scanning/README.md
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: tfsec Terraform Security Scan

on:
  # Note 2: Restricting by path avoids scanning when non-IaC changes land in the repository.
  push:
    branches: [main]  # <-- CHANGE THIS: scan your protected branch
    paths:
      - "terraform/**"  # <-- CHANGE THIS: path to your Terraform configs
  # Note 3: Matching PR paths keeps review noise focused on Terraform changes only.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]  # <-- CHANGE THIS: match your review branch policy
    paths:
      - "terraform/**"  # <-- CHANGE THIS: keep in sync with push paths

# Note 4: GitHub code scanning uploads require security-events write permission.
permissions:
  contents: read
  security-events: write

jobs:
  # Note 5: Matrix execution scans each Terraform root independently so one slow provider does not serialize the rest.
  tfsec:
    name: tfsec (${{ matrix.directory }})
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false  # Keep the full finding set even if one module has syntax issues.
      matrix:
        directory:  # <-- CHANGE THIS: list your Terraform root modules
          - aws-ecs
          - aws-eks
          - aws-lambda
          - azure-aks
          - azure-app-service
          - gcp-gke
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Run tfsec
        # Note 6: tfsec adds Terraform-specific checks for provider defaults, IAM posture, and cloud-service hardening.
        # It is especially useful for cloud-provider-specific issues such as encryption defaults, public exposure, and managed-service settings.
        uses: aquasecurity/tfsec-action@v1.0.0
        with:
          # Note 7: Scan one Terraform root per matrix job so SARIF results stay attributable to a module.
          working_directory: terraform/${{ matrix.directory }}
          format: sarif  # SARIF lets GitHub show findings in the Security tab.
          additional_args: --out tfsec-results --force-all-dirs  # Name the report and keep nested module checks enabled.

      - name: Upload SARIF
        # Note 8: Always upload the SARIF output so each module's advisory findings appear in GitHub Security.
        if: always()
        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
        with:
          sarif_file: terraform/${{ matrix.directory }}/tfsec-results.sarif
          category: tfsec-${{ matrix.directory }}  # Keep one code scanning category per Terraform root.

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Snyk Security Scan

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:

jobs:
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.

    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Note 5: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.

        with:

        env:
          SNYK_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.SNYK_TOKEN }}

﻿# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — Gitleaks Secret Detection
# WHEN TO USE: Every repository to prevent secret leaks in commits
# PREREQUISITES: None
# SECRETS NEEDED: GITLEAKS_LICENSE (required for GitHub org accounts)
# WHAT TO CHANGE: None (works out of the box)
# RELATED FILES: None
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: Gitleaks Secret Detection

on:
  # Note 2: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  # Note 3: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  pull_request:

jobs:
  # Note 4: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
  gitleaks:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Note 5: Pipeline structure separates concerns, helping teams test, deploy, and recover with smaller blast radius.
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        # Note 6: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0  # Full history for complete scan

      # Note 7: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
      - name: Run Gitleaks
        uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
        # Note 8: This line contributes to the system's declarative intent, helping future readers reason about behavior and change impact.
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          GITLEAKS_LICENSE: ${{ secrets.GITLEAKS_LICENSE }}  # Required for orgs

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: GitHub Actions — TruffleHog Verified Secret Detection
# WHEN TO USE: Deep pull request secret scanning with live credential verification
# PREREQUISITES: Pull requests opened from branches with accessible base and head commits
# SECRETS NEEDED: None
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Pull request branch filters, TruffleHog CLI version, and summary detail level
# RELATED FILES: security/secret-detection/gitleaks.yml, security/README.md
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# Note 1: Resource identity and metadata drive automation, selectors, and operational traceability.
name: TruffleHog Verified Secret Detection

on:
  # Note 2: PR-only is intentional here because deep history and verification are more expensive than fast push-time pattern scans.
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]  # <-- CHANGE THIS: match your protected review branch

# Note 3: Comment updates need issue or PR write access; checkout only needs contents read.
permissions:
  contents: read
  issues: write
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  # Note 4: Keep TruffleHog separate from Gitleaks so teams can tune expensive verified scanning independently.
  trufflehog:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        # Note 5: Base and head SHAs may sit outside a shallow clone, so full history avoids false negatives on PR diffs.
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Run TruffleHog enforcement scan
        # Note 6: Pinning a security action to a full SHA prevents an upstream branch move from silently changing scanner behaviour.
        uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@5474feb15b332595dd00d83b71f4689ab1860a1d
        with:
          path: ./
          # Note 7: The action maps base/head to --since-commit and --branch so only the PR diff is scanned instead of full history.
          base: ${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }}
          head: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}
          version: v3.95.2  # <-- CHANGE THIS: pin the TruffleHog CLI version instead of relying on latest
          # IMPORTANT: --only-verified reduces false positives, but it can miss secrets that cannot be live-verified such as internal APIs.
          extra_args: --only-verified  # Pair with Gitleaks for broader pattern coverage across unverifiable secret types.

      - name: Build job summary
        # Note 8: Re-running the same bounded diff in JSON mode gives reviewers a concise summary without re-scanning full repository history.
        if: always()
        id: summary
        shell: bash
        env:
          BASE_SHA: ${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }}
          HEAD_SHA: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}
        run: |
          results_file="$(mktemp)"
          summary_file="$(mktemp)"

          docker run --rm -v "$PWD:/tmp" -w /tmp \
            ghcr.io/trufflesecurity/trufflehog:v3.95.2 \
            git file:///tmp/ \
            --since-commit "$BASE_SHA" \
            --branch "$HEAD_SHA" \
            --only-verified \
            --json \
            --no-update > "$results_file" || true

          finding_count="$(jq -s 'length' "$results_file")"

          {
            echo "## TruffleHog PR Secret Scan"
            echo
            echo "- Base commit: \`$BASE_SHA\`"
            echo "- Head commit: \`$HEAD_SHA\`"
            echo "- Verified findings: **$finding_count**"
            echo

            if [ "$finding_count" -gt 0 ]; then
              echo "| Detector | Location |"
              echo "|---|---|"
              jq -r '[.DetectorName // "unknown", .SourceMetadata.Data.Git.file // .SourceMetadata.Data.Filesystem.file // .SourceMetadata.Data.Git.path // "unknown"] | @tsv' "$results_file" \
                | sort -u \
                | head -n 20 \
                | while IFS=$'\t' read -r detector location; do
                    printf '| %s | %s |\n' "$detector" "$location"
                  done
              echo
              echo "Verified secrets were detected in the PR diff. Review the workflow logs for the full scanner output and rotate any exposed credentials immediately."
            else
              echo "No verified secrets were found in the pull request diff."
            fi
          } | tee "$summary_file" >> "$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY"

          {
            echo "comment_body<<EOF"
            cat "$summary_file"
            echo "EOF"
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Comment on pull request
        # Note 9: Reusing the same markdown in the PR comment and job summary keeps reviewer context consistent.
        if: always() && github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name == github.repository
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        env:
          COMMENT_BODY: ${{ steps.summary.outputs.comment_body }}
        with:
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          script: |
            const marker = '<!-- trufflehog-pr-scan -->';
            const body = `${marker}\n${process.env.COMMENT_BODY}`;
            const { owner, repo } = context.repo;
            const issue_number = context.issue.number;
            const comments = await github.paginate(github.rest.issues.listComments, {
              owner,
              repo,
              issue_number,
            });
            const existing = comments.find((comment) => comment.user.type === 'Bot' && comment.body.includes(marker));

            if (existing) {
              await github.rest.issues.updateComment({
                owner,
                repo,
                comment_id: existing.id,
                body,
              });
            } else {
              await github.rest.issues.createComment({
                owner,
                repo,
                issue_number,
                body,
              });
            }

"""
AWS Secrets Manager — Single-User Database Rotation Lambda
===========================================================
WHEN TO USE : Rotate a database password stored in AWS Secrets Manager.
              Works for PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB.  Adapt the DB_ENGINE
              constant and the SQL statements for other engines.

HOW IT WORKS: Secrets Manager calls this Lambda at four lifecycle steps
              (createSecret → setSecret → testSecret → finishSecret).
              Each step is idempotent so a retry is safe.

PREREQUISITES:
  - The secret JSON must contain:
      { "username": "...", "password": "...",
        "host": "...", "port": 5432, "dbname": "..." }
  - Lambda must have network access to the database (VPC + SG rules).
  - Lambda execution role needs:
      secretsmanager:GetSecretValue
      secretsmanager:PutSecretValue
      secretsmanager:DescribeSecret
      secretsmanager:UpdateSecretVersionStage

WHAT TO CHANGE: Variables marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
"""

import boto3
import json
import logging
import os
import secrets
import string
import psycopg2          # pip install psycopg2-binary  # <-- CHANGE THIS: use pymysql for MySQL

logger = logging.getLogger()
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

# --- Configuration -------------------------------------------------------
DB_ENGINE    = "postgresql"   # <-- CHANGE THIS: "mysql" | "postgresql" | "mariadb"
PASSWORD_LEN = 32             # <-- CHANGE THIS: minimum 20 recommended
# Exclude characters that break DSN strings or shell quoting
EXCLUDED     = '"\'@/\\'
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------


def lambda_handler(event, context):
    """Entry point called by Secrets Manager at each rotation step."""
    arn   = event["SecretId"]
    token = event["ClientRequestToken"]
    step  = event["Step"]

    client = boto3.client("secretsmanager", region_name=os.environ["AWS_REGION"])

    metadata = client.describe_secret(SecretId=arn)
    if not metadata.get("RotationEnabled"):
        raise ValueError(f"Rotation is not enabled for secret {arn}")

    versions = metadata.get("VersionIdsToStages", {})
    if token not in versions:
        raise ValueError(f"Token {token} is not associated with secret {arn}")

    if "AWSCURRENT" in versions[token]:
        logger.info("Token is already AWSCURRENT — nothing to do")
        return
    if "AWSPENDING" not in versions[token]:
        raise ValueError(f"Token {token} is not in AWSPENDING stage")

    dispatch = {
        "createSecret": _create_secret,
        "setSecret":    _set_secret,
        "testSecret":   _test_secret,
        "finishSecret": _finish_secret,
    }
    if step not in dispatch:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown step: {step}")

    dispatch[step](client, arn, token)


# ── Step 1: generate and store a new password in AWSPENDING ──────────────
def _create_secret(client, arn, token):
    try:
        client.get_secret_value(SecretId=arn, VersionStage="AWSPENDING",
                                VersionId=token)
        logger.info("createSecret: AWSPENDING already exists — skipping")
        return
    except client.exceptions.ResourceNotFoundException:
        pass

    current = _get_secret(client, arn, "AWSCURRENT")
    current["password"] = _generate_password()

    client.put_secret_value(
        SecretId=arn,
        ClientRequestToken=token,
        SecretString=json.dumps(current),
        VersionStages=["AWSPENDING"],
    )
    logger.info("createSecret: stored new password in AWSPENDING")


# ── Step 2: apply the new password in the actual database ────────────────
def _set_secret(client, arn, token):
    pending = _get_secret(client, arn, "AWSPENDING", token)
    current = _get_secret(client, arn, "AWSCURRENT")

    conn = _db_connect(current)
    try:
        with conn.cursor() as cur:
            if DB_ENGINE == "postgresql":
                cur.execute(
                    "ALTER USER %s WITH PASSWORD %s",
                    (pending["username"], pending["password"]),
                )
            else:
                # MySQL / MariaDB
                cur.execute(
                    "ALTER USER %s@'%%' IDENTIFIED BY %s",
                    (pending["username"], pending["password"]),
                )
        conn.commit()
        logger.info("setSecret: password updated in database")
    finally:
        conn.close()


# ── Step 3: verify the new credentials actually work ─────────────────────
def _test_secret(client, arn, token):
    pending = _get_secret(client, arn, "AWSPENDING", token)
    conn = _db_connect(pending)
    try:
        with conn.cursor() as cur:
            cur.execute("SELECT 1")
        logger.info("testSecret: new credentials verified")
    finally:
        conn.close()


# ── Step 4: promote AWSPENDING to AWSCURRENT ─────────────────────────────
def _finish_secret(client, arn, token):
    metadata = client.describe_secret(SecretId=arn)
    current_version = next(
        (v for v, stages in metadata["VersionIdsToStages"].items()
         if "AWSCURRENT" in stages),
        None,
    )
    if current_version == token:
        logger.info("finishSecret: already AWSCURRENT — nothing to do")
        return

    client.update_secret_version_stage(
        SecretId=arn,
        VersionStage="AWSCURRENT",
        MoveToVersionId=token,
        RemoveFromVersionId=current_version,
    )
    logger.info("finishSecret: promoted AWSPENDING to AWSCURRENT")


# ── Helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def _get_secret(client, arn, stage, version_id=None):
    kwargs = {"SecretId": arn, "VersionStage": stage}
    if version_id:
        kwargs["VersionId"] = version_id
    value = client.get_secret_value(**kwargs)
    return json.loads(value["SecretString"])


def _generate_password(length: int = PASSWORD_LEN) -> str:
    alphabet = string.ascii_letters + string.digits + string.punctuation
    safe = "".join(c for c in alphabet if c not in EXCLUDED)
    # Guarantee at least one of each required character class
    pwd = [
        secrets.choice(string.ascii_uppercase),
        secrets.choice(string.ascii_lowercase),
        secrets.choice(string.digits),
        secrets.choice("!#$%^&*()-_=+[]{}|;:,.<>?"),
    ]
    pwd += [secrets.choice(safe) for _ in range(length - len(pwd))]
    secrets.SystemRandom().shuffle(pwd)
    return "".join(pwd)


def _db_connect(secret: dict):
    if DB_ENGINE == "postgresql":
        return psycopg2.connect(
            host=secret["host"],
            port=secret.get("port", 5432),
            dbname=secret["dbname"],
            user=secret["username"],
            password=secret["password"],
            connect_timeout=5,
            sslmode="require",          # always require TLS in production
        )
    # MySQL / MariaDB
    import pymysql  # noqa: PLC0415
    return pymysql.connect(
        host=secret["host"],
        port=secret.get("port", 3306),
        db=secret["dbname"],
        user=secret["username"],
        password=secret["password"],
        connect_timeout=5,
        ssl={"ssl": True},
    )

# ============================================================
# TEMPLATE: Azure Key Vault — Near-Expiry Secret Rotation
# WHEN TO USE: Automatically rotate a secret in Azure Key Vault
#              before it expires, triggered by an EventGrid event.
# PREREQUISITES:
#   - Azure Key Vault with an existing secret that has an expiry date set.
#   - Azure Function App (Consumption or Flex) with system-assigned identity.
#   - Function app identity must have "Key Vault Secrets Officer" on the vault.
# WHAT TO CHANGE: Lines marked  # <-- CHANGE THIS
# RELATED FILES: security/secret-rotation/external-secrets-operator.yaml
# MATURITY: Stable
# ============================================================

# ---------------------------------------------
# 1. EventGrid System Topic — Key Vault events
# ---------------------------------------------
resource "azurerm_eventgrid_system_topic" "keyvault" {
  name                   = "evgt-kv-rotation"       # <-- CHANGE THIS
  resource_group_name    = var.resource_group_name
  location               = var.location
  source_arm_resource_id = var.key_vault_id
  topic_type             = "Microsoft.KeyVault.vaults"
  tags                   = var.common_tags
}

# ---------------------------------------------
# 2. EventGrid Subscription → Azure Function
# ---------------------------------------------
resource "azurerm_eventgrid_system_topic_event_subscription" "near_expiry" {
  name                = "sub-kv-secret-near-expiry"
  system_topic        = azurerm_eventgrid_system_topic.keyvault.name
  resource_group_name = var.resource_group_name

  # Fire when a secret is about to expire (30-day warning)
  included_event_types = [
    "Microsoft.KeyVault.SecretNearExpiry",
    "Microsoft.KeyVault.SecretExpired",
  ]

  azure_function_endpoint {
    function_id = "${var.function_app_id}/functions/RotateSecret"
    # Ensure the function endpoint uses the same auth as the Event Grid topic
    max_events_per_batch              = 1
    preferred_batch_size_in_kilobytes = 64
  }

  retry_policy {
    max_delivery_attempts = 10
    event_time_to_live    = 1440 # 24 hours
  }
}

# Grant EventGrid permission to invoke the function
resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "eventgrid_to_function" {
  scope                = var.function_app_id
  role_definition_name = "Website Contributor"
  principal_id         = azurerm_eventgrid_system_topic.keyvault.identity[0].principal_id
}

# Grant Function identity access to Key Vault
resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "function_kv_secrets_officer" {
  scope                = var.key_vault_id
  role_definition_name = "Key Vault Secrets Officer"
  principal_id         = var.function_identity_principal_id # <-- CHANGE THIS: system-assigned MI principal
}

variable "resource_group_name" { type = string }
variable "location"            { type = string }
variable "key_vault_id"        { type = string }
